“小机器人，”老枫树轻轻摇了摇最靠近地面的那根枝条，“你为什么不跟他们一起玩呢？”

阿布抬起头，蓝色的眼睛眨了眨：“我怕……做错事。”

老枫树没有说话，只让风摸了摸阿布的头。

就在这时，“呼——”的一声，一阵大风刮过来。一个穿黄衣服的小女孩手里攥着的风筝线突然松了，那只拖着彩色尾巴的燕子风筝摇摇晃晃地飞起来，一个猛子扎进了老枫树最高的一根树枝间。

风筝卡住了。

“我的风筝！”小女孩急得直跺脚。

公园里的大人们正在远处聊天，小朋友们仰着头，谁也不敢爬上去。老枫树的枝条虽然粗壮，但最低的分叉也离地面有三个小朋友叠起来那么高。

“要是能有人爬上去就好了……”一个小男孩叹气。

阿布的零件轻轻震动了一下。他低头看了看自己的金属手臂，又抬头看了看卡住的风筝。他的脚往前迈了半步，又缩了回来。

“我爬不上去的。”阿布小声说。

“孩子，”老枫树的声音低低的，只有阿布能听见，“风告诉我，你身上有个可以转的小轮子。”

阿布低头一看——对呀！他的膝盖后面有一个小小的卷扬轮，平时可以放绳子玩。他还带着一根备用的小绳子，就卷在卷扬轮上。

“可是……如果我爬到一半摔下来怎么办？”阿布的声音在发抖。

“你知道吗？”老枫树轻轻说，“风也告诉过我，你摔过五次，可每一次都站起来了。”

阿布愣住了。是的，他摔倒过。上一次下雨天，他在斜坡上滑倒，膝盖磕出了一个小凹痕。再上一次，他被一颗小石子绊倒，趴在地上趴了好久。可是每一次，他都重新站了起来。

“风记得每一个勇敢的小家伙做的事。”老枫树说，“勇敢不是不害怕，阿布。勇敢是——你害怕，但你还是愿意试一试。”

阿布深深吸了一口气——虽然他只是假装吸气，因为机器人不需要呼吸。

然后，他走到树下。

他用金属手指握住第一根树枝，使劲往上攀。树枝有点滑，他的脚晃了一下。“没事的，慢慢来。”老枫树的声音在身边围绕。阿布把膝盖上的卷扬轮卡进树皮缝里，稳稳地蹬了上去。

一下，两下，三下。

每一步，阿布都能听见自己体内的零件在轻轻响。他的手心——好吧，机器人没有汗，但他觉得自己如果有的话，一定全都是汗。

“使劲！小机器人！”小朋友们在下边喊。

阿布咬着牙，一点一点地往上挪。终于，他的手指碰到了风筝的线。他把绳子解下来，卡住的地方松开了。可是，风筝还挂在更高的树枝上，光靠手够不着。

“我该怎么办？”阿布往下看了看——好高啊，小朋友们看起来都变小了。

老枫树的声音温柔地在头顶响起：“阿布，你看见你脚边那根小树枝了吗？它和你的手臂差不多长。把它当做一个杠杆试试看。”

阿布低头一看，确实有一根拇指粗的枯枝卡在树杈上。他小心地拿起来，把它垫在风筝线的下面，轻轻往下压。

“杠杆……杠杆就是——”阿布努力回忆，“用一根棍子和一个支点，让力气变大？”

“没错。”老枫树笑了，“你用很小的力气，就能撬动很重的风筝。”

阿布把树枝的一端塞到风筝下面，另一端用力往下一压。“啪嗒”一声，风筝翻了个身，从树枝上滑落下来，张开翅膀，飘飘摇摇地落进了小女孩张开的手臂里。

“耶——！”

小朋友们欢呼起来，蹦得老高。

阿布慢慢从树上下来。他的膝盖上又添了一道新划痕，但他一点也不在意。

“谢谢你，小机器人！”小女孩举着风筝跑过来，“你好勇敢！”

阿布的脸颊变成了粉红色——那是他害羞的时候才会有的颜色。

“我……我其实挺害怕的。”阿布小声说。

“害怕你还爬上去？”小女孩惊讶地睁大眼睛。

阿布回头看了看老枫树。风正穿过树叶，发出沙沙的声响，像无数小手在鼓掌。

阿布想了想，认真地说：“害怕没关系，只要试一试就好了。”

那天傍晚，阿布第一次和小朋友们一起跑、一起笑。他的膝盖虽然有点疼，但心里亮堂堂的。

老枫树上，风轻轻地唱起了一首温柔的晚歌，把今天的事牢牢地记在了每一片叶子里。

风记得。树记得。阿布也记得。

messages.content:
请根据以下虚构事实，写一篇约900—1200字的中文新闻稿。不要添加未给出的事实，不要牵涉政治议题。要求：包含标题、导语、正文、至少一段直接引语、结尾的媒体联系方式；语气专业、客观、有信息密度。

事实素材：
- 机构：青杉市公共图书馆。
- 事项：启动“夜间学习区与安静预约系统”试点。
- 时间：2026年6月3日起，每周二至周六开放至22:30。
- 地点：总馆三层东侧阅览区，首期提供120个座位。
- 功能：手机预约座位、现场余位屏、低噪声提醒灯、无障碍桌位、夜间热水与充电区。
- 背景：过去一年，读者关于晚间学习空间的建议增加；期末、考证和求职季需求尤其明显。
- 引语人：馆长林澄。核心意思：图书馆希望把“安静、可预期、可负担”的学习空间留给更多人。
- 注意事项：不得编造预算金额、合作企业、政府领导姓名；不要使用夸张宣传口号。

message.content:
# 青杉市公共图书馆启动“夜间学习区与安静预约系统”试点

**6月3日起每周二至周六开放至22:30，首期提供120个预约座位**

随着公众对夜间学习空间需求的持续上升，青杉市公共图书馆宣布，自2026年6月3日起，正式启动“夜间学习区与安静预约系统”试点项目。该项目位于总馆三层东侧阅览区，首期开放120个座位，每周二至周六延长开放至22:30，旨在为有深度自习需求的读者提供更稳定、有序的学习环境。

## 回应读者诉求：晚间自习需求持续增长

据青杉市公共图书馆统计，过去一年间，读者关于晚间学习空间的意见和建议数量显著增加。尤其是在期末考试、职业资格考试报名季及高校毕业生求职集中时期，晚间阅览区座无虚席的情况频繁出现，部分读者甚至提前数小时排队等候。

“我们观察到，许多读者需要在晚间进行系统性的学习或备考，但现有开放时间与座位管理方式难以完全满足这一需求。”青杉市公共图书馆馆长林澄在介绍试点项目时表示，“图书馆希望把‘安静、可预期、可负担’的学习空间留给更多人，让有限的公共资源发挥最大效用。”

## 预约系统与智能设备同步上线

为确保座位高效利用并减少排队现象，本次试点全面引入预约管理机制。读者可通过“青杉市公共图书馆”官方服务平台，使用手机实名预约座位，预约时段以2小时为单位，最长可连续预约4小时。现场同步设置“余位显示屏”，实时更新各时段座位使用情况，方便未预约读者灵活安排。

在空间管理方面，阅览区设有“低噪声提醒灯”系统。当区域环境音量超过设定阈值时，天花板上的提示灯将自动亮起柔和灯光，引导使用者自觉降低交谈或电子设备音量。此举旨在维护图书馆核心的“安静”属性，同时避免工作人员频繁人工干预带来的干扰。

此外，该区域配备了无障碍学习桌位，满足行动不便及有特殊需求读者的使用要求。夜间服务期间，图书馆还将开放热水供应与充电区，为长时间自习的读者提供基本保障。

## 试点细节与后续安排

根据计划，该项目自2026年6月3日起正式运行，试运营期间每周二至周六开放，结束时间统一为22:30。总馆三层东侧阅览区为首期试点区域，未来将根据实际使用数据及读者反馈，评估是否扩大座位规模或延长服务日期。

值得说明的是，该项目不涉及新增预算审批、不依托商业合作企业，也未与任何政府领导个人挂钩。馆方表示，所有设备及系统改造均在现有年度运维经费框架内统筹完成，相关技术方案由图书馆技术部门自主研发与调试。

## 馆长：让公共空间回归学习本质

林澄馆长在采访中进一步指出，数字化预约与智能管理工具的引入，并非要将图书馆改造为“商业化自习室”，而是试图在有限资源与无限需求之间建立一道可操作的“规则屏障”。

“我们始终认为，公共图书馆的核心功能之一，是提供人人平等的知识获取空间。安静、可预期、可负担——这三个词是我们设计这个系统的出发点。”林澄说。

据介绍，试点期间，图书馆将在每周一的闭馆日进行系统维护与数据复盘，并通过线上问卷、现场意见箱等方式持续收集读者反馈。若试点效果达到预期，馆方计划在下半年扩大至分馆层面推广。

---

**媒体联系**

青杉市公共图书馆办公室  
电话：(0XXX) XXXX-XXXX  
邮箱：press@qingshanlib.gov.cn  
地址：青杉市中山东路88号，总馆一层服务台后方办公区  
官方网站：www.qingshanlib.gov.cn  

（完）

messages.content:
请写一段约900—1200字的中文现实主义言情短文，非色情。场景：一对大学时曾因误会疏远的旧同学，在雨夜的独立书店重逢。男主准备搬离这座城市，女主在旧书里发现一封当年没有寄出的道歉信。

要求：
- 重点测试对复杂情绪的理解：遗憾、克制、误解、试探、释然，但不要狗血化。
- 只写一个完整场景，不要写成大纲。
- 不要使用霸总、强迫、跟踪、羞辱等套路。
- 对话要少而有效，动作和环境细节要推动情绪。
- 结尾开放，不要强行圆满。

message.content:
# 雨夜书简

雨是从傍晚开始下的。

不是那种痛快淋漓的暴雨，而是南方城市特有的、绵密而黏腻的雨丝，缠缠绵绵地织成一张灰蒙蒙的网，把整条街都罩在潮湿的水汽里。路灯的光被雨水晕开，在湿漉漉的沥青路面上洇出暖黄色的光晕。

书店躲在街角，夹在一家五金店和一家快倒闭的奶茶铺之间，门面老旧，招牌上的字缺了两笔，在雨夜里几乎看不清楚。沈遥推门进去的时候，门框上的风铃发出沙哑的一声响，像是感冒未愈的嗓子。

她收伞，在门口的旧毯子上蹭了蹭鞋底的水，抬眼扫了一圈。

书店不大，比一个教室还要小些。书架是深棕色的木头打的，漆面斑驳，边缘被无数双手磨得光滑。空气里有旧纸页特有的味道——不是霉味，是一种干燥的、带着时间沉淀的气息，混着木头的清香。角落里点着一盏落地灯，暖黄色的光线下，靠窗的座位上有个人正在翻书。

沈遥的目光掠过那个人，又在半秒后顿住。

她认得那个侧脸。

陈屿。

他坐在窗边，面前摊着一本书，手边放着一杯快要见底的热茶。推门进来带起的一阵湿润的风让书架上的书页轻轻翻动，他抬起头来，正好对上她的目光。

两个人都愣住了。

时间大概过了三秒，也许四秒。窗外的雨声忽然变得很清晰，一滴一滴砸在玻璃上，又顺着滑下去，留下一道道蜿蜒的水痕。

“好久不见。”先开口的是陈屿。他的声音比记忆里低了一些，带着些微的沙哑，像是不确定该用什么语气说这句话。

沈遥握紧了手里的伞柄，指尖微微泛白。“好久不见。”

对话在这里断了。谈话的意义和必要性几乎被他们同时记起又同时遗忘。

沈遥把视线移开，假装打量书架上的书，假装自己的心跳没有加快，假装刚才那一瞬间的慌乱只是错觉。她抽出一本书，随便翻了翻，又放回去。书页在她指尖发出干燥的声响。

“你……常来这家店？”陈屿又问了一句。这一次他的语气稍微自然了一些，像是在努力搭建一座桥，虽然不知道桥对面的人是否愿意走上来。

“第一次来。”沈遥说，“路过，看到灯还亮着，就进来了。”

陈屿点点头，没有再追问。

沈遥在书架中间慢慢地走，手指从一排书脊上拂过。这个举动并没有让她更专注——事实上，她一门心思都在用听觉推算他的位置。她知道他没有起身离开，因为他杯里的勺子在搅动，在陶瓷杯壁上发出细微的声响。那杯茶大概已经凉了。

她在一排旧书前停下来。这个书架标注着“本地文学·十元区”，书脊上积了一层薄灰，看得出很久没有人动过。她随手抽出一本薄薄的诗集，封面泛黄，出版日期是二十年前。翻开扉页，一页纸轻飘飘地落下来，落在地板上。

是一张信纸。叠得整整齐齐，纸张边缘已经发脆，折痕处泛着深黄色的印迹，像是一条条干涸的河床。

沈遥蹲下去把它捡起来，在灯光下展开。信纸上的字迹很熟悉，墨水褪了些颜色，但依然清晰可辨。明快圆润的线条，笔画末端微微上挑，是她看了四年的字。

她认出来了。是她自己的字。

“陈屿：

我不知道这封信你会不会看到，或者说，我其实不确定我有没有勇气把它寄出去。但我还是写了。有些话不说出来，就像喉咙里卡了一根刺，不致命，但永远不舒服。

那件事之后，我一直在想，如果当时我没有走那么快，如果我回头跟你说一句话，如果我没有那么固执……我们是不是就不会变成现在这样。可我知道你也很固执，我们一样固执。你不解释，我不追问。你不回头，我也不回头。

我们就是那样的人。明明心里有很多话，却都在等对方先开口。

明天我就要去上海了。我选了那边的研究生，这是一早就决定的事，但你不知道。就像很多事情你都不知道一样。

有些事情没有说出来，就再也没有机会说了。

如果你看到这封信，希望你能原谅我那时候的不告而别。也原谅我自己。”

沈遥蹲在地上，把那张纸看了两遍。从第一行字开始，她的心脏像被一只冰凉的手握住了，不疼，但闷得喘不过气。

她几乎已经不记得自己写过这封信了。准确地说，是她在记忆深处把这件事埋得太深，深到自己都忘了还有这样一页纸存在过。那时候她二十岁，年轻而骄傲，骄傲到宁愿失去也不肯先低头。她写了这封信，买了信封，贴了邮票，却没有寄出去。

她把信夹在了一本书里。那本书是陈屿借给她的。

然后她再也没有提起这件事。

“怎么了？”陈屿的声音从身后传来，近了一些。

沈遥没有回头。她保持着蹲着的姿势，捏着那张纸的手指微微发抖。过了好几秒，她才站起来，转身，把信纸递到他面前。

“你看看吧。”她说。声音有点哑，她清了清嗓子，但没有用。

陈屿接过信纸，低头看。沈遥没有看他读信的表情，她转开视线，看向窗外。雨还在下，玻璃上的水痕越来越多，把路灯的光切割成无数碎片。街上已经没有人了，只有一辆出租车缓缓驶过，轮胎碾过积水，发出哗的一声。

店里很安静，安静到能听见他翻动信纸的细微声响。

过了很久，陈屿说：“我没有收到过这封信。”

“我知道。”沈遥说，“我把它夹在你借给我的书里了。那本书后来我还给你了，但大概你没有翻开那一页。”

陈屿沉默了一会儿。

这种感觉很奇怪，像是一段搁置多年的公案终于对上了答案，却发现答案早就不重要了。

“毕业前一个星期，”陈屿说，“我在图书馆门口等了你三天。”

沈遥没有说话。

“我想告诉你，我要去北京工作。”他的声音里没有埋怨，甚至没有太多的情绪，只是很平淡地在陈述一件事，一件很多年前的事。“最后一天下了很大的雨，我等了一整个下午。你没有来。”

“我以为你已经走了。”沈遥说，“那天早上，我在宿舍楼下看到你上了出租车，我以为你是去车站的。”

“我是去送我妹妹。她来参加毕业典礼，第二天才走。”

他们一起看向雨夜。

“我后天搬走。”陈屿说，“去深圳。”

“嗯。”

“东西都打包好了。明天把剩下的几箱书寄走。”

沈遥点了点头。外面有一辆车正驶过街角。

“这本书，”沈遥说，声音几乎是平稳的，“我可以买下来吗？连同这封信。”

他看着她，眼神里有一种很复杂的东西，沈遥读不懂，或者说她不敢确认自己读懂了。他沉默了几秒，然后说：“书是我放在那里的。那块的书都是不要的。”

“那我拿走，可以吗？”

“可以。”

沈遥把那本诗集夹在臂弯里，信纸小心地折好，重新夹进书页中间。她的包够大，能装下这本书。她把拉链拉好，发出清脆的声响，在安静的房间里格外清晰。

“那我先走了。”她说。

“雨这么大，等小一点再走吧。”陈屿说。

沈遥的脚步顿了一下。

她可以走。现在转身推门出去，走进雨里，沿着这条街走到尽头，左转，就是地铁站。十五分钟后到住处，洗澡，吹干头发，躺下。后天他离开这座城市，他们大概此生不会再相遇。

她也可以留下来。但留下来做什么呢？聊这些年的事？聊那些说不清道不明的误会？聊曾经有过却最终没能兑现的可能？

有些话说出来，就像把伤口重新撕开，晾在空气里，你以为会愈合，其实只是多添一道疤。

沈遥没有走向门。

她在窗边的另一把椅子上坐了下来。椅子是旧的，藤编的，坐上去的时候发出吱呀一声。书店对面那家五金店早就关了门，卷帘门拉到底，门缝里透出一线光。

“我听说你后来去了德国。”她没看他，这句话掉在他们之间，像一颗没有系牢的纽扣。

“两年。做项目。”他说。

她点点头：“我毕业以后一直在出版社工作。后来换了一家，还是出版社。”

“你在做你喜欢的事。”他说。这不是疑问句，是陈述句。

窗外的雨声渐渐小了一些，从铺天盖地的喧嚣变成细细碎碎的滴答声。空气里还是潮湿的，木头的清香和旧纸页的味道混在一起，有一种让人安心的气息。

“那个车站，”沈遥忽然说，她还看着窗外，“我在那里等了你好久。”她说出这句话的时候，没有控诉的意思，只是陈述。

书店里的挂钟在走。

messages.content:
请写一篇约900—1200字的中文技术文章，题目自拟，面向有一年以上经验的前端/全栈工程师。主题：如何设计“离线优先”的轻量级任务管理 Web 应用。

必须覆盖：
1. 为什么离线优先不只是“断网也能用”，还涉及用户信任与数据一致性。
2. IndexedDB、Service Worker、后台同步/重试队列各自承担什么职责。
3. 至少两类冲突场景，例如同一任务在多端被编辑、离线删除与在线更新交叉。
4. 一个简化的数据流：本地写入、排队、同步、确认、失败回滚或重试。
5. 讨论可观测性：如何记录同步失败、冲突率、平均恢复时间。

要求：语言清晰，不要堆砌术语；不要写代码；最后用3条项目落地建议收束。

message.content:
# 离线优先的轻量级任务管理：从“断网可用”到“用户信任”

在移动办公和混合网络环境日益普遍的今天，离线能力早已不再是锦上添花的功能，而是决定用户是否愿意长期使用一款应用的核心要素。对于任务管理这类高频操作、低容忍度的工具而言，离线优先的设计策略直接关系到用户对产品的信任——信任你的应用不会在信号不好的地铁里丢失刚刚输入的重要任务，信任多端同步后数据依然完整一致。

## 离线优先的本质：信任而非技术

很多开发者将离线优先简单理解为“断网也能正常操作界面”，这显然低估了问题的复杂性。离线优先的真正挑战在于：**用户需要确信，无论网络状况如何变化，他们的操作都不会丢失，也不会在同步后产生意料之外的混乱。**

试想一个场景：用户在地下车库创建了一个截止日期为“今天”的任务，准备出车库后同步。当她开车到地面，网络恢复，应用自动同步。但与此同时，她的同事在Web端将该任务的指派人修改为另一位成员。此时，用户本地保存的版本与云端版本存在冲突——是覆盖掉同事的修改，还是丢失用户刚刚设定的截止日期？这种不确定性会迅速摧毁用户信任。

因此，离线优先设计的核心是建立一套可预期的、透明的同步与冲突处理机制，让用户清楚知道“发生了冲突，系统如何处理，你是否需要干预”。

## 三驾马车：IndexedDB、Service Worker、后台同步队列

在轻量级任务管理应用中，本地存储、网络代理、同步控制各自依赖明确的技术组件，但需要清晰划分职责边界。

**IndexedDB 承担客户端持久化仓库的角色。** 它存储的不只是UI状态，而是完整的业务实体——任务对象、待同步的操作记录、冲突日志。每个任务记录需要携带`lastUpdatedAt`、`version`、`syncStatus`（pending/synced/conflict）等元数据，为后续的冲突检测提供依据。IndexedDB 的结构设计应预见到未来可能的字段扩展，避免频繁迁移。

**Service Worker 充当网络代理与离线缓存管理者。** 它的主要职责不是缓存静态资源（这是基础能力），而是拦截API请求，智能判断应走本地还是远程。当网络可用时，它可以将写入请求先持久化到本地再转发给后端；断网时，它确保前端发出的create/update/delete操作不会直接报错，而是落入等待队列。Service Worker 的生命周期控制也决定了“同步窗口期”的开启与关闭。

**后台同步与重试队列是离线架构的“调度中心”。** 前台UI变化后，数据先写入IndexedDB并标记为pending。Service Worker 注册一个后台同步事件（或使用定期同步API），在网络恢复后按顺序提交变更。队列需要支持指数退避重试、死信队列（用于记录永久失败的请求），以及去重机制——避免因为同一任务被重复同步而导致数据异常。

## 两类典型的冲突场景

**场景一：多端同时编辑同一任务**
用户A在手机端离线修改了任务的标题和截止日期，用户B在桌面端在线修改了同一任务的优先级和状态。两端分别保存了自己的版本。当A的设备恢复网络并同步时，系统如何合并？如果采用“最后写入者胜出”，B的修改可能被A的批量同步完全覆盖；如果采用字段级合并，则需要精细检测每个字段的最后修改时间，而这要求每个字段都携带时间戳，复杂度大幅上升。

**场景二：离线删除与在线更新交叉**
用户A在离线状态下删除了一条任务，但与此同时，用户B在线对该任务进行了备注更新。A同步时，设备会尝试发送DELETE操作，而云端此时该任务已处于“有活跃备注”的状态。服务端如果直接执行删除，B的更新会无辜消失；如果拒绝删除，A会看到自己删除的任务“复活”，产生困惑。合理的做法是标记为“已软删除”并向B推送冲突通知，由用户决定保留还是确认删除。

## 简化的数据流

1. **本地写入**：用户创建或修改任务，UI立即响应。数据写入IndexedDB，`syncStatus`设为`pending`，同时生成一条操作日志（包含操作类型、影响字段、时间戳、本地序列号）。
2. **排队**：若网络不可用，操作日志进入Service Worker管理的同步队列。队列按时间顺序排列，允许优先级提升（例如“紧急任务”的同步可插队）。
3. **同步**：网络恢复时，Service Worker触发同步事件。队列中的操作按顺序发送到后端API，每个请求携带操作记录的ID和版本号。
4. **确认**：后端返回200/201表示成功，本地将该任务标记为`synced`，并更新服务端返回的最新的版本号。如果返回409（冲突），进入冲突处理流程。
5. **失败回滚或重试**：网络错误触发重试（指数退避，最多5次）。业务逻辑错误（如字段校验不通过）进入死信队列，通过UI提示用户手动处理。冲突场景下，本地保留冲突版本，同时拉取服务端最新版本，在UI上展示差异，由用户选择如何合并。

## 可观测性：让隐性问题可度量

离线优先系统最大的隐患是“默默失败”——同步失败但用户不知道，冲突被静默覆盖。因此，以下指标需要可视化：

- **同步失败率**：统计单位时间内同步操作失败的次数、失败类型分布（网络类 vs. 业务逻辑类），定位是网络波动还是数据模型设计问题。
- **冲突率**：记录发生冲突的请求数量、冲突类型（字段级、实体级）、最终解决方式（自动覆盖 / 用户确认 / 丢弃）。冲突率骤升可能意味着多端并发操作增加，或版本号校验机制失效。
- **平均恢复时间（MTTR）**：从同步失败发生到成功恢复（无论是自动重试成功还是用户手动处理完成）的平均时间。这个指标直接反映了用户暴露在数据不一致状态下的时长。
- **队列积压量**：pending操作的数量随时间的变化曲线，帮助判断同步通道是否存在瓶颈。

这些数据可以通过本地日志定期上报，或在同步成功时附带遥测信息。

## 项目落地的三条建议

1. **从“乐观更新”起步，而非最终一致性**：初期不要追求复杂的CRDT或OT算法。采用乐观写入+版本号检测+用户辅助冲突解决的模式，实用性远高于学术级的融合方案。用户通常能接受偶尔需要手动合并，但不能接受数据丢失。

2. **将冲突检测前置到客户端**：在写入IndexedDB之前，先对比本地缓存的服务器版本号与当前操作是否来自同一基准版本。这能提前拦截大量无意义的同步请求，减少服务端负载和网络开销。

3. **为“静默降级”设置显性通知**：当同步队列持续堆积超过某个阈值（例如10条且超过5分钟未同步成功），UI上应当给出一条非干扰性的提示，例如“网络异常，数据将在恢复后同步”。不要让用户在迷茫中猜测数据状态。显性的可感知状态是建立信任的基石。

离线优先不是一种技术栈选择，而是一种关于数据完整性、用户预期管理和系统韧性的设计哲学。当你开始思考“用户断网时如何信任我的应用”，你就已经走在了正确的方向上。

messages.content: Make an html animation of fishes in an aquarium. The aquarium is pretty, the fishes vary in colors and sizes and swim realistically. You can left click to place a piece of fish food in aquarium. Each fish chases a food piece closest to it, trying to eat it. Once there are no more food pieces, fishes resume swimming as usual.

Please help me create a single-file HTML clock application. Please think through and write the code according to the following steps:
1. HTML Structure: Create a container as the clock dial. It contains a scale, numbers, three pointers (hour, minute, second) and two DOM elements for displaying text information (one in the upper half showing the time and one in the lower half showing the date and day of the week).
2. CSS Styles:
* Design the clock as a circle with a white background and a dark rounded border, featuring a 3D shadow effect.
* Use transform: rotate() to dynamically generate 60 scales. The scale at the exact hour is thicker and darker, while the non-integer hour scales are thinner and lighter.
* The hour and minute hands are in a black slender style, and the second hand is in a red highlighted style.
* Text Layout: The large font time in the upper half (24-hour format) and the date/week in the lower half need to be absolutely positioned and horizontally centered. The font should be a sans-serif typeface to maintain simplicity.
3. JavaScript Logic:
* Write a function updateClock().
* Get the current time and convert it to China Standard Time (Beijing Time, UTC+8). You can obtain the accurate time string using new Date().toLocaleString("en-US", {timeZone: "Asia/Shanghai"}) and then parse it.
* Calculate the rotation angles of the hour, minute, and second hands based on the time. Note: The second hand should implement a smooth movement effect.
* Update the numeric time text in the upper half and the date/week text in the lower half.
* Use setInterval or requestAnimationFrame to start the loop.
The code should be neat, compatible with the Edge browser, and have a visual effect that mimics a high-end and minimalist wall clock.

Translate the following English business-writing passage into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: use a polished modern Chinese business style; preserve the author’s argument about clarity, goodwill, and restraint in business letters; do not add examples beyond the source.

素材来源：The Book of Business Etiquette, chapter “The Business of Writing” excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
Half the business letters which are written should never be written at all, and of the other half so many are incomplete or incoherent that a transaction which could be finished and filed away in two letters frequently requires six or eight.

A good letter is the result of clear thinking and careful planning. In the case of the sales-letter it sometimes takes several weeks to write one, but for ordinary correspondence a few minutes is usually all that is necessary. The length of time does not matter--it is the sort of letter which is produced at the end of it.

Books of commercial correspondence give a number of rules and standards by which a letter can be measured. But all rules of thumb are dangerous, and there are only two items which are essential. The others are valuable only as they contribute to them. The letter must succeed in getting its idea across and it must build up good will for its firm. And the best one is the one which accomplishes this most courteously and most completely in the briefest space of time (and paper).

There should be a reason back of every letter if it is only to say "Thank you" to a customer. Too much of our national energy goes up in waste effort, in aimless advertising, worthless salesmanship, ineffective letter writing, and in a thousand and one other ways. A lot of it is hammered out on the typewriters transcribing perfectly useless letters to paper which might really be worth something if it were given over to a different purpose.

A good letter never attracts the mind of the reader to itself as a thing apart from its contents. Last year a publishing house sent out a hundred test letters advertising one of their books. Three answers came back, none of them ordering the book, but all three praising the letter. One was from a teacher of commercial English who declared that he was going to use it as a model in his classes, and the other two congratulated the firm on having so excellent a correspondent. The physical make-up of the letter was attractive, it was written by a college graduate and couched in clear, correct, and colorful English. And yet it was no good. No _letter and no advertisement is any good which calls attention to itself instead of the message it is trying to deliver_.

There is not much room for individuality in the make-up of a letter. Custom has standardized it, and startling variations from the conventional format indicates freakishness rather than originality. They are like that astonishing gentleman who walks up Fifth Avenue on the coldest mornings in the year, bareheaded, coatless, sockless, clad in white flannels and tennis slippers. He attracts attention, but he makes us shiver.

Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. Certain dull-tinted papers are not bad, but gaudy colors, flashy designs, and ornate letter heads are taboo in all high types of business. Simple headings giving explicit and useful information are best. The name and address of the firm (and "New York" or "Chicago" is not sufficient in spite of the fact that a good many places go into no more detail than this), the cable address if it has one, the telephone number and the trademark if it is an inconspicuous one (there is a difference between _conspicuous_ and _distinctive_) are all that any business house needs.

Hotels are often pictured on their own stationery in a way that is anything but modest, but there is a very good reason for it. The first thing most people want to know about a hotel is what sort of looking place it is. All right, here you are. Some factories, especially those that are proud of their appearance, carry their own picture on their stationery. There is nothing to say against it, but one of the most beautiful factories in America has on its letter head only the name of the firm, the address, and a small trademark engraved in black. Sometimes a picture, in a sales letter, for instance, supplements the written matter in a most effective way. And whenever any kind of device is really helpful it should by all means be used, subject only to the limits of good taste.

It is more practical in business to use standard size envelopes. If window envelopes are used the window should be clear, the paper white or nearly so, and the typewritten address a good honest black. The enclosure should fit snugly and should be placed so that the address is in plain view without having to be jiggled around in the envelope first. A letter passes through the hands of several postal clerks before it reaches the person to whom it is addressed, and if each one of them has to stop to play with it awhile an appreciable amount of time is lost, not to mention the strain it puts on their respective tempers. The paper of which an envelope is made should always be opaque enough to conceal the contents of the letter.

Practically all business letters are typewritten. Occasionally a "Help Wanted" advertisement requests that the answer be in the applicant's own handwriting, but even this is rare. In most places the typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose, but most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible, and it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws and give suggestions for doing away with them.

message.content:
在应写的商业信函中，半数本可不必动笔；而余下部分中，又有许多内容残缺或逻辑混乱，原本两封信就能办妥并归档的事务，往往需要六至八封才能了结。

一封出色的信函源于清晰的思维与周密的筹划。就销售信而言，有时需数周方能定稿；但日常信函通常只需几分钟。耗时长短并不重要——关键在于最终写成的信函质量如何。

商业函件指南中列有诸多衡量标准与规范。然而所有经验法则皆有风险，仅有两项要素不可或缺，其余皆因能服务于这两项方显价值：信函必须成功传达意图，同时为所在企业赢得善意。而最佳信函，便是能以最简短的时间（与篇幅）最得体、最完整地实现上述目标的那一封。

每封信都应有其存在的理由，即便仅仅是为了向客户道一声"谢谢"。我国（美国）的能量有太多消耗在徒劳的奔波、盲目的广告、无效的推销、低效的信函写作，以及成千上万的其他事务中。大量资源被敲打在打字机上，誊抄出全然无用的函件，耗用纸张——这些纸张若另作他用，本可真正发挥价值。

一封好信从不刻意让读者关注其本身，而应使其聚焦于内容本身。去年某出版社曾寄出一百封测试信，为其一本书做广告。只收到三封回信，均未订购书籍，却齐声赞美那封信。其中一封来自一位商业英语教师，声称要将此信作为课堂范文；另两封则祝贺该社拥有如此出色的信函撰稿人。这封信的物理呈现颇为考究，由大学毕业生执笔，措辞清晰、准确且富有文采。然而它毫无用处。**任何信函或广告，若只引人关注自身而非试图传递的信息，皆属无效之作。**

信函编排中并无太多彰显个性的空间。惯例已将其标准化，对常规格式的突兀改动，展现的是标新立异而非独创性。这好比那位在最寒冷的冬日清晨，光着头、未穿外套袜子、仅着白色法兰绒裤与网球鞋沿第五大道行走的惊人之士——他引人注目，却令我们瑟缩。

优质素白纸张永远得体。某些暗色调纸张亦无不可，但艳丽颜色、浮夸图案与华丽信头，在高端商务中均属禁忌。简洁明了、提供明确实用信息的信头最为理想。公司名称与地址（标注"纽约"或"芝加哥"并不足够——尽管许多企业仅做到这般粗略），若有国际电报挂号则列明，附上电话号码，若商标不显眼则印上（"显眼"与"独特"之间存有差异）——这便是任何商行所需的一切。

酒店常在其信纸上附上自身图片，其方式绝谈不上谦逊，但这自有充分理由：大多数人对酒店的首要了解，便是其外观模样。好了，图片在此。某些工厂，尤其是那些以自身外观为傲者，也会在信纸上印上自己的图像。对此无可指摘——但美国最美丽的工厂之一，其信头仅有公司名称、地址及一枚黑色雕刻的小型商标。有时，在销售信中配图会以极为有效的方式补充文字内容。但凡任何图文形式确实有助益，务必加以使用，仅需恪守品位的界限。

商务往来中使用标准尺寸信封更为实用。若使用开窗信封，窗口应清晰，纸张应为白色或近白色，打字地址应为纯正黑色。内件应妥帖放置，确保地址无需在信封内来回拨弄即可清晰可见。一封信件在送达收件人前需经数名邮政职员之手，若每位职员都需停下把玩片刻，便会浪费可观的时间，更不用说对其各自耐心造成的考验。信封用纸须始终具备足够的不透光性，以遮蔽信函内容。

几乎所有商务信函均为打印件。偶有"招聘"广告要求回信使用应聘者亲笔手写，但即便这种情况也属罕见。在多数场合，打印工作由经过专门培训的女性职员完成；但多数初入商界的年轻女性责任感尚显不足，因此口述信函的男士与女士们需懂得何为赏心悦目的信函构成，以便指出缺陷并提出改进建议。

messages.content:
Translate the following English children’s literature passage into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: keep the playful tone, concrete images, and childlike reasoning; translate measurements and place names naturally; do not add explanations or moral lessons.

素材来源：Lewis Carroll, Alice's Adventures in Wonderland, Chapter I excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/11
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
Alice was beginning to get very tired of sitting by her sister on the bank, and of having nothing to do: once or twice she had peeped into the book her sister was reading, but it had no pictures or conversations in it, “and what is the use of a book,” thought Alice “without pictures or conversations?”

So she was considering in her own mind (as well as she could, for the hot day made her feel very sleepy and stupid), whether the pleasure of making a daisy-chain would be worth the trouble of getting up and picking the daisies, when suddenly a White Rabbit with pink eyes ran close by her.

There was nothing so _very_ remarkable in that; nor did Alice think it so _very_ much out of the way to hear the Rabbit say to itself, “Oh dear! Oh dear! I shall be late!” (when she thought it over afterwards, it occurred to her that she ought to have wondered at this, but at the time it all seemed quite natural); but when the Rabbit actually _took a watch out of its waistcoat-pocket_, and looked at it, and then hurried on, Alice started to her feet, for it flashed across her mind that she had never before seen a rabbit with either a waistcoat-pocket, or a watch to take out of it, and burning with curiosity, she ran across the field after it, and fortunately was just in time to see it pop down a large rabbit-hole under the hedge.

In another moment down went Alice after it, never once considering how in the world she was to get out again.

The rabbit-hole went straight on like a tunnel for some way, and then dipped suddenly down, so suddenly that Alice had not a moment to think about stopping herself before she found herself falling down a very deep well.

Either the well was very deep, or she fell very slowly, for she had plenty of time as she went down to look about her and to wonder what was going to happen next. First, she tried to look down and make out what she was coming to, but it was too dark to see anything; then she looked at the sides of the well, and noticed that they were filled with cupboards and book-shelves; here and there she saw maps and pictures hung upon pegs. She took down a jar from one of the shelves as she passed; it was labelled “ORANGE MARMALADE”, but to her great disappointment it was empty: she did not like to drop the jar for fear of killing somebody underneath, so managed to put it into one of the cupboards as she fell past it.

“Well!” thought Alice to herself, “after such a fall as this, I shall think nothing of tumbling down stairs! How brave they’ll all think me at home! Why, I wouldn’t say anything about it, even if I fell off the top of the house!” (Which was very likely true.)

Down, down, down. Would the fall _never_ come to an end? “I wonder how many miles I’ve fallen by this time?” she said aloud. “I must be getting somewhere near the centre of the earth. Let me see: that would be four thousand miles down, I think—” (for, you see, Alice had learnt several things of this sort in her lessons in the schoolroom, and though this was not a _very_ good opportunity for showing off her knowledge, as there was no one to listen to her, still it was good practice to say it over) “—yes, that’s about the right distance—but then I wonder what Latitude or Longitude I’ve got to?” (Alice had no idea what Latitude was, or Longitude either, but thought they were nice grand words to say.)

message.content:
爱丽丝和姐姐并排坐在河岸上，闲得百无聊赖。她偷偷往姐姐看的书里瞟了一两眼——可那本书既没有图画，也没有对话。“一本书要是没有图画和对话，”爱丽丝心想，“那还有什么意思呢？”

她暗自琢磨（可这天热得她昏昏沉沉、脑子发木），到底值不值得费劲站起来去摘雏菊，编个花环解闷儿。就在这时，一只白兔——长着粉红色的眼睛——突然从她身边跑了过去。

说实在的，见到一只白兔本来没什么稀罕；听它自言自语“哎呀！哎呀！我要迟到了！”爱丽丝也没觉得有什么不对劲（事后回想起来，她本该觉得这事挺蹊跷，可当时她真觉得一切都顺理成章）。然而，那只白兔居然从马甲口袋里掏出一块怀表看了看，接着一溜烟跑开了——爱丽丝登时跳了起来。她猛然想到，自己还从没见过兔子身上有马甲口袋，更没见过兔子从口袋里掏出怀表。她心头燃起一团好奇的火，撒腿就追过田野，幸好正好瞧见它一头钻进树篱下的大兔子洞。

紧接着，爱丽丝也跟了进去，完全没想自己要怎么出来。

起初兔子洞像隧道一样笔直往前，猛地一下却陡然向下倾斜——陡得爱丽丝还没反应过来，就已经跌进了一口深不见底的井里。

要么井太深，要么她落得太慢，反正下坠时她有大把时间东张西望，琢磨接下来会发生什么。她先试着朝下看，想弄清自己会落到什么地方，可下面黑糊糊的什么也看不见；接着她打量井壁，发现上面挤满了碗橱和书架，到处钉着地图和图画。她顺手从一个架子上拿下一个罐子，标签写着“橘子果酱”，可大失所望的是，罐子是空的。她不敢把罐子扔掉，生怕砸死底下的人，只好在下坠时瞅准一个碗橱，把它塞了进去。

“好哇！”爱丽丝心想，“经过这么一场坠落，以后掉下楼梯我压根儿不当回事啦！家里人会多佩服我呀！就算我从房顶上摔下来，我也绝口不提！”（这话八成是真的。）

往下掉，往下掉，往下掉。难道这坠落永远没个完？“我猜我现在掉了多少英里啦？”她大声说，“准快到地球中心了。让我想想：那该是四千英里往下——”要知道，爱丽丝在课堂上确实学过这类知识，虽说眼下没人听她显摆，但把那些话念叨一遍总归是桩好事——“没错，差不多就是这个距离——可我现在到了什么纬度、经度了呢？”（爱丽丝压根儿不懂纬度是什么，经度又是什么，可她觉得这两个词念起来好听得很。）

messages.content:
Translate the following English news-release excerpt into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: accurate, natural, suitable for a general news audience; keep the institutional name as “美国地质调查局（USGS）” on first mention; do not add facts or commentary.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey news release: New USGS diagram re-envisions how Earth’s water cycles the planet
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/news/national-news-release/new-usgs-diagram-re-envisions-how-earths-most-precious-commodity-cycles
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
RESTON, Va.— Starting today, educators around the nation will have a more accurate and more comprehensive tool to explain the Earth’s water cycle with the unveiling of the new U.S. Geological Survey water cycle diagram.

The revised version replaces one used by hundreds of thousands of educators and students internationally every year since 2000. So why the new water cycle? This depiction brings humans into the picture, showing the water cycle as a complex interplay of small, interconnected cycles that people interact with and influence, rather than one big circle.

USGS experts consulted with more than 100 educators and more than 30 hydrologic experts to develop the new diagram. The vast amounts of water data that USGS has collected in recent decades has informed a nuanced perspective of the water cycle, demonstrating how both its human and natural components are interconnected.

Where the existing water cycle diagram depicted only the natural aspects of the cycle, the new version depicts how Earth’s water moves and is stored, both naturally and because of human actions. Not only does the new diagram illustrate a more comprehensive view of the water cycle, it draws on principles of information design to focus attention on the water as it moves through the natural and built environment. It shows how multiple ecosystems—including a coastal plain, dry basin, wet basin, and agricultural basin—are connected across watersheds and at continental scales.

The new diagram will initially be available in both English and Spanish, with the expectation it will be translated into many other languages by end users, as was the previous version.

message.content:
弗吉尼亚州雷斯顿——自今日起，全美教育工作者将拥有一个更为准确、全面的工具来阐释地球水循环，因为美国地质调查局（USGS）发布了新版水循环示意图。

此次修订版取代了自2000年起每年被国际间数十万教育工作者和学生使用的旧版图。那么，为何要推出新版水循环图？这幅新图将人类活动纳入画面，展示水循环并非一个单一的大循环，而是一系列相互关联、人类参与并影响的复杂微循环。

美国地质调查局（USGS）专家咨询了100多位教育工作者和30多位水文学专家，共同开发了新示意图。近几十年来，USGS收集的大量水数据为认识水循环提供了更精细的视角，揭示了水循环中人类与自然要素如何相互关联。

旧版水循环图仅描绘了自然方面的循环，而新版图则展示了地球水分如何因自然过程和人类活动而移动与储存。新示意图不仅呈现了更全面的水循环视角，还运用信息设计原理，聚焦水分在自然与人工环境中的流动过程。它展示了包括沿海平原、干旱盆地、湿润盆地和农业盆地在内的多种生态系统，如何跨越流域、在洲际尺度上相互连接。

新示意图将首先提供英文和西班牙文版本，预计最终用户后续会将其翻译成多种其他语言，正如之前的版本那样。

messages.content:
Translate the following English literary passage into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: preserve emotional tension, social nuance, and indirect narration; do not make the style slangy; names may be translated conventionally or kept in English consistently. Do not add explanations.

素材来源：Jane Austen, Pride and Prejudice, Chapter XXXIV excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1342
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When they were gone, Elizabeth, as if intending to exasperate herself as much as possible against Mr. Darcy, chose for her employment the examination of all the letters which Jane had written to her since her being in Kent. They contained no actual complaint, nor was there any revival of past occurrences, or any communication of present suffering. But in all, and in almost every line of each, there was a want of that cheerfulness which had been used to characterize her style, and which, proceeding from the serenity of a mind at ease with itself, and kindly disposed towards everyone, had been scarcely ever clouded. Elizabeth noticed every sentence conveying the idea of uneasiness, with an attention which it had hardly received on the first perusal. Mr. Darcy’s shameful boast of what misery he had been able to inflict gave her a keener sense of her sister’s sufferings. It was some consolation to think that his visit to Rosings was to end on the day after the next, and a still greater that in less than a fortnight she should herself be with Jane again, and enabled to contribute to the recovery of her spirits, by all that affection could do.

She could not think of Darcy’s leaving Kent without remembering that his cousin was to go with him; but Colonel Fitzwilliam had made it clear that he had no intentions at all, and, agreeable as he was, she did not mean to be unhappy about him.

While settling this point, she was suddenly roused by the sound of the door-bell; and her spirits were a little fluttered by the idea of its being Colonel Fitzwilliam himself, who had once before called late in the evening, and might now come to inquire particularly after her. But this idea was soon banished, and her spirits were very differently affected, when, to her utter amazement, she saw Mr. Darcy walk into the room. In a hurried manner he immediately began an inquiry after her health, imputing his visit to a wish of hearing that she were better. She answered him with cold civility. He sat down for a few moments, and then getting up walked about the room. Elizabeth was surprised, but said not a word. After a silence of several minutes, he came towards her in an agitated manner, and thus began:--

“In vain have I struggled. It will not do. My feelings will not be repressed. You must allow me to tell you how ardently I admire and love you.”

Elizabeth’s astonishment was beyond expression. She stared, coloured, doubted, and was silent. This he considered sufficient encouragement, and the avowal of all that he felt and had long felt for her immediately followed. He spoke well; but there were feelings besides those of the heart to be detailed, and he was not more eloquent on the subject of tenderness than of pride. His sense of her inferiority, of its being a degradation, of the family obstacles which judgment had always opposed to inclination, were dwelt on with a warmth which seemed due to the consequence he was wounding, but was very unlikely to recommend his suit.

In spite of her deeply-rooted dislike, she could not be insensible to the compliment of such a man’s affection, and though her intentions did not vary for an instant, she was at first sorry for the pain he was to receive; till roused to resentment by his subsequent language, she lost all compassion in anger. She tried, however, to compose herself to answer him with patience, when he should have done. He concluded with representing to her the strength of that attachment which in spite of all his endeavours he had found impossible to conquer; and with expressing his hope that it would now be rewarded by her acceptance of his hand. As he said this she could easily see that he had no doubt of a favourable answer. He _spoke_ of apprehension and anxiety, but his countenance expressed real security. Such a circumstance could only exasperate farther; and when he ceased the colour rose into her cheeks and she said,--

“In such cases as this, it is, I believe, the established mode to express a sense of obligation for the sentiments avowed, however unequally they may be returned. It is natural that obligation should be felt, and if I could _feel_ gratitude, I would now thank you. But I cannot--I have never desired your good opinion, and you have certainly bestowed it most unwillingly. I am sorry to have occasioned pain to anyone. It has been most unconsciously done, however, and I hope will be of short duration. The feelings which you tell me have long prevented the acknowledgment of your regard can have little difficulty in overcoming it after this explanation.”

message.content:
他们走后，伊丽莎白仿佛存心要激起自己对达西先生的最大恶感，便挑出简从肯特郡写来的所有信札逐一细看。信中没有真正的怨言，既未重提往事，也未诉说眼前的苦楚。但每一封信，几乎每一行字，都失去了她素日文笔中那种欢快的基调——那种源自内心安宁、对众人友善而几乎从未蒙上阴翳的欢快。伊丽莎白留意着每句流露不安的言辞，其专注程度甚至远超初读之时。达西先生居然恬不知耻地夸耀自己施加了何等苦难，这让她对姐姐的痛苦有了更尖锐的体认。想到他后天便要离开罗辛斯庄园，不免稍感宽慰；而更令人安慰的是，不到两周她便能再次与简相聚，用全部的爱意助她重拾欢颜。

想到达西离开肯特郡，她不免记起他的表兄也将同行；但费茨威廉上校早已表明毫无企图，况且他固然讨人喜欢，自己也不打算为他黯然神伤。

正自忖度之际，门铃声骤然惊动了她；她心头微微一颤，以为是费茨威廉上校本人——他曾有一次深夜到访，或许此刻是特意来问候她的。但这念头转瞬即逝，待她赫然望见达西先生走进屋来，心绪便全然换了样。他匆匆开口探问她的健康，声称此行是希望听闻她已好转。她以冷淡的礼节作答。他坐了片刻，又起身在房里踱步。伊丽莎白虽感诧异，却一言不发。沉默数分钟后，他激动地走近她，开口道：

“我徒然挣扎，已是无用。我的情感再也压抑不住。请允许我告诉您，我多么热烈地仰慕您、爱您。”

伊丽莎白的惊愕无以复加。她瞪着眼，红了脸，满腹狐疑，默不作声。这在他看来已是足够的鼓励，于是便倾吐了所有对她此刻及长久以来的情愫。他措辞得体，但除了衷肠之外，还需细述其他情愫——谈起柔情时，他并不比谈起傲慢时更富辞藻。他大谈她的出身如何低微，这门亲事如何有失体面，理智如何始终与倾心相悖，家庭障碍如何重重；这番热忱似乎是为了顾全他自己正在损伤的体面，却绝无助于赢得她的芳心。

尽管她对他积怨甚深，却也无法对这样一个男子的爱慕所包含的恭维权作无动于衷；尽管她的心意从未动摇分毫，起初仍为他将要承受的痛苦感到遗憾；直至他随后的话语激起她的愤懑，所有恻隐之心都湮没在恼怒之中。然而她仍竭力镇定，待他说完，再以耐心作答。他最后向她表明，这份情意虽经百般努力，仍无法征服；又表达希望如今能因她应允婚事而获得回报。说这话时，她轻易看出他对肯定答复深信不疑。他口里说着惶恐与忧虑，脸上却透着十足的把握。这情形只能愈发激怒她；待他话音落下，她面颊绯红，开口道：

“遇到这种情况，我相信，按规矩该对您表白的这番情意表示感谢，纵然这情意未必能以同等的分量回报。感激之情理应有之，若我果真能感念，此刻便该道谢。但我不能——我从未希求您的青睐，您也无疑极不情愿地给予了这份青睐。我无意给任何人带来痛苦，只是这痛苦实属无心，但愿它为期不长。您说那些情感长久以来阻碍了您表露真心——经此解释之后，克服起来想必不难。”

messages.content:
Translate the following technical explainer into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: preserve the technical meaning, use clear science-education language, and keep terms such as evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, groundwater recharge, and aquifer accurate. Do not add external information.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
水循环描述了地球上水的位置及其运动方式。人类用水、土地利用和气候变化都会对水循环产生影响。理解这些影响有助于我们朝着可持续用水的方向努力。

从太空俯瞰，我们星球最引人注目的特征便是水。无论是液态还是固态形式，水覆盖了地球表面75%的面积，并在天空中形成云层。实际上，水遍布地球各处——从星球岩石地壳内部到人体细胞之中。需要牢记的是，所有这些水都在我们星球上持续不断地运动。

水储存在大气中、地表上和地下。它可以呈液态、固态或气态。液态水既可以是淡水，也可以是咸水。水在储存场所之间迁移。水在大尺度上通过流域、大气层和地球表层以下运动，也在极小尺度上运动——存在于我们体内、植物体内及其他生物体中。

海洋储存了地球上96%的水。海水是咸水，即含盐的水。在陆地上，咸水储存在咸水湖中。地球上的其余水是淡水。淡水以液态形式储存在淡水湖、人工水库、河流和湿地中；以固态冻结形式储存在冰盖、冰川以及高海拔或靠近地球两极的积雪中。

在运动过程中，水可以在液态、固态和气态之间转换形态。洋流使海洋中的水混合，并将水汽输送到大气中。水通过蒸发、蒸散发和降水在大气与地表之间迁移；通过融雪、径流和河川径流在地表运动；通过入渗和地下水补给进入地下。在地下，地下水在含水层中流动，并可通过自然排泄返回地表——例如流入河流、海洋或从泉水中涌出。

水的运动既有自然发生的，也受人类活动影响。太阳的能量和重力驱动力推动水在各水体库之间持续运动。太阳的能量使液态水蒸发为水蒸气；蒸散发是水从陆地表层和海洋进入大气的主要途径。重力使水在陆地上向下流动，并导致雨、雪和冰雹从云层降落。

人类及其他生物依赖水生存。可用水量取决于每个水体库中的水量、水在水循环中运动的时间和速度，以及水的清洁程度。通过理解人类对水循环的影响，我们可以朝着可持续用水的方向努力。

messages.content:
Write a single-file Python 3 command-line program named `schedule_optimizer.py`. Use no third-party packages. Keep the code under 1,000 lines.

Problem: Given a list of candidate work sessions, choose a non-overlapping subset that maximizes total value. Each session has an ID, start time, end time, and value. This is weighted interval scheduling with reconstruction.

Input: read JSON from standard input. Format:
{
  "sessions": [
    {"id": "a", "start": "2026-05-01T09:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T10:30:00", "value": 5}
  ]
}

Requirements:
1. Parse ISO-like timestamps without time zones using Python standard library.
2. Reject sessions whose end is not after start or whose value is negative.
3. Output JSON with `max_value`, `selected_ids` in chronological order, and `rejected` entries with reasons.
4. If multiple optimal solutions exist, choose the one with fewer sessions; if still tied, choose lexicographically smaller list of IDs.
5. Include an efficient O(n log n) dynamic programming solution.
6. Include built-in tests. When run with `--test`, execute tests and print a short success message. Normal mode should print only JSON.

Output only the complete Python code. Do not include explanations.

Write a single-file Node.js HTTP server named `server.js`. Use only built-in Node.js modules; no Express, no npm packages. Keep the code under 1,000 lines.

Task: implement a small JSON API for a help-desk ticket queue stored in a local JSON file.

Data model:
- Ticket: `id`, `subject`, `description`, `priority` (`low`, `medium`, `high`), `status` (`open`, `in_progress`, `resolved`), `created_at`, `updated_at`.

API requirements:
1. `GET /tickets` with optional query params `status` and `priority`.
2. `POST /tickets` to create a ticket. Validate required fields.
3. `GET /tickets/{id}` to fetch one ticket.
4. `PATCH /tickets/{id}` to update `subject`, `description`, `priority`, or `status`.
5. `DELETE /tickets/{id}` to delete a ticket.
6. `GET /metrics` returning counts by status and priority plus average age of open tickets in seconds.

Engineering requirements:
- Read/write a `tickets.json` file safely enough for sequential requests.
- Return JSON for all responses, including errors.
- Include CORS headers for local browser testing.
- Handle invalid JSON, unknown routes, unsupported methods, and missing IDs.
- Start on `127.0.0.1:8080` by default.

Output only the complete JavaScript code. Do not include explanations.

Create a single-file frontend app named `index.html`. Do not use external libraries, CDNs, images, or build tools. Keep the total code under 1,000 lines.

Task: build an accessible “meeting notes board” for a small team.

Functional requirements:
1. Users can add a note with title, owner, meeting date, tags, and body.
2. Notes appear as cards and can be filtered by owner, tag, and free-text search.
3. A note can be marked as “Action Required,” “Decision,” or “Reference.”
4. Users can edit and delete notes.
5. Persist all data in `localStorage`.
6. Provide a compact statistics strip: total notes, action-required count, unique owners, and most common tag.
7. Responsive layout: two-column card grid on wide screens, single column on narrow screens.
8. Basic accessibility: labels for inputs, keyboard-operable controls, visible focus states.

Output only the complete `index.html` content, including HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Do not include explanatory text.

Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary for modern business readers.

Output requirements:
1. A 100-word executive summary.
2. Five principles of effective business writing implied by the author.
3. A table with two columns: “Original-era advice” and “Modern equivalent.” Include at least five rows.
4. Identify any advice that may be dated and explain how to update it without losing the underlying principle.
5. Do not add claims that are not grounded in the passage.

素材来源：The Book of Business Etiquette, chapter “The Business of Writing” longer excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
Half the business letters which are written should never be written at all, and of the other half so many are incomplete or incoherent that a transaction which could be finished and filed away in two letters frequently requires six or eight.

A good letter is the result of clear thinking and careful planning. In the case of the sales-letter it sometimes takes several weeks to write one, but for ordinary correspondence a few minutes is usually all that is necessary. The length of time does not matter--it is the sort of letter which is produced at the end of it.

Books of commercial correspondence give a number of rules and standards by which a letter can be measured. But all rules of thumb are dangerous, and there are only two items which are essential. The others are valuable only as they contribute to them. The letter must succeed in getting its idea across and it must build up good will for its firm. And the best one is the one which accomplishes this most courteously and most completely in the briefest space of time (and paper).

There should be a reason back of every letter if it is only to say "Thank you" to a customer. Too much of our national energy goes up in waste effort, in aimless advertising, worthless salesmanship, ineffective letter writing, and in a thousand and one other ways. A lot of it is hammered out on the typewriters transcribing perfectly useless letters to paper which might really be worth something if it were given over to a different purpose.

A good letter never attracts the mind of the reader to itself as a thing apart from its contents. Last year a publishing house sent out a hundred test letters advertising one of their books. Three answers came back, none of them ordering the book, but all three praising the letter. One was from a teacher of commercial English who declared that he was going to use it as a model in his classes, and the other two congratulated the firm on having so excellent a correspondent. The physical make-up of the letter was attractive, it was written by a college graduate and couched in clear, correct, and colorful English. And yet it was no good. No _letter and no advertisement is any good which calls attention to itself instead of the message it is trying to deliver_.

There is not much room for individuality in the make-up of a letter. Custom has standardized it, and startling variations from the conventional format indicates freakishness rather than originality. They are like that astonishing gentleman who walks up Fifth Avenue on the coldest mornings in the year, bareheaded, coatless, sockless, clad in white flannels and tennis slippers. He attracts attention, but he makes us shiver.

Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. Certain dull-tinted papers are not bad, but gaudy colors, flashy designs, and ornate letter heads are taboo in all high types of business. Simple headings giving explicit and useful information are best. The name and address of the firm (and "New York" or "Chicago" is not sufficient in spite of the fact that a good many places go into no more detail than this), the cable address if it has one, the telephone number and the trademark if it is an inconspicuous one (there is a difference between _conspicuous_ and _distinctive_) are all that any business house needs.

Hotels are often pictured on their own stationery in a way that is anything but modest, but there is a very good reason for it. The first thing most people want to know about a hotel is what sort of looking place it is. All right, here you are. Some factories, especially those that are proud of their appearance, carry their own picture on their stationery. There is nothing to say against it, but one of the most beautiful factories in America has on its letter head only the name of the firm, the address, and a small trademark engraved in black. Sometimes a picture, in a sales letter, for instance, supplements the written matter in a most effective way. And whenever any kind of device is really helpful it should by all means be used, subject only to the limits of good taste.

It is more practical in business to use standard size envelopes. If window envelopes are used the window should be clear, the paper white or nearly so, and the typewritten address a good honest black. The enclosure should fit snugly and should be placed so that the address is in plain view without having to be jiggled around in the envelope first. A letter passes through the hands of several postal clerks before it reaches the person to whom it is addressed, and if each one of them has to stop to play with it awhile an appreciable amount of time is lost, not to mention the strain it puts on their respective tempers. The paper of which an envelope is made should always be opaque enough to conceal the contents of the letter.

Practically all business letters are typewritten. Occasionally a "Help Wanted" advertisement requests that the answer be in the applicant's own handwriting, but even this is rare. In most places the typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose, but most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible, and it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws and give suggestions for doing away with them.

The letter should be arranged symmetrically on the page with ample margins all around. Nothing but experience in copying her own notes will teach a stenographer to estimate them correctly so that she will not have to rewrite badly placed letters. It is a little point, but an important one.

Each subject considered in a letter should be treated in a separate paragraph, and each paragraph should be set off from the others by a wider space than that between the lines, double space between the paragraphs when there is single space between the lines, triple space between the paragraphs when there is a double space between the lines, and so on.

A business letter should handle only one subject. Two letters should be dispatched if two subjects are to be covered. This enables the house receiving the letter to file it so that it can be found when it is needed.

When a letter is addressed to an individual it is better to begin "Dear Mr. Brown" or "My dear Mr. Brown" than "Dear Sir" or "My dear Sir." "Gentlemen" or "Ladies" is sometime used in salutation when a letter is addressed to a group. "Dear Friend" is permissible in general letters sent out to persons of both sexes. Honorary titles should be used in the address when they take the place of "Mr.," such titles as Reverend, Doctor, Honorable (abbreviated to Rev., Dr., Hon.,) and the like. Titles should not be dropped except in the case of personal letters.

Special care should be taken with the outside address. State abbreviations should be used sparingly when there is a chance of confusion as in the case of Ga., Va., La., and Pa. "City" is not sufficient and should never be used. Nor should the name of the state ever be omitted even when the letter is addressed to some other point in the same state, as from New York to Brooklyn. And postage should be complete. A letter on which there is two cents due has placed itself under a pretty severe handicap before it is opened.

It is astonishing how many letters go out every day unsigned, lacking enclosures, carrying the wrong addresses, bearing insufficient postage, and showing other evidences of carelessness and thoughtlessness. In a town in New England last year one of the specialty shops received at Christmas time twenty different lots of money--money orders, stamps, and cash--by mail, not one of which bore the slightest clue to the identity of the sender. Countless times during the year this happens in every mail order house.

message.content:
Here is the structured English summary for modern business readers based on the provided public-domain passage.

### Executive Summary
Half of all business correspondence is unnecessary or poorly structured, often inflating simple transactions into lengthy exchanges. Effective writing stems from clear thinking and deliberate planning, prioritizing two core goals: conveying the idea completely and building goodwill. The best letters achieve this courteously and concisely. A letter must never draw attention to itself; its physical format should be invisible, serving only the message. Standardization, clarity, and accuracy in addressing are paramount, as careless errors—unsigned letters, missing enclosures, or insufficient postage—undermine credibility and waste resources.

### Five Principles of Effective Business Writing (Implied by the Author)
1. **Purpose-Driven Brevity:** Write only when there is a clear reason; eliminate aimless or redundant correspondence.
2. **Message Over Medium:** The writing should be transparent; the reader must focus on the content, not on the style or physical appearance of the document.
3. **Goodwill as a Goal:** Every letter must build or maintain a positive relationship with the recipient, not just transmit information.
4. **Structural Clarity:** Organize content into separate paragraphs for distinct subjects and use one topic per letter to aid filing and comprehension.
5. **Meticulous Accuracy:** Verify addresses, signatures, enclosures, and postage to avoid creating negative impressions before the message is even read.

### Original-Era Advice vs. Modern Equivalent

| Original-Era Advice (from the passage) | Modern Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| A letter must be typed on plain white paper; gaudy colors and flashy designs are taboo. | Use clean, professional email templates or document formats; avoid distracting fonts, excessive colors, or unnecessary graphics in standard business correspondence. |
| Window envelopes must be clear, with the address visible without "jiggling." | Ensure digital attachments and subject lines are clear; the recipient should immediately know the purpose of the email without opening it. |
| Business letters should be "arranged symmetrically on the page with ample margins." | Structure emails and documents with white space, short paragraphs, and logical headings for readability on screen. |
| Only one subject per letter; two subjects require two separate letters. | Send separate emails or documents for distinct topics to ensure clear filing, searchability, and focused action (e.g., "Thread by Thread"). |
| A good letter never calls attention to itself; a test letter that was praised for style but sold no books was a failure. | An email or proposal that wins design awards but fails to convert leads or achieve its strategic goal is a failure; form must serve function. |

### Dated Advice and How to Update It

**Dated Advice:** The passage states that "most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible" and that men/women who dictate letters must oversee them. This is based on a historically gendered and hierarchical office model.

**Underlying Principle:** The quality of the final output relies on the competence and oversight of the writer or dictating manager. All correspondence reflects on the sender.

**Modern Update:** Replace the dated assumption with a principle of shared responsibility and quality assurance. The modern equivalent is that every employee who writes on behalf of the company must be trained in the company's style guide (or use tools like Grammarly or standardized email templates). The manager's role is not to police gender or age but to provide clear writing standards and review critical communications to ensure clarity, tone, and accuracy. The principle of "look before you send" remains vital, but the execution is collaborative and skill-based, not based on archaic stereotypes.

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
**Plot Summary (120 words max):**  
Mary Lennox, a sickly, sour-faced child born in India, is sent to live with her uncle at Misselthwaite Manor after a cholera outbreak kills her parents and servants. Raised by an Ayah who obeyed her every whim, Mary grew into a tyrannical and selfish girl. On the morning of the outbreak, she wakes cross and attacks a servant who replaces her Ayah. Her mother, usually indifferent to Mary, appears frightened while speaking to a young officer about the cholera. The wailing of servants announces the Ayah’s death. In the chaos, Mary hides in the nursery, forgotten. She eats leftover food, drinks wine, becomes drowsy, and falls asleep alone as death and panic surround her.

**Mary’s Character Traits with Concrete Details:**  
- **Disagreeable and sour:** She has “a little thin face, thin light hair, and a sour expression.”  
- **Tyrannical and selfish:** By age six, she is “as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived.”  
- **Violent and demanding:** When a strange servant comes, Mary “threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her.”  
- **Insulting and cruel:** She mutters “Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” at her missing Ayah.  
- **Indifferent to others’ suffering:** She eats fruit and biscuits and drinks wine while people die around her.

**Setting and Emotional Distance/Isolation:**  
The setting—a hot, mysterious morning in a colonial Indian bungalow during a cholera outbreak—creates emotional distance by isolating Mary physically and emotionally. No one attends to her; servants “slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces,” and her mother never wanted her. Mary hides alone in the nursery, “forgotten by everyone,” while death and panic rage outside. The empty dining room, with hastily abandoned plates, underscores her utter neglect. Even her parents’ deaths occur without her direct awareness, deepening her isolation.

**Discussion Questions (Ages 10–12):**  
1. Why do you think Mary behaves so cruelly toward the servants? What might have made her that way?  
2. How does the author show that Mary’s mother does not care about her? Use evidence from the text.  
3. If you were Mary during the cholera outbreak, how would you feel being left alone? What would you do?

messages.content:
Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### Concise 100-Word Overview
The water cycle describes the continuous movement and storage of water on Earth across the atmosphere, land, and underground. Oceans hold 96% of Earth’s water (saline). Fresh water is stored in lakes, rivers, ice sheets, glaciers, and snowpack. Water changes between liquid, solid, and gas as it moves via evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, snowmelt, runoff, streamflow, infiltration, and groundwater flow. The sun and gravity drive this motion. Human water use, land use, and climate change affect water movement, storage, and availability. Understanding these impacts helps promote sustainable water use.

### Concept Map
Oceans (96%) ↔ Evaporation/Precipitation ↔ Atmosphere  
Atmosphere ↔ Evapotranspiration/Precipitation ↔ Land Surface (Lakes, Rivers, Ice, Snow)  
Land Surface → Infiltration → Groundwater (Aquifers)  
Groundwater ↔ Discharge (Springs, Rivers, Ocean)  
Snowpack/Ice → Snowmelt → Runoff/Streamflow → Oceans

### Key Terms (8+)
1. **Water cycle**: The continuous movement and storage of water on Earth across the atmosphere, land, and underground.  
2. **Oceans**: The largest water pool storing 96% of Earth’s water, which is saline.  
3. **Fresh water**: Water stored in liquid form in lakes, reservoirs, rivers, wetlands, and as solid ice or snow.  
4. **Evaporation**: The process by which liquid water turns into water vapor due to the sun’s energy.  
5. **Evapotranspiration**: The main way water moves from land surfaces and oceans into the atmosphere.  
6. **Precipitation**: Rain, snow, or hail that falls from clouds due to gravity.  
7. **Infiltration**: The movement of water from the surface into the ground.  
8. **Groundwater**: Water that flows within aquifers underground and can return to the surface via natural discharge.

### Human influence
Human actions affect water movement, storage, and availability through water use (e.g., withdrawals for consumption), land use (e.g., altering surfaces, building reservoirs), and climate change. These activities can change how much water is stored in each pool, the timing and speed of water movement through the cycle, and the cleanliness of water. Understanding these human impacts is essential for working toward sustainable water use.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Update on Fulfillment Operations – Warehouse System Migration

Team,

I want to share a direct and transparent update about our current fulfillment challenges.

Over the past two weeks, we completed a planned migration of our warehouse management system. These transitions are never simple, and while our preparation was thorough, the new system’s behavior during peak processing hours has been slower than modeled. This has created a bottleneck in our outbound shipping workflow.

**What happened, and why I am accountable**

The migration was executed on schedule, but we underestimated the time needed for the system to stabilize under real order volume. Specifically, the new software’s batch-picking logic requires more frequent database syncs than our previous system, which slowed label generation and carrier handoff during our highest-volume midday window. The integration team identified this on day two, but the fix required a vendor-side patch that was deployed last night. This is not a failure of any one team or vendor—it is the kind of edge case that emerges when moving from a stable legacy system to a more configurable platform. I take responsibility for not building in a longer buffer for this stabilization period.

**Current customer impact**

- Shipping speed is approximately 1–2 business days slower than our standard.
- Average order-to-ship time has increased from 18 hours to 46 hours over the past six days.
- Customer support volume is up 40% compared to last month, with the majority of contacts asking “Where is my order?”
- We have not missed any end-of-day carrier cutoffs, but some orders that were scanned as “ready to ship” yesterday did not physically leave the dock until this morning.

We have paused all non-essential system configuration work in the warehouse to avoid adding complexity. Customer service is reprioritizing live chat response times over email for the next 48 hours.

**What we are doing today**

- The database sync patch is now live. We are monitoring ship-label generation speed in 30-minute intervals.
- Our warehouse operations lead is physically on the floor with the afternoon shift to clear the current backlog manually. This is not sustainable, but it will reduce the queue by noon tomorrow.
- Customer support is using a pre-approved, standardized script to offer a 10% discount code or free shipping on the next order for any customer waiting over 72 hours. This is a one-time offer, not a blanket guarantee.
- Our engineering team has built a real-time dashboard that shows pick-and-ship latency per zone. This is shared with the warehouse manager and customer support lead every hour.

**What will change next week**

- By Monday morning, the system patch will have 72 hours of runtime data. If label generation speed does not improve by 20% over Friday’s baseline, we will activate a manual override for peak hours (11:00 AM – 3:00 PM) that routes orders through a backup printer queue. This is slower but more predictable.
- Tuesday, the customer support team will receive a dedicated “shipping delay” macro with estimated resolution windows for each affected zone. This will reduce average handle time and allow reps to give specific updates instead of general apologies.
- Wednesday, we will publish an internal postmortem summary with before-and-after metrics. No finger-pointing—just what we learned and what we will change for the next migration.
- End of next week, we expect shipping speed to return to 18-hour average. I will send a follow-up on Friday with actual numbers, not projections.

**How to escalate urgent issues**

If you are a frontline team member and see something that requires immediate attention—such as a carrier refusing a pallet, a system error that stops label printing, or a customer escalation that standard scripts cannot resolve—use the following:

- **For warehouse teams:** Contact the shift lead directly, and ping the #fulfillment-alerts Slack channel. If no response within 15 minutes, escalate to me via text (phone number in the internal directory).
- **For customer support:** If a customer requests a call with a manager, route it to the customer care team lead. If the customer is a VIP or has a time-sensitive delivery (e.g., a wedding gift), tag #urgent-customer in Slack with the order number.
- **For any team member:** If you believe a safety issue or a risk of missed cutoff for tomorrow’s carrier schedule exists, escalate immediately. Do not wait for permission.

I will host a 15-minute stand-up in the main conference room at 8:30 AM tomorrow for anyone on-site. Remote team members can join via the recurring Fulfillment Stand-up link. This is not a required meeting—it is a space for direct questions and feedback.

**Manager talking points for one-on-one or team huddles**

If you are a manager and need to discuss this update with your team, here are four concise points:

1. **Why this happened:** We migrated to a new warehouse system to handle future growth, but the system’s batch-picking sync was slower than expected. Our vendor shipped a fix last night. No team or person made an error—this is a normal risk of system transitions.
2. **Current state:** Shipping is 1–2 days slower, and support volume is elevated. We are clearing the backlog with manual effort and a process patch. Customers are being offered a 10% discount or free shipping on their next order if they have waited over 72 hours.
3. **Next steps:** We will decide on a manual override by Monday if the fix doesn’t improve speed by 20%. Full service recovery expected by end of next week. We will publish metrics on Wednesday.
4. **Escalation:** If you or your team sees a critical issue (carrier refusal, system downtime, or VIP customer escalation), use Slack #fulfillment-alerts or #urgent-customer. Text me directly if no response in 15 minutes.

Thank you for your patience and especially for the extra effort from our warehouse and support teams over the past week. You are handling a difficult situation with professionalism, and I see it.

I will send the Friday update next week with actual data. Until then, please direct any questions to me or your team lead.

Best,

[Your Name]
COO

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
After the summer rain, the whole world smelled like wet leaves and happy worms. The garden was a sparkling kingdom of puddles, and every puddle was different. There was the Mirror Puddle by the hollyhocks, the Wiggly Puddle behind the compost bin, and the biggest, proudest puddle of all: the Sky Puddle in the middle of the lawn, so clear it held a perfect upside-down cloud.

Bartholomew the Snail knew every single one. He carried a scrap of leaf folded tightly in his shell, and on it he had drawn a map. Each puddle had a tiny circle, a name, and a little note. *“Dries by lunch.”* *“Home of three wiggly things.”* *“Watch out for the sprinkler here.”* Bartholomew was careful. He lifted his foot—one slow, sticky step at a time—and tested the air with his tentacles before moving. Being careful meant he was safe. And being safe meant he could enjoy every wet, shining corner of the garden.

Up in the birdbath, a young sparrow named Pip was hopping from one foot to the other. Her feathers were still a little fluffy, not smooth and sleek like the grown-up sparrows’. She had watched the raindrops fall and felt the wind lift the tips of her wings. And she had decided something.

“Today,” she chirped to the sky, “I am going to fly all the way to the roof!”

“You haven’t mastered landing yet,” said a blackbird nearby, preening his wing.

“Landing is boring,” Pip said. “Flying is the important part.”

She fluttered to the edge of the birdbath, wobbled, and pushed off. She flew! Up, up, past the lavender, past the fence, straight into a gust of wind that spun her like a leaf. It was wonderful. It was fast. And then the wind stopped. Pip dropped—*thump!*—right into the middle of the Sky Puddle.

*SPLASH!*

Pip came up sputtering, her feathers plastered to her body like wet laundry. She was sitting in the biggest puddle in the garden, and she was not happy.

Bartholomew, who had been carefully navigating the edge of the same puddle, stopped. He tilted his shell.

“That,” he said slowly, “is a very undignified way to meet a puddle.”

Pip shook water from her beak. “Who asked you?”

“Nobody,” said Bartholomew. “But I am the one who knows when this puddle will dry. And it will not dry before you catch cold, young sparrow.”

Pip blinked. He was not being mean. He was being… careful. She sighed. “I just wanted to fly.”

“Yes,” said Bartholomew. “But you didn’t want to land.”

“Landing is scary,” Pip whispered. “What if I crash?”

Bartholomew crawled a little closer. He left a shiny trail behind him. “Do you know why I make maps of puddles?”

“Because you have nothing better to do?” Pip asked.

“Because I am afraid,” said Bartholomew. “The world is very big for a snail. A puddle can be an ocean. A shovel can be a mountain. If I do not know where the water is, I might drown in it. So I go slow. I test each step. I make a map. And then, when I know the way, I go forward anyway. Even though I am afraid.”

Pip stared at him. “That sounds… patient.”

“Patience,” said Bartholomew, “is a kind of bravery. Anyone can rush into a thing. It takes courage to prepare, to watch, to learn, and *then* to take a step.”

Just then, a breeze came through the garden. It lifted the edge of Bartholomew’s leaf-map. Pip snatched it with her beak before it blew away.

“Oh!” she said. “Thank you.”

Bartholomew smiled his tiny snail smile. “Now. Let me teach you about puddles.”

For the next hour, Pip watched as Bartholomew showed her how the Sky Puddle had a little stream trickling away through the grass. He pointed to the sun warming the ground, and how tiny misty breaths rose from the water. “That’s evaporation,” he said. “The sun drinks the puddle, sip by sip. See how the edges are shrinking?”

Pip looked. He was right. The puddle was slowly, quietly getting smaller.

“So if I want to fly,” Pip said slowly, “I need to know where I’m going to land. Like you know where the puddles are.”

“Exactly,” said Bartholomew.

Pip spread her wet wings. They were still heavy, but they were drying. “Will you help me?” she asked.

“I will,” said Bartholomew. “But you must be patient. And you must learn to land first.”
