| A letter should handle only one subject; send two letters if covering two topics (for filing purposes). | Use clear, specific subject lines in emails; for multiple topics, send separate emails so each can be searched, filed, or forwarded independently. |
| Begin "Dear Mr. Brown" rather than "Dear Sir" for personal correspondence. | Use the recipient's name in the salutation (e.g., "Dear Alex Chen") rather than a generic "To Whom It May Concern" or "Sir/Madam." |
| Use standard size envelopes; ensure the window shows the address plainly. (Address must be honest black text on white paper). | Ensure email subject lines and preview text are clear and accurate; use readable fonts and avoid overly stylized formatting that might break in different email clients. |
| Signatures, enclosures, and addresses must be verified before sending. | Run spell-check, verify all attachments are included, and double-check the "To," "CC," and "BCC" fields before hitting send. |

### Dated Advice and Modern Adaptations

**Dated Advice:** The passage recommends that business letters be typed by a "stenographer" (typically a young girl) and that the manager must check their formatting. It also advises against omitting the state name even when mailing within the same state (e.g., from New York to Brooklyn).

**Modern Application (Preserving the Principle):** The underlying principle is **quality control, accuracy, and professional presentation**. Today, the equivalent is not about supervising a typist, but about using your tools effectively. A modern writer should:
- **Proofread** their own drafts before sending, rather than relying on a secretary.
- **Use email preview features** and mobile testing to ensure formatting looks clean on all devices.
- **Include full, unambiguous details in the address block**, even for internal or same-city recipients (e.g., include the building name, floor, or department code).
- **Use clear, descriptive subject lines** in emails (e.g., "Q3 Marketing Budget Approval – Action Required") rather than vague ones like "Meeting" or "Update."

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary (120 words max)
When orphaned Mary Lennox is sent to live with her uncle at Misselthwaite Manor, she is described as a disagreeable, sour-looking child. Born in India to a neglectful, party-loving mother and an ill, busy father, Mary was raised by servants who obeyed her every whim. By age six, she became tyrannical and selfish. One hot morning in India, Mary wakes cross and demands her Ayah, but a strange servant tells her the Ayah cannot come. The household is mysterious and fearful; servants are missing or frightened. Mary overhears her mother speaking anxiously with a young officer about a terrible illness. Cholera breaks out, killing the Ayah and others. During the chaos, Mary hides alone in the nursery, forgotten by everyone, eating leftover food and drinking wine that makes her fall asleep.

### Mary’s Character Traits with Supporting Details
- **Disagreeable appearance**: She has a “little thin face,” “thin light hair,” and a “sour expression.”
- **Tyrannical**: She “beat and kicked” the strange servant who replaced her Ayah.
- **Selfish**: As a baby and toddler, she was kept out of sight because she was “fretful,” and her needs were always met to avoid disturbing her mother.
- **Spoiled and demanding**: She insists, “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”
- **Angry and insulting**: She mutters “Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” at her missing Ayah.
- **Lonely and forgotten**: During the cholera outbreak, “nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her.”

### Setting and Emotional Distance/Isolation
The setting of colonial India during a cholera outbreak creates intense emotional distance and isolation for Mary. The frantic, frightened servants speak in “low strange voices” and avoid explaining the mysterious events, leaving Mary bewildered and alone. Her mother, who never wanted her, is preoccupied with her own panic and escape, while Mary is forgotten in the nursery. The empty dining room with hastily abandoned chairs and plates underscores how suddenly she is abandoned. Even as death surrounds her, Mary’s only comfort comes from leftover food and wine, highlighting her profound neglect. This chaotic, indifferent environment deepens her emotional solitude, as no one notices or cares for her.

### Discussion Questions for Ages 10–12
1. Why do you think Mary’s mother handed her over to the Ayah and kept her out of sight? How does that affect Mary’s behavior?
2. How does the author show that Mary is both selfish and lonely? Can you find two examples?
3. If you were Mary during the cholera outbreak, how would you feel being forgotten by everyone? What would you do?

Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### Concise 100-Word Overview
The water cycle describes the constant movement of water on Earth, stored in oceans, atmosphere, land, and underground. Oceans hold 96% of all water (saline); the rest is fresh water in lakes, ice sheets, rivers, and groundwater. Water changes between liquid, solid, and gas as it moves. The sun’s energy drives evaporation and evapotranspiration; gravity drives precipitation, runoff, and infiltration. Water circulates through the atmosphere, across land, and underground. Human actions—water use, land use, and climate change—affect water movement, storage, and availability. Understanding these impacts helps us use water sustainably.

### Concept Map (Text Form)
Sun’s energy → Evaporation & Evapotranspiration → Atmosphere  
Atmosphere → Precipitation (rain, snow, hail) → Land & Oceans  
Land → Snowmelt → Runoff & Streamflow → Oceans  
Land → Infiltration → Groundwater (aquifers) → Springs, Rivers, Oceans  
Groundwater → Natural discharge → Ocean  
Oceans → Evaporation → Atmosphere  
(Gravity drives downward movement: precipitation, runoff, infiltration, groundwater flow)

### Key Terms (At Least Eight)
1. **Water cycle** – The continuous movement and storage of water on Earth.  
2. **Evaporation** – Liquid water changes into water vapor using energy from the sun.  
3. **Evapotranspiration** – The main way water moves into the atmosphere from land and oceans.  
4. **Precipitation** – Rain, snow, or hail that falls from clouds due to gravity.  
5. **Infiltration** – Water moves into the ground from the surface.  
6. **Groundwater recharge** – Water that infiltrates to replenify underground aquifers.  
7. **Runoff** – Water that flows across the land surface toward streams or oceans.  
8. **Aquifer** – An underground layer where groundwater flows and is stored.  

### Human Influence
Human water use, land use, and climate change alter the movement, storage, and availability of water in the cycle. Human actions affect how much water is in each pool, when and how fast water moves, and the cleanliness of water. These changes can disrupt natural flows and storage patterns. Understanding these impacts supports sustainable water use.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Update on Warehouse Operations & Customer Experience  

**Team,**  

I’m writing with a direct update on our recent warehouse management system (WMS) migration and how it’s affecting our shipping speed and your work. I want to start by acknowledging the pressure this has put on our frontline teams—especially fulfillment, customer support, and operations. You’ve been handling a difficult transition with professionalism, and I appreciate your patience and focus.  

**What happened**  

Over the last two weeks, we completed a migration from our legacy WMS to a new platform. This was a planned upgrade intended to improve long-term inventory accuracy and order routing. During the cutover, we encountered data-mapping inconsistencies between the old and new systems, specifically around bin locations and pick sequences. As a result, fulfillment teams have had to manually verify approximately 15% of orders each day—slowing throughput and delaying shipments.  

This was not caused by any single team or vendor. Our implementation partner and internal IT worked through weekends to apply patches. The root cause was a gap in how the legacy system structured location data versus the new system’s requirements, and we did not fully account for it in our testing. I take responsibility for not allocating more time for parallel testing before cutover.  

**Current customer impact**  

- **Shipping speed:** Average time from order to carrier scan increased from 1.2 days to 3.8 days over the past week. About 20% of orders placed between October 23–29 have not yet left the warehouse.  
- **Customer support volume:** Inbound tickets are up 240% versus last week, primarily about shipment delays and missing tracking updates. Average response time has increased from 4 hours to 14 hours.  
- **Returns and refunds:** We have not seen a spike in returns, but refund requests are up 50% because customers assume orders are lost.  

**What we are doing today**  

1. **Manual order verification is being triaged by order value and age.** Our ops team is prioritizing orders placed 5+ days ago and orders over $200. We have two additional supervisors on the floor this week to double-check picks.  
2. **Customer support has been cross-trained on the new WMS’s order-lookup tool** so they can provide accurate ETAs rather than generic apologies. We’ve also added a temporary auto-reply that acknowledges the delay and gives a 48-hour recheck window.  
3. **IT and our implementation partner are running a batch fix tonight** to reconcile the remaining location mismatches (about 3,800 bins). This should eliminate the manual verification step for about 80% of orders by tomorrow morning.  

**What will change next week**  

- **Monday–Tuesday:** The batch fix will be live, and we expect manual verification to drop to under 5% of orders. Shipping speed should return to 1.5–2 days by Wednesday.  
- **Wednesday:** We will pause order intake for 12 hours (midnight to noon PST) to allow fulfillment to clear the backlog. No new orders will be accepted during this window. We will communicate this to customers via email and a site banner starting Sunday.  
- **Thursday–Friday:** We’ll run a full inventory reconciliation to confirm bin accuracy. Customer support volume should decrease as tracking updates become automated again.  

**How to escalate urgent issues**  

If you encounter a situation that isn’t covered by our current workarounds—such as a warehouse safety concern, a customer escalation that ties directly to a health or safety issue, or a system error that halts an entire shift—please escalate to your direct supervisor first. If immediate, contact the on-call ops lead via Slack #ops-urgent. For customer-facing teams, if a customer needs a time-sensitive replacement (e.g., a broken item in a rental home), route directly to our expedited fulfillment queue by tagging “priority-now” in Zendesk.  

**Manager talking points (for 1:1s and stand-ups)**  

Please use these four points when discussing the situation with your teams. Keep the language simple and avoid blame.  

1. **The migration timing was mine to own.** We moved too fast during a peak shipping period. The decision was made to avoid a longer disruption in Q1, but in hindsight, I should have delayed the rollout.  
2. **The fix is data, not people.** The slowdown is due to mismatched location files, not anyone working slower. Fulfillment and support teams are doing exactly what they should.  
3. **Next week’s pause is not a demand signal.** The 12-hour intake pause is to clear backlog, not to cut costs or stop growth. We will resume normal intake Thursday morning.  
4. **Escalation is a sign of good judgment, not failure.** Encourage your teams to use #ops-urgent if they see something that risks a safety issue or a customer order going completely missing. No one will be questioned for escalating.  

**Closing**  

I know this week has been exhausting, especially for teams who are used to shipping orders on time and keeping customers informed. The long-term benefit of the new WMS—faster picking, fewer mis-ships, and real-time inventory—will be real, but I regret that the short-term cost fell on your shoulders. If you have ideas for how we can handle future transitions more smoothly, please send them directly to me or your director. I will include the most actionable suggestions in a post-mortem document shared company-wide by next Friday.  

Thank you for holding the line this week.  

Best,  
**[Your Name]**  
COO, [Company Name]  

*P.S. If you are in the warehouse or support center today, I’ll be walking the floor between 2–4 PM. Please flag me if you have a process that is still broken.*

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
The After-Rain Map of Puddle Lane

The world after a summer rain is a different place. The air smells like clean dirt and wet leaves. The grass shines like a million tiny green swords. And in the garden of the little yellow house, the best part of all was the puddles.

There were big, wobbly puddles near the tomato plants, and long, skinny puddles along the stone path. There were perfect, round puddles that looked like mirrors for the sky.

No one knew the puddles better than Milton.

Milton was a snail. He was small and brown, with a shell that whorled like a cinnamon bun. Milton was also very, very careful. Every time it rained, he would wait until the last drop fell. Then, he would creep out of his favorite spot under the rhubarb leaf, and he would begin his work.

He carried a tiny leaf, folded like a map. On it, he drew a picture of every puddle he found. A squiggle for the deep one by the hose. A dot for the tiny teacup puddle under the birdbath. A star for the wide, shallow one that looked like a silver plate.

“One has to know where the slippery mud is,” he would whisper to himself, adding a little X to his map. “And one must never, ever step into a puddle deeper than one’s head.”

Up in the maple tree, a different creature was watching. Her name was Pip.

Pip was a young sparrow. She had stubby wings and a chest the color of a toasted marshmallow. She was also very, very impatient.

“Milton!” she chirped, hopping down to a low branch. “Why do you crawl so slow? Why do you draw pictures of the ground? The sky is the place to be!”

Milton looked up. His eyes were on the ends of little stalks, and they wiggled politely. “Good morning, Pip. The ground is full of wonderful secrets after a rain.”

“Secrets are boring,” Pip said, puffing out her feathers. “Flying is the only secret worth knowing. Watch this!”

Pip fluttered down from the branch. She flapped her wings up and down, up and down. But instead of gliding gracefully, she bounced and stumbled. She hopped and tripped. Finally, she took a running start across the lawn and leaped into the air.

She went up! Up, up, up into the blue air! For a second, she was flying.

Then she didn’t know what to do next.

She crashed—*thumpa-thumpa-thump*—right into the middle of the big, wobbly puddle by the tomatoes.

*SPLASH!*

Water went everywhere. Milton hurried to his map and drew a new number next to the tomato puddle: *Depth: 1 very wet sparrow.*

“Are you okay?” Milton asked, peeking over a daisy.

Pip sat in the water, looking soggy and sad. “I can fly!” she said, her beak trembling. “I just can’t… stop.”

“Ah,” said Milton. “You have not learned how to land.”

“Landing is for boring things,” Pip sniffed.

Milton shook his head slowly. “Landing is how you come home. It is how you rest. Even the sun knows when to set.”

Pip hopped out of the puddle and shook herself dry. She watched Milton inch his way across a stone. He was slower than slow. It looked, to Pip, like the most boring thing in the world.

“I want to be brave and fast,” she said.

“Patience can be a kind of bravery, too,” said Milton. “Look.”

He pointed his eyestalk at the puddle she had just landed in. The edges were already a little smaller. The sun was peeking through the clouds, and its warm fingers were touching the water.

“Puddles don’t last forever,” Milton explained. “The sun drinks them up, tiny sip by tiny sip. They disappear into the sky. A puddle is only here for a little while. That is why I draw them. To remember them before they go.”

Pip looked at the puddle. She saw how it shimmered. She saw a dragonfly land on its surface and take off again. She saw the clouds floating in its reflection.

“I never noticed that,” she whispered.

“Come,” said Milton. “I will show you my map. And I will show you a place to practice landing.”

He led her on the slowest, most careful journey across the garden. Pip wanted to zoom ahead, but she stayed with him. Step… by… step. She saw the paths he chose—the dry ones. She saw how he avoided the big cracks. She felt the world slow down around her.

Finally, they reached a patch of soft, clover-covered earth. A small hill of moss sat beside it. “This is the best landing spot in the garden,” Milton said. “It is soft. It is dry. It will forgive a bumpy arrival.”

Pip looked at the moss. She took a breath. She fluttered up—not high, just a little. Then she aimed for the moss and—*fump!*—she landed. Her feet touched the clover. She wobbled. But she stayed up.

She did it again. And again. Each time, she went a little higher. Each time, she aimed for the soft spot Milton had shown her.

She was learning to land.

As the sun sank lower, the last puddles began to shrink. Pip watched them go. She didn’t feel sad. She felt like she understood them now.

“Thank you, Milton,” she said. “For showing me.”

Milton smiled his tiny snail smile. He rolled up his map and tucked it under his shell. “You are welcome, Pip. Where will you fly tomorrow?”

Pip looked at the sky. It was full of pink and gold. She wanted to go there.

But first, she wanted to come back.

“Tomorrow,” she said, “I will fly to the top of the maple tree. And then, I will land right here beside you.”

“I will be waiting,” said Milton. And he was. The next day, when the sun came up and the puddles were all gone, Pip flew high and fast. And then, with a gentle, careful *flump*, she landed in the clover next to her snail friend.

She had learned that the bravest thing wasn’t just flying up. It was also knowing how to come back down. That was a secret even better than a puddle.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden to Open New “Pollinator Path” Educational Trail on July 12**

**HARBORLEAF, CA – May 15, 2026** – Harborleaf Botanical Garden announced today the official opening of its newest public education attraction, the “Pollinator Path,” a dedicated trail designed to teach visitors how to support local bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds through thoughtful planting. The 0.8-mile accessible loop, located in the garden’s east meadow, will open to the public on July 12, 2026, featuring a series of hands-on learning stations, native plant demonstration beds, and volunteer-led weekend programming.

The trail was developed in direct response to growing visitor demand for practical, actionable guidance on creating pollinator-friendly habitats at home. Over the past two seasons, garden staff documented a sharp increase in questions from guests about which plants attract specific pollinators, how to design small-scale gardens for maximum ecological benefit, and what steps individuals can take to support declining insect populations. The Pollinator Path aims to answer those questions through immersive, self-guided education.

“We kept hearing the same thing from visitors: ‘I want to help the bees and butterflies, but I don’t know where to start,’” said Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf Botanical Garden. “What started as a simple interpretive sign idea grew into a full loop with multiple teaching stations, because we realized that people need to see, touch, and smell the plants that make a difference. The Pollinator Path is designed to show that even small planting decisions—like swapping one ornamental shrub for a native milkweed or adding a patch of evening primrose—can create connected habitats that stretch across neighborhoods.”

The trail begins at the east meadow entrance and winds through a series of themed zones. The “Night-Blooming Flower Station” features plants such as evening stock, moonflower, and four-o’clocks, which are specifically chosen to attract moths and nocturnal pollinators. Nearby, the “Native Plant Demonstration Beds” showcase regional species including California fuchsia, coyote mint, and buckwheat, each labeled with tactile signage that allows visually impaired visitors to identify plants by touch. The signage includes raised text and braille descriptions, as well as QR codes linking to audio guides narrated by garden educators.

In addition to signage, the trail offers youth observation cards—laminated, reusable sheets that guide children through activities such as counting flower visits per minute and identifying insect body parts. These cards are available at a kiosk near the trailhead and can be returned after use. Shaded resting points with benches and interpretive panels are placed at roughly quarter-mile intervals, allowing visitors to pause and read about specific topics like solitary bee nesting habits or the relationship between flower color and pollinator preference.

Volunteer-led weekend walks will begin the morning of July 12 and continue every Saturday and Sunday through October. Led by trained garden docents, these 45-minute walks will focus on seasonal highlights, such as which nectar sources are most active during a given week. No registration is required, and walks are free with garden admission.

The location of the trail—the east meadow loop—was chosen for its existing diversity of native grasses and wildflowers, which garden staff have supplemented with additional plantings over the past 18 months. The loop is fully accessible via paved, level pathways, meeting Americans with Disabilities Act standards. Benches are designed with armrests and back support, and the tactile signage is positioned at a height accessible to wheelchair users and children alike.

Maya Ortiz emphasized that the Pollinator Path is not a political statement but an educational resource grounded in horticultural science. “Pollinators don’t care about state lines or property boundaries. A single backyard garden can serve as a vital rest stop for a migrating monarch or a nesting site for a solitary bee. We want visitors to leave with a clear, practical plan—three plants to add, one thing to stop doing, and a sense that their own small space matters in a larger network.”

To celebrate the opening, the garden will host a ribbon-cutting ceremony at 9:00 a.m. on July 12, followed by the first guided walk at 10:00 a.m. Regular garden hours apply; admission is $12 for adults, $8 for seniors and students, and free for children under five. Garden members receive free entry year-round.

Harborleaf Botanical Garden, established in 1972, is a nonprofit horticultural institution dedicated to plant conservation, education, and public enjoyment. The garden maintains more than 50 acres of themed collections, including a desert garden, a redwood grove, and seasonal butterfly enclosures. For more information about the Pollinator Path or other programs, visit the garden’s website or contact the media relations team.

###

**Media Contact:**
Liam Stroud
Communications Manager
Harborleaf Botanical Garden
555-0198
l.stroud@harborleafgarden.org

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
- Let physical details and subtext carry the emotion; dialogue should be sparse but meaningful.
- End with a quiet unresolved gesture rather than a full confession.

message.content:
The coastal laundromat smelled of salt, damp cotton, and the faint chemical sweetness of dryer sheets. Outside, the storm had knocked out power across the strip; inside, the only light came from a squat battery lantern set on the folding counter, its glow pooling against the white linoleum like spilled honey.

Ellie had been cramming towels into a machine when the lights flickered and died. At first, she’d laughed—a short, exhausted sound—and sat down on the bench to wait. The drum of her washer had stopped mid-cycle, a half-finished load sloshing in dark water. She was still sitting there when the door chimed and a man stepped in from the rain.

He was tall, leaner than she remembered, with gray at his temples that hadn’t been there six years ago. He carried a canvas bag of laundry and a small flashlight, which he clicked off the moment he saw the lantern. For a long second, they both stood frozen in that buttery light.

“Ellie.” His voice was quiet, stripped of surprise.

“Leo.” She hadn’t meant to say his name. It came out flat, a reflex.

He set his bag down slowly, as if the motion might startle her. “I thought you moved to Portland.”

“I did. I’m just—visiting my mother.” She gestured at the dead washer. “She needed towels for the guest room. The storm had other plans.”

He nodded. The silence stretched, filled by the drumming of rain on the roof. She remembered the last time she’d seen him: a July evening, him standing on his porch two doors down, waving as she pulled out of the driveway. He hadn’t known she was leaving for good. She hadn’t told him. She’d told no one.

“You never came back,” he said. Not an accusation. A statement, worn smooth by time.

“I know.”

He moved closer, not to her, but to the bench, setting his laundry bag beside it. He sat down heavily, elbows on his knees. The lantern cast his shadow large against the tiled wall. “I left you a voicemail. About a month after you left.”

Ellie’s stomach tightened. She remembered the voicemail. She had saved it for years, until the phone itself died and she couldn’t retrieve it anymore. His voice, hesitant, asking if she was okay, if she wanted to get coffee, if she’d like to talk. She had listened to it seventeen times. She had never called back.

“I never deleted it,” she said. The words cost her something.

He looked up. His eyes were the same—hazel, with a fleck of green in the left one. “Did you listen to it?”

“Yes.”

“Then you know what I said.”

She nodded. *I miss seeing you at the window. I miss your laugh. I don’t know what I did wrong, but I’m sorry.* She had memorized the pauses, the way his voice cracked on the word *sorry*.

“I should have answered,” she said. “I was—ashamed.”

“Of what?”

She pulled her knees up onto the bench, hugging them. The rain softened to a rhythmless drizzle. “Of needing to leave. Of not being able to explain it. I didn’t have a good reason. No fight, no betrayal. I was just… hollow. And I couldn’t stay hollow in front of you.”

He let out a breath. “You think I would have judged you?”

“No.” She shook her head. “That was the problem. You would have understood. And I couldn’t bear that. It felt easier to vanish.”

The washing machine clicked, a futile sound against the darkness. Outside, a car splashed by, headlights sweeping across the glass before fading.

“I don’t need an apology,” he said. “I just—I always wondered if it was something I did. Or didn’t do.”

Ellie turned to face him fully. The lantern light caught the wet strands of her hair, the fine lines around her mouth. “You didn’t do anything wrong. You were kind. You were patient. And I was terrified of that.”

Leo reached over and picked up the lantern, setting it on the bench between them. The light shifted, casting half his face in shadow. “I’m not that patient anymore,” he said, and there was a trace of old hurt in his voice, a splinter she hadn’t heard before.

“I’m sorry,” she said. The words were small, inadequate. They sat between them like a stone in the sand.

He didn’t say *it’s okay*. He didn’t forgive her. He simply looked at the dark load of towels. “The power might not come back tonight.”

“I know.”

“You can leave them. The owner will run a new cycle in the morning.”

She laughed, a dry sound. “My mother will kill me if I come home with wet towels.”

A corner of his mouth lifted. It wasn’t quite a smile, but it was something. “There’s a coin dryer at the motel on the pier. I saw a sign. They have a generator.”

“That’s three blocks.”

“I have an umbrella.” He stood, slinging his bag over his shoulder. He didn’t offer to carry hers. He just waited, holding the lantern.

Ellie hesitated. The machine was dark, the water cold. She could leave the towels. She could go home, and tomorrow she would drive back to Portland, and she would never know if Leo had found someone else, if he still lived in the house with the yellow door, if he ever thought of her on quiet nights.

She grabbed the wet bundle and stood.

The rain had thinned to a mist. They walked side by side, the lantern swinging from his hand, her laundry bag dripping onto the pavement. He held the umbrella over her, not quite touching. Neither spoke. The air smelled of brine and asphalt, and somewhere a gull called, a lonely sound against the dark.

When they reached the motel, the clerk let them in with a nod. Leo fed coins into the dryer. Ellie stood by the window, watching the rain slick the streetlights. He came to stand beside her, close enough that she could smell the salt on his jacket.

“I still have the house,” he said, looking at nothing. “The yellow door needs painting.”

She didn’t answer. She couldn’t.

The dryer hummed. The lantern glowed. And the space between them, for the first time in six years, felt not like a distance, but like a beginning she wasn’t sure she had the courage to take.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word technical article for backend engineers titled by you. Topic: designing rate limiting for a multi-tenant API used by small teams and enterprise customers.

You must cover:
1. The difference between authentication, authorization, quotas, and rate limits.
2. Token bucket vs. fixed window vs. sliding window at a practical level.
3. How to avoid punishing an entire tenant for one noisy integration.
4. What headers and error messages should be returned to API clients.
5. Observability: metrics, logs, and alerts that show whether limits are protecting reliability without blocking legitimate use.

Constraints: no code; no vendor-specific services; include a short “recommended baseline design” section at the end.

message.content:
# Leaky Faucets and Fair Shares: Designing Rate Limits for Multi‑Tenant APIs

You’ve built an API that serves both a five‑person startup and a Fortune 500 enterprise. The startup sends 10 requests per minute; the enterprise’s automated integration fires 10,000. When the enterprise hits a rate limit, the startup’s dashboard breaks because you designed a single global counter. Your users don’t care about “tenant isolation”—they care that their own requests succeed. This article walks through how to design a multi‑tenant rate limiter that protects reliability without punishing tenants for each other’s behavior.

## Distinguishing the Four Pillars of Access Control

Before drawing boxes, engineers must agree on vocabulary. Authentication, authorization, quotas, and rate limits address different problems and failure modes.

- **Authentication** answers *who you are*. It’s the API key, OAuth token, or JWT. The limiter operates *after* authentication—an unauthenticated request should be rejected before it reaches the rate limiter.

- **Authorization** answers *what you may do*. Even if a tenant has remaining quota, they may lack permission to call a specific endpoint. Rate limits should not duplicate authorization logic; they operate on already‑authorized requests.

- **Quotas** are *financial or contractual limits*. Example: “Your plan allows 100,000 API calls per month.” Quotas are enforced asynchronously (typically via billing systems) and allow brief overages with a soft “over‑age” notification.

- **Rate limits** are *operational limits* that prevent system overload. Example: “You may send 100 requests per second.” Rate limits are enforced synchronously and should be adapted dynamically as infrastructure health changes.

A well‑designed system checks authentication, then authorization, then rate limits, and finally deducts from the quota in an eventually‑consistent manner.

## Algorithmic Options: Token Bucket, Fixed Window, Sliding Window

Three algorithms dominate production rate limiters. Each has tradeoffs that matter in multi‑tenant scenarios.

**Fixed Window** partitions time into discrete buckets (e.g., one minute). The counter resets exactly at the minute boundary. This is simple to implement but suffers from *boundary bursts*—a tenant can send 100 requests at 00:59 and another 100 at 01:01, effectively doubling capacity across the boundary. For multi‑tenant systems, fixed windows amplify noise: one noisy integration can spike at the boundary, and the reset doesn’t smooth usage for quieter tenants.

**Sliding Window Log** keeps a timestamp for each request. It provides precise, memory‑intensive enforcement. For a tenant sending 1,000 requests/sec, you maintain a log of that many timestamps. In multi‑tenant systems with thousands of tenants, memory and garbage‑collection overhead become non‑trivial. Sliding window logs are academic purity that rarely survive production cost constraints.

**Token Bucket** is the pragmatic workhorse. A bucket holds a number of tokens; tokens are added at a fixed rate (the *fill rate*) up to a *burst capacity*. When a request arrives, it consumes one token. If the bucket is empty, the request is delayed or rejected. Token buckets naturally handle bursts: a tenant that has been idle for 10 seconds can send a burst of tokens, then settle back to the fill rate. In multi‑tenant systems, each tenant receives its own bucket, and the system can assign different fill rates and bursts per tier.

Practical recommendation: notional token bucket per tenant. Enforce with a distributed counter (e.g., Redis `INCR` with expiry, or an in‑memory bucket if you accept per‑process limits). The burst size should be at least 2× the fill rate per second to accommodate client retry spikes.

## Avoiding Tenant Punishment: Per-Tenant Isolation with Global Overrides

The cardinal sin is a single global counter. Tenants become correlated—one noisy integration causes all tenants to be throttled. The first step: **every rate limit decision must be scoped to a tenant identifier** (API key prefix, customer ID, or JWK key ID).

But isolated per‑tenant buckets have a hidden failure mode. Imagine your infrastructure can handle 10,000 requests per second total. If you give each tenant a 1,000‑req/sec bucket and you have 20 tenants, a simultaneous burst from all tenants overwhelms the backend. You need **two layers of concurrency protection**:

1. **Per‑tenant bucket**: enforces the contract per customer.
2. **Global capacity bucket**: enforces the total infrastructure capacity.

The global bucket shares tokens across tenants. When the global bucket is empty, *all* tenants are throttled—but critically, a request that fails the per‑tenant bucket is rejected before it touches the global bucket. This prevents one tenant’s overage from consuming global capacity intended for others.

Additionally, implement **integration‑level sub‑buckets**. If a tenant has five API keys (one for each integration), each key should have its own token bucket, nested under the tenant bucket. This prevents a misbehaving script from consuming all of the tenant’s capacity. Why does a noisy integration matter at the tenant level? Because the tenant pays the bill. Sub‑buckets let you reject the noisy integration while the tenant’s other keys remain unthrottled.

## HTTP Headers and Error Messages: Predictability over Surprise

Clients need deterministic feedback. Return these headers on every response:

- `X-RateLimit-Limit`: the maximum requests per window (for the tenant’s plan).
- `X-RateLimit-Remaining`: remaining requests in the current window.
- `X-RateLimit-Reset`: Unix timestamp of when the window resets (or when the bucket refills). Use a consistent epoch—not “seconds from now”—so clients can schedule retries precisely.
- `Retry-After`: number of seconds the client must wait. Return this *only* when throttled (HTTP 429). The value should be the estimated time until at least one token replenishes.

HTTP status code for rejection: **429 Too Many Requests**. Never use 503 (service unavailable) for rate limits—503 implies the server is overloaded, not that the client exceeded its allowance. A client that receives 429 should retry with exponential backoff based on `Retry-After`. A client that receives 503 may throttle back, but the signal is ambiguous.

Error body (JSON):

```json
{
  "error": "rate_limit_exceeded",
  "message": "You have exceeded the rate limit of 100 requests per second. Retry after 2 seconds.",
  "retry_after_seconds": 2
}
```

Keep the message actionable. Avoid vague phrasing like “too many requests.” Mention the limit and the retry interval.

## Observability: Metrics, Logs, Alerts That Tell a Story

Rate limiters are invisible when they work. When they fail, they fail loudly. Instrument these signals:

**Key metrics** (three cardinalities: tenant, endpoint, status code):

- `rate_limit_requests_total`: count of all requests entering the limiter, labeled by `tenant_id`, `endpoint`, `decision` (allowed vs. rejected).
- `rate_limit_bucket_usage`: for each tenant, current token count. Track as a gauge. Alert when a tenant’s usage consistently stays above 90% of their limit—this predicts future rejection and suggests the client needs a plan upgrade.
- `rate_limit_global_bucket_exhausted`: boolean gauge. If the global bucket is empty for more than 5 seconds, page the on‑call team. This indicates systemic overload that per‑tenant limits cannot mask.
- `rate_limit_integration_sub_bucket_rejection`: count of rejections due to an integration sub‑bucket, broken down by `tenant_id` and `integration_id`. This metric reveals which specific integration is abusive without alerting on the entire tenant.

**Logs** are for debugging, not alerting. Log when a request is rejected with the tenant ID, sub‑bucket ID (if any), and the `Retry-After` value. Do *not* log every allowed request—your log volume will exceed your request volume.

**Alerts** should be rare but meaningful:

- **P0 alert**: Global bucket exhausted for >5 seconds → backend capacity needs scaling.
- **P1 alert**: Any single tenant is >95% rejected for >1 minute → client misconfiguration or intentional abuse.
- **P2 warning**: A tenant’s global rejection rate exceeds 10% → possible integration issue; notify the customer success team.

## Recommended Baseline Design

For most small‑to‑enterprise APIs, start here:

- **Algorithm**: Token bucket per tenant, with per‑API‑key sub‑buckets.
- **Storage**: In‑memory counters for low‑latency (if single‑process), or a shared key‑value store with 1‑second TTL for distributed deployments. Avoid global locks; use atomic increment‑and‑expire.
- **Capacity tiers**: Three tiers—Small (10 req/s, burst 20), Medium (100 req/s, burst 200), Enterprise (negotiated per contract, burst 2× fill rate). Global capacity set to 80% of your infrastructure’s peak throughput.
- **Headers**: Always return `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`. Return `Retry-After` on 429.
- **Observability**: Export per‑tenant bucket usage as a histogram (not just a gauge), and set up a dashboard that shows the ratio of allowed/rejected per tenant per endpoint.

This design is not perfect—it’s a starting point. The enterprise that runs ETL jobs at midnight will need different limits than the startup’s real‑time dashboard. Your job is to make the system adaptable without making it fragile. Rate limiting is a social contract: you promise predictable behavior; the client promises to respect the headers. Build the instrument that enforces that contract fairly.

请帮我写一个单文件 HTML 水族箱动画。

要求：
1. 画面里有多条不同颜色、不同大小的鱼，游动要尽量自然。
2. 用户左键点击水族箱时，可以在点击位置放下一粒鱼食。
3. 每条鱼会追逐离自己最近的鱼食并尝试吃掉它；没有鱼食时恢复自由游动。
4. 需要有完整的 HTML、CSS 和 JavaScript，能直接保存为一个 `.html` 文件运行。
5. 视觉效果要精致一些，不要只给伪代码或解释，直接给出完整代码。

请帮我创建一个单文件 HTML 时钟应用，要求直接给出完整代码。

功能和视觉要求：
1. 时钟表盘是圆形，有刻度、数字、时针、分针、秒针，并在表盘内部显示当前时间和日期。
2. 表盘需要有简洁的高级感：白色背景、深色边框、轻微 3D 阴影。
3. 用 JavaScript 动态生成 60 个刻度，整点刻度更粗更深。
4. 时间必须转换为中国标准时间（北京时区，Asia/Shanghai）。
5. 写一个 updateClock() 函数，计算时针、分针、秒针角度；秒针需要尽量平滑运动。
6. 使用 setInterval 或 requestAnimationFrame 驱动刷新。
7. 代码需要兼容 Edge 浏览器。

请将下面的中文商务写作材料翻译成英文。要求：面向现代职场读者，语气清楚、专业、克制；不要翻译成生硬腔调；保留作者关于“商务信函应传达信息并建立善意”的核心观点。

素材来源：根据公共领域英文书《The Book of Business Etiquette》相关章节改写的中文素材
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists the source eBook as public domain in the USA; this Chinese passage is a benchmark rewrite.

【待处理素材】
许多商务信函本不必写，而已经写出的信中，又有不少因为信息残缺、结构混乱，使原本两封信便能完成并归档的交易，拖成六封或八封往返。好的信件来自清楚的思考和周密的安排。销售函有时需要数周打磨；普通业务往来只需几分钟也足够。关键不在于花了多久，而在于最终写出的信是否能准确完成任务。

衡量商务信函的规则很多，但真正不可缺少的只有两点：第一，信必须让收件人明白你的意思；第二，它必须为公司建立善意。最好的信，是用最简短、最礼貌、最完整的方式做到这两点。每封信背后都应有明确理由，哪怕只是向客户说一声谢谢。许多精力浪费在无目的的广告、无效的推销和多余的来信上；这些文字占用了打字机和纸张，却没有推动任何事情前进。

一封好信不应让读者注意它本身的文采，而应让读者接收到它要传达的消息。某出版社曾寄出一批推销新书的测试信，回信者称赞信写得漂亮，却无人订购图书。这说明文字若抢走了信息的焦点，再优雅也不是成功的商务写作。信纸、信头、版式、段落都应整洁、克制、清楚；过分花哨的设计和故作新奇的格式，只会让人分心。

商务写作还应尊重读者的时间。清楚的标题、适当的称呼、按主题分段的正文、明确的请求和完整的联系方式，往往比华丽措辞更能建立信任。若一封信需要收件人回复，写信者应说明希望对方在何时以前回复、需要提供哪些信息，以及下一步将如何处理。这样，信件不仅传递态度，也推动事务向前。

message.content:
Many business letters should never have been written in the first place. And of those that are written, a significant number suffer from incomplete information or disorganized structure, turning what could have been a straightforward two-letter exchange into six or eight rounds of correspondence. Good letters come from clear thinking and careful planning. A sales letter may take weeks to refine; a routine business communication may require only a few minutes. What matters is not the time spent, but whether the final letter accurately accomplishes its purpose.

There are many rules for evaluating business correspondence, but only two are truly essential. First, the letter must make your meaning clear to the recipient. Second, it must build goodwill for the company. The best letters achieve both in the shortest, most courteous, and most complete manner possible. Every letter should have a clear reason behind it, even if it is simply to say thank you to a client. Too much effort is wasted on pointless advertisements, ineffective pitches, and unnecessary correspondence—words that consume typewriter ribbon and paper without moving anything forward.

A good letter should not draw attention to its own style. It should deliver its message so clearly that the reader receives the content, not the prose. A publishing house once sent out a batch of test letters promoting a new book. Many recipients praised the writing as elegant—but no one ordered the book. This shows that when language competes with the message for the reader's attention, even graceful writing fails as business communication. Letterhead, layout, formatting, and paragraph structure should all be clean, restrained, and clear. Overly elaborate design or forced novelty only distracts.

Business writing must also respect the reader’s time. Clear subject lines, appropriate salutations, topic-based paragraph breaks, explicit requests, and complete contact information build trust far more effectively than polished phrases. If a letter requires a reply, the writer should specify the expected response deadline, what information is needed, and how the next step will proceed. In this way, the letter does more than convey attitude—it moves the transaction forward.

messages.content:
请将下面的繁体中文寓言/哲理片段翻译成英文，目标读者为青少年读者。要求：保留想象力和节奏；遇到典故不要长篇解释；译文应清楚、流畅，适合作为儿童文学或寓言文本阅读。

素材来源：《莊子的故事》节选：逍遥游开篇
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23913
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
北冥有魚，其名為鯤。鯤之大，不知其幾千里也。化而為鳥，其名為鵬。鵬之背，不知其幾千里也；怒而飛，其翼若垂天之雲。是鳥也，海運則將徙於南冥。南冥者，天池也。齊諧者，志怪者也。諧之言曰：「鵬之徙於南冥也，水擊三千里，摶扶搖而上者九萬里，去以六月息者也。」野馬也，塵埃也，生物之以息相吹也。天之蒼蒼，其正色邪？其遠而無所至極邪？其視下也，亦若是則已矣。且夫水之積也不厚，則其負大舟也無力。覆杯水於坳堂之上，則芥為之舟；置杯焉則膠，水淺而舟大也。風之積也不厚，則其負大翼也無力。故九萬里，則風斯在下矣，而後乃今掊風；背負青天而莫之夭閼者，而後乃今將圖南。蜩與學鳩笑之曰：「我決起而飛，槍榆枋而止，時則不至而控於地而已矣，奚以這九萬里而南為？」適莽蒼者，三餐而反，腹猶果然；適百里者，宿舂糧；適千里者，三月聚糧。之二蟲又何知！小知不及大知，小年不及大年。奚以知其然也？朝菌不知晦朔，蟪蛄不知春秋，此小年也。楚之南有冥靈者，以五百歲為春，五百歲為秋；上古有大椿者，以八千歲為春，八千歲為秋。此大年也。而彭祖乃今以久特聞，眾人匹之，不亦悲乎！湯之問棘也是已。窮髮之北有冥海者，天池也。有魚焉，其廣數千里，未有知其脩者，其名為鯤。有鳥焉，其名為鵬，背若泰山，翼若垂天之雲，摶扶搖羊角而上者九萬里，絕雲氣，負青天，然後圖南，且適南冥也。斥鴳笑之曰：「彼且奚適也？我騰躍而上，不過數仞而下，翱翔蓬蒿之間，此亦飛之至也，而彼且奚適也？」此小大之辯也。故夫知效一官，行比一鄉，德合一君，而徵一國者，其自視也亦若此矣。而宋榮子猶然笑之。且舉世而譽之而不加勸，舉世而非之而不加沮，定乎內外之分，辯乎榮辱之竟，斯已矣。彼其於世，未數數然也。雖然，猶有未樹也。夫列子御風而行，泠然善也，旬有五日而反。彼於致福者，未數數然也。此雖免乎行，猶有所待者也。若夫乘天地之正，而御六氣之辯，以遊無窮者，彼且惡乎待哉！故曰：至人無己，神人無功，聖人無名。

message.content:
**In the Northern Ocean, there lives a fish called Kun.**  
The Kun is so huge—no one knows how many thousand miles across.  
It transforms into a bird named Peng.  
The Peng’s back stretches—no one knows how many thousand miles wide.  
When it rises in anger, its wings spread like clouds hanging from the sky.  

This bird, when the seas stir, sets out for the Southern Ocean—the Celestial Lake.  

There’s a book called *Qi Xie*, a collection of strange tales.  
It says: “When the Peng migrates to the Southern Ocean, it churns up the water for three thousand miles.  
It rides the whirlwind and soars ninety thousand miles high, leaving on the breath of June.”  

Wild horses galloping? Dust clouds swirling?  
Or living things blowing their breath at one another?  

The sky—is that its true color?  
Or is it so distant, with no end in sight?  
When the Peng looks down, that’s just how it sees the world.  

If water isn’t deep enough, it can’t carry a big boat.  
Pour a cup of water on a hollow in the floor—a mustard seed becomes a boat.  
But set a cup there, and it sticks: shallow water, big boat.  

If wind isn’t thick enough, it can’t support great wings.  
So at ninety thousand miles, the wind is below—gathered, gripped.  
Then, with the blue sky on its back and nothing in its way, the Peng heads south.  

A cicada and a little dove laugh: “We flap up, land on an elm tree or a sandalwood bush—sometimes we miss and just drop to the ground.  
Why fly ninety thousand miles to the south?”  

For a trip to the suburbs, you eat three meals and come back full.  
For a hundred-mile journey, you pound grain for a night.  
For a thousand-mile journey, you store up grain for three months.  
What do those two little creatures know?  

Small knowledge can’t match big knowledge.  
Short lives can’t match long lives.  
How do we know?  
A morning mushroom doesn’t know dawn and dusk.  
A summer cicada doesn’t know spring and autumn.  
That’s a short life.  

South of Chu, there’s a spirit tree—five hundred years for its spring, five hundred for its autumn.  
In ancient times, there was a giant tree—eight thousand years for its spring, eight thousand for its autumn.  
That’s a long life.  

And yet Peng Zu is famous for his long life—everyone tries to match him.  
Isn’t that sad?  

Tang, the wise king, asked Ji the same thing.  
In the barren north, there’s a dark sea—the Celestial Lake.  
A fish lives there, thousands of miles wide—no one knows its length. Its name is Kun.  
A bird lives there, too. Its name is Peng. Its back is like Mount Tai. Its wings are like clouds from the sky.  
It rides the whirlwind up ninety thousand miles, cuts through clouds, carries the blue sky, then heads south to the Southern Ocean.  

A little quail laughs: “Where does he think he’s going?  
I jump up, fly a few yards, then drop back—flitting among the reeds and bushes.  
That’s the best flying I know!  
So where does he think he’s going?”  

That’s the difference between small and great.  

So a man who can do a little job, help a little village,  
serve one ruler, or win one kingdom—  
he sees himself just like that quail.  

Song Rongzi would laugh at such a man.  
He wouldn’t be cheered by the whole world’s praise,  
nor saddened by its blame.  
He knew the line between self and world.  
He knew the border between honor and shame.  
That was it.  
He wasn’t always fussing about the world.  
But still, he hadn’t planted his roots deep enough.  

Then there’s Liezi, who rode the wind so coolly and beautifully.  
He’d come back after fifteen days.  
He wasn’t always hunting for luck.  
But even he had to depend on something—the wind.  

What about someone who rides the true nature of heaven and earth,  
rides the changing winds of the six seasons,  
and wanders through the endless?  
What would he have to depend on?  

So it’s said:  
The perfect person has no self.  
The spiritual person has no achievement.  
The sage has no name.

messages.content:
请将下面的中文新闻稿素材翻译成英文。目标读者为普通英语新闻读者。要求：准确、自然、新闻稿风格；不要添加事实；保留机构名的含义，可译为 U.S. Geological Survey；不要使用夸张宣传语。

素材来源：改写自 U.S. Geological Survey 水循环图发布新闻稿
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/news/national-news-release/new-usgs-diagram-re-envisions-how-earths-most-precious-commodity-cycles
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain; this Chinese source is a benchmark rewrite based on that public-domain material.

【待处理素材】
美国地质调查局发布新版水循环教学图，帮助教育者更完整地解释地球水在自然系统与人类活动之间的流动。新版图示取代了自2000年以来被大量课堂使用的旧版本。旧图多以一个大循环表现蒸发、降水、径流等自然过程，而新版把人类用水、土地利用、蓄水工程、灌溉和地下水开采等因素纳入同一张图中，展示许多小循环如何彼此连接、相互影响。

该图的开发团队咨询了一百多名教育工作者和三十多名水文学专家，并参考了美国地质调查局长期积累的水文数据。新版图示不仅说明水如何在大气、地表和地下储存、转移和改变形态，也强调水量、水质和水到达时间对可用水资源的影响。例如，降水并不会简单地从天空落下再回到海洋；一部分水会进入土壤和含水层，一部分会被植物吸收并通过蒸腾回到空气中，一部分会被水库、农田、城市排水系统和家庭用水改变路径。

设计者采用信息设计原则，让读者能顺着水的路径观察沿海平原、干旱盆地、湿润盆地和农业盆地之间的联系。图中还强调，不同区域的用水选择会影响下游水量和水质，因此理解水循环不能只看自然过程，也要观察人类活动如何改变水的储存位置、移动速度和清洁程度。新版图示最初提供英文和西班牙文版本，预期将由使用者翻译成更多语言。发布方表示，这一工具旨在帮助学生、教师和自然资源管理者以更清晰的方式理解水的复杂旅程，并认识到日常用水选择会影响水资源的可持续利用。

message.content:
**U.S. Geological Survey Releases Updated Water Cycle Diagram for Classrooms**

The U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) has released a new educational diagram of the water cycle, designed to help educators provide a more complete explanation of how Earth’s water moves through natural systems and human activities.

The updated diagram replaces a version that has been widely used in classrooms since 2000. The older diagram primarily depicted a single large cycle showing natural processes such as evaporation, precipitation, and runoff. The new version incorporates human water use, land use, water storage infrastructure, irrigation, and groundwater extraction into a single illustration, showing how many smaller cycles are connected and influence one another.

The development team consulted more than 100 educators and over 30 hydrology experts, and drew on long-term hydrological data collected by the USGS. The updated diagram not only shows how water is stored, transferred, and transformed in the atmosphere, on the surface, and underground, but also emphasizes how water quantity, quality, and timing affect the availability of water resources. For example, precipitation does not simply fall from the sky and return to the ocean; some water enters soil and aquifers, some is taken up by plants and released back into the air through transpiration, and some is diverted by reservoirs, farmland, urban drainage systems, and household use.

Using principles of information design, the diagram allows readers to trace the path of water through connections between coastal plains, arid basins, humid basins, and agricultural basins. It also highlights that water use choices in one area can affect the quantity and quality of water downstream. Understanding the water cycle, the diagram suggests, requires looking not only at natural processes but also at how human activities change where water is stored, how fast it moves, and how clean it is.

The new diagram is initially available in English and Spanish, and it is expected that users will translate it into additional languages. The USGS stated that the tool is intended to help students, teachers, and natural resource managers better understand the complex journey of water and recognize that everyday water use choices affect the sustainability of water resources.

请将下面的繁体中文小说片段翻译成英文。要求：保留人物心理、礼仪分寸和环境细节；英文要自然、有文学感，但不要过度现代化；人名可采用拼音，例如 Lin Daiyu、Jia residence。不要添加解释性段落。

素材来源：《紅樓夢》节选：林黛玉进贾府
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/24264
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
且說黛玉自那日棄舟登岸時，便有榮國府打發了轎子并拉行李的車輛久候了．這林黛玉常听得母親說過，他外祖母家与別家不同．他近日所見的這几個三等仆婦，吃穿用度，已是不凡了，何況今至其家．因此步步留心，時時在意，不肯輕易多說一句話，多行一步路，惟恐被人恥笑了他去．自上了轎，進入城中從紗窗向外瞧了一瞧，其街市之繁華，人煙之阜盛，自与別處不同．又行了半日，忽見街北蹲著兩個大石獅子，三間獸頭大門，門前列坐著十來個華冠麗服之人．正門卻不開，只有東西兩角門有人出入．正門之上有一匾，匾上大書"敕造宁國府"五個大字．黛玉想道：這必是外祖之長房了．想著，又往西行，不多遠，照樣也是三間大門，方是榮國府了．卻不進正門，只進了西邊角門．那轎夫抬進去，走了一射之地，將轉彎時，便歇下退出去了．后面的婆子們已都下了轎，赶上前來．另換了三四個衣帽周全十七八歲的小廝上來，复抬起轎子．眾婆子步下圍隨至一垂花門前落下．眾小廝退出，眾婆子上來打起轎帘，扶黛玉下轎．林黛玉扶著婆子的手，進了垂花門，兩邊是抄手游廊，當中是穿堂，當地放著一個紫檀架子大理石的大插屏．轉過插屏，小小的三間廳，廳后就是后面的正房大院．正面五間上房，皆雕梁畫棟，兩邊穿山游廊廂房，挂著各色鸚鵡，畫眉等鳥雀．台磯之上，坐著几個穿紅著綠的丫頭，一見他們來了，便忙都笑迎上來，說：“剛才老太太還念呢，可巧就來了。”于是三四人爭著打起帘籠，一面听得人回話：“林姑娘到了。”黛玉方進入房時，只見兩個人攙著一位鬢發如銀的老母迎上來，黛玉便知是他外祖母．方欲拜見時，早被他外祖母一把摟入怀中，心肝儿肉叫著大哭起來．當下地下侍立之人，無不掩面涕泣，黛玉也哭個不住．一時眾人慢慢解勸住了，黛玉方拜見了外祖母．____此即冷子興所云之史氏太君，賈赦賈政之母也．當下賈母一一指与黛玉：“這是你大舅母，這是你二舅母，這是你先珠大哥的媳婦珠大嫂子。”黛玉一一拜見過．賈母又說：“請姑娘們來．今日遠客才來，可以不必上學去了。”眾人答應了一聲，便去了兩個．不一時，只見三個奶嬤嬤并五六個丫鬟，簇擁著三個姊妹來了．第一個肌膚微丰，合中身材，腮凝新荔，鼻膩鵝脂，溫柔沉默，觀之可親．第二個削肩細腰，長挑身材，鴨蛋臉面，俊眼修眉，顧盼神飛，文彩精華，見之忘俗．第三個身量未足，形容尚小．其釵環裙襖，三人皆是一樣的妝飾．黛玉忙起身迎上來見禮，互相廝認過，大家歸了坐．丫鬟們斟上茶來．不過說些黛玉之母如何得病，如何請醫服藥，如何送死發喪．不免賈母又傷感起來，因說：“我這些儿女，所疼者獨有你母，今日一旦先舍我而去，連面也不能一見，今見了你，我怎不傷心！

message.content:
Let us speak of Daiyu. On the day she left her boat and set foot on shore, attendants from the Rongguo mansion were already waiting with a sedan-chair and carts for her luggage. Lin Daiyu had often heard her mother say that her grandmother’s household was different from others. The few third-class serving women she had seen in recent days were already remarkable in their dress and manner—how much more so would it be when she arrived at the house itself. Therefore she watched every step she took, was cautious in every moment, unwilling to speak one word more or take one step more than necessary, lest she be laughed at for her ignorance.

Once she had entered the sedan-chair and passed into the city, she looked out through the gauze window. The bustling streets and prosperous crowds were unlike anything she had seen elsewhere. After riding for some time, she suddenly noticed two great stone lions crouching on the northern side of the street, and a grand gate with three openings, its lintels carved with beast-heads. Before the gate sat a dozen men in rich clothes and fine caps. The central gate was closed, while only the eastern and western side gates were open for entry and exit. Above the main gate hung a horizontal tablet, on which were inscribed five large characters: “Imperially Commissioned Ningguo Mansion.” Daiyu thought to herself: This must be the elder branch of my maternal grandfather’s family.

With this thought, she continued westward. After a short distance, she saw another mansion with three grand gateways, equally imposing—this was the Rongguo Mansion. Her sedan-chair did not enter through the main gate, but rather through the western side gate. The bearers carried it inside for the distance of a bowshot. Just before turning a corner, they set it down and withdrew. The elderly women who had followed behind had already alighted from their own chairs and hurried forward. Two or three young page boys, seventeen or eighteen years old, neatly dressed and properly capped, took up the chair again and carried it onward. Crowds of matronly attendants walked alongside, following on foot until they reached a gate with hanging flower-patterned panels, where the chair was set down. The young pages withdrew, and the matrons came forward to lift the curtain and help Daiyu alight.

Leaning on the hand of one of the matrons, Daiyu entered through the hanging-flower gate. On either side were covered verandas connected by winding corridors, and in the center lay a passage hall. Directly before her stood a large screen of marble set in a frame of red sandalwood. Walking around the screen, she found a small three-bay hall. Behind it was the main courtyard of the inner compound. Facing her were five principal rooms, with carved beams and painted rafters. On both sides ran open corridors linking the side chambers, from which hung cages of parrots, thrushes, and other birds. Upon the stone steps sat several maids dressed in red and green. When they saw the party approaching, they quickly rose with smiles and came forward, saying, “The old lady was just speaking of you—how fortunate that you have arrived!” Two or three of them hurried to lift the door curtain, and a voice inside was heard announcing, “Miss Lin has arrived.”

The moment Daiyu stepped inside, she saw two attendants supporting an old woman with silver-white hair, who came forward to greet her. Daiyu knew at once that this was her grandmother. She was about to pay her respects, but before she could, the old woman took her into her arms and, crying out “my heart! my flesh!” wept loudly. All those standing in the room covered their faces and wept as well. Daiyu herself could not stop crying. After some time, those present gradually comforted them both. Only then did Daiyu formally bow to her grandmother.

(This was the Dowager Lady Shi, of whom Leng Zixing had spoken—the mother of Jia She and Jia Zheng.)

Presently Lady Dowager pointed out each person to Daiyu: “This is your elder uncle’s wife; this is your second uncle’s wife; this is the wife of your late elder brother, Zhu.” Daiyu made her bows to each in turn. Lady Dowager then said, “Summon the young ladies. Today a distant guest has arrived—they need not go to school.” The servants acknowledged and two of them went out. Not long after, three nurses and five or six maids came in, surrounding three young sisters. 

The first had a slightly full figure and a medium build, with cheeks like fresh lychees and a nose as smooth as goose fat. Her manner was gentle and quiet—she had a pleasing, approachable air. The second had sloping shoulders and a slender waist, a tall, willowy frame, and a face like a duck’s egg. Her eyes were handsome and her brows finely arched; her glance was lively and spirited, and her bearing one of refined elegance—one look at her and commonness fled. The third was still small, her figure not yet fully formed. Their hair ornaments, bracelets, skirts, and jackets were all alike in style.

Daiyu quickly stood up to greet them, and after they had made each other’s acquaintance, they all took their seats. The maids poured tea. They spoke of how Daiyu’s mother had fallen ill, how doctors had been called and medicine administered, and how she had been mourned and buried. At this, Lady Dowager could not help grieving again. “Among all my children,” she said, “the one I cherished most was your mother. Now she has left me without my so much as seeing her face once more. And when I see you, how can I not be heartbroken?”
