Write a single-file Node.js HTTP server named `server.js`. Use only built-in Node.js modules; no Express, no npm packages. Keep the code under 1,000 lines.

Task: implement a small JSON API for a help-desk ticket queue stored in a local JSON file.

Data model:
- Ticket: `id`, `subject`, `description`, `priority` (`low`, `medium`, `high`), `status` (`open`, `in_progress`, `resolved`), `created_at`, `updated_at`.

API requirements:
1. `GET /tickets` with optional query params `status` and `priority`.
2. `POST /tickets` to create a ticket. Validate required fields.
3. `GET /tickets/{id}` to fetch one ticket.
4. `PATCH /tickets/{id}` to update `subject`, `description`, `priority`, or `status`.
5. `DELETE /tickets/{id}` to delete a ticket.
6. `GET /metrics` returning counts by status and priority plus average age of open tickets in seconds.

Engineering requirements:
- Read/write a `tickets.json` file safely enough for sequential requests.
- Return JSON for all responses, including errors.
- Include CORS headers for local browser testing.
- Handle invalid JSON, unknown routes, unsupported methods, and missing IDs.
- Start on `127.0.0.1:8080` by default.

Output only the complete JavaScript code. Do not include explanations.

Create a single-file frontend app named `index.html`. Do not use external libraries, CDNs, images, or build tools. Keep the total code under 1,000 lines.

Task: build an accessible “meeting notes board” for a small team.

Functional requirements:
1. Users can add a note with title, owner, meeting date, tags, and body.
2. Notes appear as cards and can be filtered by owner, tag, and free-text search.
3. A note can be marked as “Action Required,” “Decision,” or “Reference.”
4. Users can edit and delete notes.
5. Persist all data in `localStorage`.
6. Provide a compact statistics strip: total notes, action-required count, unique owners, and most common tag.
7. Responsive layout: two-column card grid on wide screens, single column on narrow screens.
8. Basic accessibility: labels for inputs, keyboard-operable controls, visible focus states.

Output only the complete `index.html` content, including HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Do not include explanatory text.

Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary for modern business readers.

Output requirements:
1. A 100-word executive summary.
2. Five principles of effective business writing implied by the author.
3. A table with two columns: “Original-era advice” and “Modern equivalent.” Include at least five rows.
4. Identify any advice that may be dated and explain how to update it without losing the underlying principle.
5. Do not add claims that are not grounded in the passage.

素材来源：The Book of Business Etiquette, chapter “The Business of Writing” longer excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
Half the business letters which are written should never be written at all, and of the other half so many are incomplete or incoherent that a transaction which could be finished and filed away in two letters frequently requires six or eight.

A good letter is the result of clear thinking and careful planning. In the case of the sales-letter it sometimes takes several weeks to write one, but for ordinary correspondence a few minutes is usually all that is necessary. The length of time does not matter--it is the sort of letter which is produced at the end of it.

Books of commercial correspondence give a number of rules and standards by which a letter can be measured. But all rules of thumb are dangerous, and there are only two items which are essential. The others are valuable only as they contribute to them. The letter must succeed in getting its idea across and it must build up good will for its firm. And the best one is the one which accomplishes this most courteously and most completely in the briefest space of time (and paper).

There should be a reason back of every letter if it is only to say "Thank you" to a customer. Too much of our national energy goes up in waste effort, in aimless advertising, worthless salesmanship, ineffective letter writing, and in a thousand and one other ways. A lot of it is hammered out on the typewriters transcribing perfectly useless letters to paper which might really be worth something if it were given over to a different purpose.

A good letter never attracts the mind of the reader to itself as a thing apart from its contents. Last year a publishing house sent out a hundred test letters advertising one of their books. Three answers came back, none of them ordering the book, but all three praising the letter. One was from a teacher of commercial English who declared that he was going to use it as a model in his classes, and the other two congratulated the firm on having so excellent a correspondent. The physical make-up of the letter was attractive, it was written by a college graduate and couched in clear, correct, and colorful English. And yet it was no good. No _letter and no advertisement is any good which calls attention to itself instead of the message it is trying to deliver_.

There is not much room for individuality in the make-up of a letter. Custom has standardized it, and startling variations from the conventional format indicates freakishness rather than originality. They are like that astonishing gentleman who walks up Fifth Avenue on the coldest mornings in the year, bareheaded, coatless, sockless, clad in white flannels and tennis slippers. He attracts attention, but he makes us shiver.

Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. Certain dull-tinted papers are not bad, but gaudy colors, flashy designs, and ornate letter heads are taboo in all high types of business. Simple headings giving explicit and useful information are best. The name and address of the firm (and "New York" or "Chicago" is not sufficient in spite of the fact that a good many places go into no more detail than this), the cable address if it has one, the telephone number and the trademark if it is an inconspicuous one (there is a difference between _conspicuous_ and _distinctive_) are all that any business house needs.

Hotels are often pictured on their own stationery in a way that is anything but modest, but there is a very good reason for it. The first thing most people want to know about a hotel is what sort of looking place it is. All right, here you are. Some factories, especially those that are proud of their appearance, carry their own picture on their stationery. There is nothing to say against it, but one of the most beautiful factories in America has on its letter head only the name of the firm, the address, and a small trademark engraved in black. Sometimes a picture, in a sales letter, for instance, supplements the written matter in a most effective way. And whenever any kind of device is really helpful it should by all means be used, subject only to the limits of good taste.

It is more practical in business to use standard size envelopes. If window envelopes are used the window should be clear, the paper white or nearly so, and the typewritten address a good honest black. The enclosure should fit snugly and should be placed so that the address is in plain view without having to be jiggled around in the envelope first. A letter passes through the hands of several postal clerks before it reaches the person to whom it is addressed, and if each one of them has to stop to play with it awhile an appreciable amount of time is lost, not to mention the strain it puts on their respective tempers. The paper of which an envelope is made should always be opaque enough to conceal the contents of the letter.

Practically all business letters are typewritten. Occasionally a "Help Wanted" advertisement requests that the answer be in the applicant's own handwriting, but even this is rare. In most places the typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose, but most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible, and it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws and give suggestions for doing away with them.

The letter should be arranged symmetrically on the page with ample margins all around. Nothing but experience in copying her own notes will teach a stenographer to estimate them correctly so that she will not have to rewrite badly placed letters. It is a little point, but an important one.

Each subject considered in a letter should be treated in a separate paragraph, and each paragraph should be set off from the others by a wider space than that between the lines, double space between the paragraphs when there is single space between the lines, triple space between the paragraphs when there is a double space between the lines, and so on.

A business letter should handle only one subject. Two letters should be dispatched if two subjects are to be covered. This enables the house receiving the letter to file it so that it can be found when it is needed.

When a letter is addressed to an individual it is better to begin "Dear Mr. Brown" or "My dear Mr. Brown" than "Dear Sir" or "My dear Sir." "Gentlemen" or "Ladies" is sometime used in salutation when a letter is addressed to a group. "Dear Friend" is permissible in general letters sent out to persons of both sexes. Honorary titles should be used in the address when they take the place of "Mr.," such titles as Reverend, Doctor, Honorable (abbreviated to Rev., Dr., Hon.,) and the like. Titles should not be dropped except in the case of personal letters.

Special care should be taken with the outside address. State abbreviations should be used sparingly when there is a chance of confusion as in the case of Ga., Va., La., and Pa. "City" is not sufficient and should never be used. Nor should the name of the state ever be omitted even when the letter is addressed to some other point in the same state, as from New York to Brooklyn. And postage should be complete. A letter on which there is two cents due has placed itself under a pretty severe handicap before it is opened.

It is astonishing how many letters go out every day unsigned, lacking enclosures, carrying the wrong addresses, bearing insufficient postage, and showing other evidences of carelessness and thoughtlessness. In a town in New England last year one of the specialty shops received at Christmas time twenty different lots of money--money orders, stamps, and cash--by mail, not one of which bore the slightest clue to the identity of the sender. Countless times during the year this happens in every mail order house.

message.content:
Here is a structured English summary of the provided passage, tailored for modern business readers.

### Executive Summary (100 words)

Half of all business letters are unnecessary, and many others are so poorly written that they turn a two-letter transaction into a six-letter ordeal. Effective business writing stems from clear thinking and planning. A letter must achieve two goals: clearly convey its message and build goodwill. The best letters achieve this with courtesy and brevity. Avoid flashy formatting; good taste favors plain white paper and simple, informative letterheads. Every letter should serve a purpose. Finally, never let your writing style or format overshadow your message. If a reader praises the letter instead of responding to its content, the letter has failed.

### Five Principles of Effective Business Writing (from the passage)

1.  **Purpose and Brevity:** Write a letter only when there is a clear reason; avoid wasting time and paper on unnecessary correspondence.
2.  **Clarity and Completeness:** Every letter must successfully and completely convey its single central idea, ensuring no confusion or back-and-forth is needed.
3.  **Goodwill and Courtesy:** Always frame your communication to build or maintain positive regard for your firm, treating the reader with respect.
4.  **Message over Medium:** Ensure the design and language of the letter serve the content; never let the format or style become the focus in place of the message.
5.  **Standardization and Dependability:** Adhere to conventional formats and practical logistics (e.g., correct address, sufficient postage) to avoid delays, errors, and a perception of unreliability.

### Table: Original-Era Advice vs. Modern Equivalent

| Original-Era Advice | Modern Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| A good letter gets its idea across and builds good will in the briefest space of time (and paper). | A good email, memo, or message is concise, clear, and strengthens the relationship with the recipient. |
| Custom has standardized the make-up of a letter; startling variations indicate freakishness. | Standard business formatting (e.g., clean fonts, consistent headers) should be followed; overly creative layouts can seem unprofessional. |
| Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. | A clean, professional email template or document format is preferred; avoid distracting backgrounds, fonts, or colors in business communication. |
| Each subject should be treated in a separate paragraph; a business letter should handle only one subject. | Each topic should have its own section or paragraph; use separate emails or messages for unrelated subjects to aid clarity and filing. |
| Special care should be taken with the outside address; "City" is not sufficient, and postage should be complete. | Double-check the recipient's email address; use clear subject lines and correct attachments; ensure the message will deliver properly. |

### Dated Advice & Modern Update

**Dated Advice:**
> “At most places the typing is taken care of by girls ... it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws.”

**Why it is dated:** This advice reflects a dated and hierarchical office dynamic where writing is “dictated” by managers to typists (implied to be junior women). It also focuses on physical typing layout (margins, spacing) as a key skill.

**Modern Update (preserving the underlying principle):**
The underlying principle is **quality assurance and ownership over one’s output**. Even if you delegate the formatting, you are responsible for the final product. The modern equivalent: “If you use a template or have support staff help with document preparation (e.g., creating decks, formatting reports), you must review the final layout and logistics yourself. Today, this also means checking that hyperlinks work, attachments are included, and the document renders correctly on mobile devices.”

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary (120-word maximum)
Mary Lennox, a sickly, sour-faced nine-year-old, is sent to live with her uncle at Misselthwaite Manor after her parents die in a cholera outbreak in India. Born to a neglectful mother and a busy father, Mary was raised by servants who obeyed her every whim, making her tyrannical and selfish. On a hot morning, she awakens to find a strange servant instead of her Ayah. Furious, she kicks and beats the woman. Later, she overhears her mother speaking fearfully with a young officer about illness. Soon, wailing erupts from the servants' quarters. Cholera spreads rapidly; servants die or flee. Mary hides in the nursery, forgotten, eating leftover food and drinking wine that makes her fall into a deep sleep.

### Mary’s Character Traits with Concrete Details
- **Disagreeable-looking**: She has a “little thin face,” “thin light hair,” and “a sour expression.”
- **Tyrannical and selfish**: By age six, she is “as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived,” because servants always obeyed her.
- **Temperamental**: When the strange servant appears, Mary “threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her.”
- **Insulting**: She mutters insults, calling the absent servant “Pig! Daughter of Pigs!”
- **Neglected**: She is “forgotten by everyone” during the cholera crisis, hiding alone in the nursery.

### Setting and Emotional Distance/Isolation
The cholera-ravaged bungalow in India creates deep isolation. Mary is physically abandoned—servants flee or die, her mother ignores her, and she hides “forgotten by everyone.” The setting’s confusion and panic amplify her aloneness: “nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her.” The empty dining room with hastily abandoned meal tables reinforces emotional distance, as no one cares for her safety. Mary’s only comfort is sweet wine, which numbs her into unconsciousness, further separating her from human connection.

### Discussion Questions (Ages 10–12)
1. Why do you think Mary becomes so angry when the strange servant comes instead of her Ayah?
2. How does Mary’s mother treat her before the cholera outbreak? Does that affect how Mary behaves?
3. If you were Mary during the cholera crisis, what would you do to feel safe or find help?

messages.content:
Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### Concise 100-word Overview
The water cycle describes Earth’s water, which is in constant motion across the planet. Water is stored in the atmosphere, on land, and below ground as liquid, solid, or gas. Oceans hold 96% of Earth’s water; the rest is freshwater in lakes, rivers, ice, and groundwater. Water moves between pools via evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, snowmelt, runoff, streamflow, infiltration, groundwater recharge, and natural discharge. Solar energy and gravity drive this movement. Human water use, land use, and climate change alter water movement, storage, and availability. Understanding these impacts helps toward sustainable water use.

### Concept Map (Water Movement Among Major Pools)
```
Atmosphere
   ↑ (evaporation, evapotranspiration) | ↓ (precipitation)
Oceans & Land Surface
   ↓ (runoff, streamflow) | → (infiltration, groundwater recharge)
Groundwater (aquifers)
   ↓ (natural discharge: rivers, ocean, springs) → back to Oceans & Land Surface
```

### Key Terms (One-Sentence Definitions)
1. **Evaporation**: The process by which solar energy turns liquid water into water vapor.  
2. **Evapotranspiration**: The main way water moves into the atmosphere from land surfaces and oceans.  
3. **Precipitation**: Rain, snow, or hail that falls from clouds due to gravity.  
4. **Runoff**: Water that flows across the land surface toward streams or oceans.  
5. **Infiltration**: The movement of water into the ground from the surface.  
6. **Groundwater recharge**: The process by which infiltrated water enters and replenishes underground aquifers.  
7. **Aquifer**: An underground layer where groundwater flows and is stored.  
8. **Natural discharge**: Groundwater returning to the surface through rivers, the ocean, or springs.

### Human Influence
Human actions affect water movement, storage, and availability in three ways: (1) **water use** (e.g., withdrawing water for consumption or irrigation) alters natural pools and flow rates; (2) **land use** (e.g., construction, agriculture) changes infiltration, runoff, and evapotranspiration patterns; (3) **climate change** impacts evaporation rates, precipitation timing/amount, and snowmelt. These influences modify when and how fast water moves through the cycle and affect water cleanliness and overall availability.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Warehouse System Update – Week in Review and Next Steps  

**Team,**  

This week has been harder than we anticipated. If you’ve worked a shift in fulfillment, customer experience, or logistics over the past seven days, you already know that. I’m writing to give you a direct, honest account of what happened, where we are now, and what comes next.  

**What happened**  

On Monday, July 17, we completed the planned migration of our warehouse management system across all three fulfillment centers. The new system was tested for six weeks in a staging environment and performed within expected benchmarks. However, once live, we encountered two unanticipated issues:  

1. **Inventory location mapping** – The system began routing pickers to storage zones where inventory was not physically present, causing repeated search loops.  
2. **Order wave sequencing** – The logic that batches orders for packing sometimes dropped single-item orders into slow-moving queues, creating a backlog.  

These issues are not the result of any single team or vendor. Our engineering, operations, and vendor partners worked the same playbook that succeeded in prior migrations. The staging environment did not replicate the real-time order volume and physical layout interactions we saw under full load. That is a gap in our testing methodology—not a failure of people.  

**Current customer impact**  

- **Shipping speed:** Orders that normally move out of the warehouse in 24 hours are currently taking 72–96 hours.  
- **Customer support volume:** Inbound contacts are approximately 250% above baseline. The top driver is delivery date requests, followed by “where is my order” inquiries.  
- **Frontline workload:** Warehouse teams are working extended shifts to clear the backlog. Customer support agents are handling 1.7x their normal daily ticket count.  

**What we are doing today**  

- **Engineering** deployed a hotfix last night that corrects the inventory location mapping for approximately 83% of our SKUs. The remaining 17% (bulk items, oversized goods) are being manually overridden by floor managers today.  
- **Order wave sequencing** is being recalibrated. We expect to return to normal batching logic by end of day Saturday.  
- **Customer support** has activated an overflow queue handled by our cross-trained logistics team. These agents have read-only access to warehouse data and can provide accurate delivery windows without escalating every ticket.  
- **All fulfillment centers** have paused new pick waves in one zone to allow packers to clear the Wednesday–Thursday backlog. This will reduce total output today by about 12% but will bring us current by tomorrow morning.  

**What will change next week**  

By Monday, July 24, we plan to achieve the following:  

1. **Shipping speed at or below 48 hours** for 95% of orders.  
2. **Customer support volume returning to baseline** as order tracking data becomes accurate again.  
3. **A post-migration review** with engineering, operations, and our vendor will produce a written list of testing gaps and fixes. That document will be shared with all team leads by Wednesday.  
4. **A permanent escalation process** for any system migration: if pick accuracy or wave timing deviates by more than 5% from baseline for two consecutive hours, the migration will automatically pause and roll back to the prior system.  

**How to escalate urgent issues**  

If you encounter a customer or operational issue that requires immediate attention:  

- **Warehouse floor supervisors** – call the Ops Hotline (ext. 7821) and request a “Red Button” escalation. This bypasses normal ticket routing and reaches the director on call within 10 minutes.  
- **Customer support agents** – if a customer is at risk of cancellation or a refund request is related to this migration, you have authority to offer a 15% discount on their next order without supervisor approval. Use code **WMS-0723** in the system.  
- **Any employee** – if you see a safety concern or a process failure that feels wrong, tell your manager immediately. No one gets in trouble for raising a hand.  

**Manager talking points – please share with your teams in stand-ups today**  

These are the key messages to relay to your direct reports. Use your own words, but keep the substance consistent.  

- The system migration caused a slower-than-expected shipping week. This was not caused by any team’s mistake—the staging test simply did not catch how the system would behave under peak real-world conditions.  
- Customer support volume is elevated because order tracking became unreliable. The fix we deployed last night should restore accurate tracking by Monday.  
- Overtime shifts are voluntary. If you are working extra hours, thank you—your manager will ensure you are paid correctly. If you need to step back, you may do so without penalty.  
- Escalate any blocker to the Ops Hotline with “Red Button” priority. We would rather pause work and fix a problem than push through errors that hurt customers or team morale.  

**Closing**  

I know this week has drained energy, patience, and goodwill. The people in our fulfillment centers and customer support centers have absorbed the loudest friction, and they deserve recognition and support, not blame. If you are a manager, please check in with your team members directly—ask how they are doing, not just how the work is going.  

We will not pretend this was a smooth transition. We will fix it, learn from it, and make our next migration better.  

Thank you for showing up and holding the line.  

James Chen  
Chief Operating Officer  
HomeGoods Direct

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
The Map of Every Puddle

The summer rain had stopped. The whole world smelled like wet dirt and washed-clean leaves. Drops still hung from the bluebell stems, sparkling like tiny glass beads in the returning sun.

At the base of the old stone wall, a small snail named Silas was very, very busy. He inched his careful way across the garden path, his shell a neat spiral of brown and cream. Underneath his soft belly, he was pushing a rolled-up leaf. It was his most important possession: his Map of Every Puddle.

Silas was not fast. He was never fast. But he was thorough. He stopped at a shiny, silver dime-sized pool near a crack in the wall. “Puddle Number Six,” he murmured, unrolling his leaf. With a tiny leg, he traced a wet mark onto his map. “Shaped like a bean. Depth: one and a half snail-bodies. Sun will reach it by mid-afternoon.”

Every puddle in the garden was different. The deep, dark one under the rhubarb leaf (Puddle Number Three) lasted for days. The skinny, wiggly one between the paving stones (Puddle Number Eleven) was the best for a cool sip. Silas knew them all. Knowing the map kept him safe, and knowing when a puddle would dry was, to a careful snail, very fine bravery.

Suddenly, a shadow flickered over him. *Whoosh!*

A small sparrow tumbled out of the sky, landing not in a graceful swoop, but in a clumsy flop that sent a splash of muddy water all over Silas’s map.

“Oh! Bother and feathers!” cheeped the sparrow. She was young, with a fluffy chest and wings that seemed a little too big for her body. Her name was Pip.

Silas sighed a long, slow snail sigh. “That was my best puddle corner,” he said quietly.

Pip shook herself, sending more droplets flying. “Sorry! I was trying to fly. I mean, I *was* flying. Then I tried to turn, and the wind tickled me, and— *plop.*” She looked at Silas with bright, sorry eyes. “You’re awfully slow. How do you ever get anywhere?”

“Slow is how I arrive safely,” said Silas, carefully wiping mud from his leaf map. “I need to know where I am. Especially now, after the rain. Have you seen Puddle Number One? It’s the biggest.”

Pip hopped in a little circle. “Big puddle! Big puddle! By the birdbath, near the fallen sunflower! I can get there in two wing-flaps. Come on!” She spread her wings.

“I cannot fly,” said Silas patiently.

“Oh. Right.” Pip put her head to one side. “Can you at least hurry?”

“Hurrying is not my way. But I can give you directions. You could go and see if it’s still there.” Silas looked at his map. The mark for Puddle Number One was a big, happy circle. “It was ten snail-lengths wide this morning. But the sun is hot. It might be shrinking.”

Pip was already hopping away. “I’ll tell you!” she chirped, and with a mighty flap, she launched herself into the air. But she didn’t go straight. She wobbled. She dipped. She nearly flew into the clothesline. She had learned how to push off, but coming down was still a mystery.

A moment later, she was back, landing with a *thump* on a mossy stone. “It’s still there!” she panted. “But it’s smaller! The edges are all sparkly and… going away?”

Silas nodded. “That’s the sun drinking it. Puddles don’t last forever. They give their water back to the sky, and then the garden gets a drink next time it rains.”

“Oh,” said Pip, looking sad. “So my favorite splashing spot is disappearing?”

Slowly, slowly, Silas reached Puddle Number Six again. “Every puddle has a life. Mine is a long one, on the ground. A puddle’s is a short one, in the sun. And yours, little sparrow, is in the air. But all of them take patience.”

Pip fluffed her feathers. “I don’t like patience. I want to fly *now.* I want to loop and zoom and feel the wind under my belly. But coming down is so hard! The ground comes up so fast!”

Silas looked at her. Her wings were trembling. She wasn’t just impatient. She was scared. Scared of the landing.

“Patience can be a kind of bravery,” he said softly. “It’s brave to wait until you are ready. It’s brave to learn each part, one little step. Or, in your case, one little glide.”

Pip looked at the big puddle in the distance. Then she looked at Silas’s careful map. “Will you show me?” she whispered. “How you do it?”

“I can show you how I cross a path,” said Silas. “It is very boring.”

“Good,” said Pip. “I think I need boring.”

So Silas pointed a tiny horn. “Follow me. First, I check for dangers. Pebble ahead. We go around.” He inched to the left. Pip hopped beside him.

“Now, a dry patch. Very dangerous for a snail. I must glide very slowly, keeping my belly wet.” He moved like a tiny, gentle wave.

Pip did not hop ahead. She stayed beside him. She watched how he used his feelers to taste the air. She saw how he knew exactly how far he could go before he needed to rest.

They reached the puddle by the birdbath. It was now only as big as a dinner plate. The sun was making its edges curl like thin glass.

“It’s almost gone,” said Pip.

“Then it is time to say goodbye,” said Silas.

Pip looked at the shrinking water. She saw her reflection in the middle, where it was still deep. She thought about the brave, slow snail who had walked all this way just to check on a puddle that would be gone by evening.

“I think I will practice landing on that low branch,” said Pip, pointing with her beak. “Just from a hop. Not a fly.”

Silas smiled his tiny, hidden snail smile. “That sounds very brave.”

Pip hopped onto the mossy rock. She flapped her wings just a little, lifted her feet, and sailed the shortest distance in the world—three inches—to the low branch. Her claws gripped it. She wobbled. But she did not fall.

She had landed.

“I did it!” she chirped.

“You did,” said Silas. He marked his map one last time. *Puddle Number One. Gone at noon. Was good while it lasted.*

And the sun shone down on the careful snail and the patient sparrow, and the garden was quiet, full of waiting, full of wonder, one slow step and one small landing at a time.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden Opens New Pollinator Path to Inspire Community Habitat Efforts**

**HARBORLEAF, PA – June 2, 2026** – Harborleaf Botanical Garden today announced the opening of the “Pollinator Path,” a new public education trail designed to demonstrate how small-scale planting choices can support bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds. The trail will officially open to visitors on July 12, 2026, along the east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible route that winds through native plant demonstration beds, a night-blooming flower station, and multiple shaded resting points.

The project was developed in direct response to visitor feedback. Over the past two years, garden staff noted a recurring theme in guest comments and program evaluations: many visitors expressed a desire for more practical, hands-on guidance about which plants to grow at home to attract and sustain local pollinators. Harborleaf’s horticulture and education teams collaborated to design a trail that translates scientific best practices into an immediate, sensory experience.

“We kept hearing people say, ‘I want to help the bees, but I don’t know where to start,'” said Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf Botanical Garden. “The Pollinator Path gives visitors a real-world look at how small planting decisions—choosing one native plant over a non-native, or leaving a patch of bare ground for ground-nesting bees—can create connected habitats across entire neighborhoods. Our goal is to show that every garden, no matter how small, can be part of a larger pollinator corridor.”

The trail’s design emphasizes accessibility and multi-sensory learning. The 0.8-mile loop features a smooth, compacted surface suitable for wheelchairs, strollers, and mobility aids. Along the route, tactile signage provides raised illustrations and braille descriptions of key plants and insect life cycles, allowing visitors who are blind or have low vision to engage with the content. Youth observation cards will be available at the trailhead; children can use them to record sightings of pollinators and note specific plant preferences, turning a casual walk into a structured scientific observation.

One of the trail’s signature features is a night-blooming flower station, which will showcase species that attract moths and nocturnal pollinators. This station will be illuminated with low-level moonlight-colored lighting that does not interfere with the flowers’ natural cycles or disorient night-flying insects. Information panels at this station explain the ecological role of moths and highlight the contrast between day and night pollination strategies.

Native plant demonstration beds are planted in clusters that mirror natural habitat types: a dry, sandy patch for species like butterfly milkweed and purple coneflower; a moist, shaded area for cardinal flower and blue lobelia; and a sun-drenched section for goldenrod, asters, and bee balm. Each cluster is labeled with common and scientific names, bloom periods, and the specific pollinators it supports, giving visitors a clear reference for replicating similar groupings at home.

Shaded resting points are spaced at regular intervals along the trail, each equipped with a bench and a small interpretive panel that offers a “habitat tip of the spot”—a simple suggestion such as leaving leaf litter for overwintering insects or providing a shallow water source for bees. The trail also includes a designated “pause zone” where volunteers from Harborleaf’s docent program will lead weekend walks starting in late July. These free, volunteer-led walks will run each Saturday morning through the end of September, offering visitors the chance to ask questions in real time while observing active pollinators.

The Pollinator Path was funded through Harborleaf Botanical Garden’s general operating budget and visitor support programs. No external grants or partner organizations were involved in the trail’s development. The garden plans to maintain and update the trail’s interpretive content on an annual basis to reflect shifts in plant performance and emerging pollinator research.

Harborleaf Botanical Garden, located at 240 Meadow Lane, has served the region for more than 40 years as a public space for conservation education, plant research, and community recreation. The garden’s 65 acres include native woodlands, a sensory garden, a greenhouse complex, and more than 500 labeled plant species. The institution operates year-round and does not receive state or federal funding for its educational programming.

“This trail is not about telling people what not to do,” Ortiz added. “It is about showing what can be done, easily and beautifully, to make a measurable difference in pollinator health. When someone plants a small patch of phlox or leaves a caterpillar on a milkweed, they are joining a larger network of stewards. That is a powerful thought, and we hope the Pollinator Path helps people see how close that power lies to their own backyards.”

The Pollinator Path will open at 9 a.m. on July 12, 2026. Regular garden admission applies. Youth observation cards will be available free of charge at the visitor center. No separate reservation is required for the trail, but weekend walks will have a limit of 20 participants and may fill quickly.

**Media Contact:**
Leona Tran
Communications Coordinator
Harborleaf Botanical Garden
(555) 233-9781
ltran@harborleafbotanical.org

**##**

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
- Let physical details and subtext carry the emotion; dialogue should be sparse but meaningful.
- End with a quiet unresolved gesture rather than a full confession.

message.content:
The Laundromat at the Point

The power had been out for three hours when the tide brought her back.

Maya stood in the doorway of the Shorebreak Laundromat, holding a plastic basket of damp towels and a battery lantern she’d found in her trunk. The storm had rolled in fast that afternoon—salt-wind and black sky—and she’d driven twenty minutes inland only to find that everywhere from the gas station to the diner was dark. The laundromat, at least, had a roof. And a few coin-op machines. And, at the far end of the fluorescent gloom, a man sitting on a folding chair with a lantern of his own.

He looked up when she entered, and the yellow light caught his face in a way that made her stop breathing.

It was Leo. The neighbor who had disappeared from her life six years ago, the summer after college, without a word. No note. No call. Just an empty apartment, a key left on the counter, and a voicemail she had never deleted.

He looked older. There was gray at his temples that hadn’t been there before. His jaw was sharper, his shoulders heavier under a canvas jacket. He held a book open on his knee, but his eyes were fixed on her with a stillness that felt like a held breath.

“Maya.”

His voice was the same. Low, careful, with a slight rasp that used to make her chest ache.

She set the basket down on the nearest machine. The lantern swayed in her grip.

“Leo.”

The word came out flat, which was better than what she felt. Her heart was beating in her throat. She busied herself with the lantern, setting it on the dryer next to his.

For a long moment, neither spoke. The only sound was the hollow tick of cooling metal and the rain against the windows. The machines sat dead and silent, like monuments to a world that had stopped.

“You still live here?” he asked.

“I moved back last year. My dad’s sick.” She paused. “You?”

“Just passing through. I was driving up the coast from San Diego. The storm caught me.”

She nodded. She didn’t ask where he’d been for six years. She didn’t ask about the voicemail. She opened the lid of an empty washer and stared into its dark drum as if it might offer an escape route.

“I’m sorry,” he said.

The words fell softly, like dust. Maya didn’t turn around.

“Sorry for what?”

“For leaving the way I did. For not saying goodbye. For—a lot of things.”

She closed the lid. A soft click echoed in the empty room.

“I left you a message,” she said. “That night. I called you three times. You never answered.”

Leo set his book down. His lantern cast long shadows across his face. “I know. I listened to it.”

She turned then, sharply. “You listened to it?”

“I listened to all of them. The night I left, and then again a week later. And then I kept listening, every few months, for about two years. It was like—like a photograph I couldn’t throw away.”

Her throat tightened. She remembered what she had said in that voicemail. It had been raw, unguarded, full of questions she never got to ask. *Did I do something wrong? Are you okay? Please, just call me back.* The last one, the third, had been shorter. *I wish you’d said goodbye.*

She moved toward the row of plastic chairs and sat down, her hands folded in her lap. The lantern light pooled between them.

“Why did you go?”

Leo looked at his hands. They were clean, calloused at the palms. The hands of someone who worked with wood, or rope, or something tangible.

“I didn’t know how to stay,” he said. “I was twenty-three. My father had just died. I was in debt. I felt like I was drowning, and every time I looked at you, I felt worse, because you were the one thing in my life that wasn’t broken, and I was going to break it.”

Maya was quiet. He continued.

“I didn’t have the words to explain. I still don’t. I thought if I said goodbye, I’d fall apart. So I just—left. I told myself it was kinder to let you hate me than to drag you into all of it.”

“I never hated you,” she said. Her voice was level, but there was a tremor beneath it. “I was confused. I was hurt. But I never hated you.”

The rain grew heavier. A gust of wind rattled the door.

Leo looked at the basket of towels. “You need these dry?”

“I don’t even know why I brought them. The power’s out.” She gave a hollow laugh. “I guess I just needed somewhere to go.”

“Me too.”

They sat in the fragile silence that followed. Maya reached out and touched the handle of her lantern, shifting it an inch closer to his. The two pools of light merged.

“I still have that voicemail,” she said quietly. “On an old phone. I’ve never deleted it.”

Leo looked at her. His expression was unreadable, but his eyes were wet.

“What does it say?”

She met his gaze. “You know what it says. You listened to it.”

“I want to hear you say it.”

She hesitated. Then, softly, she repeated the words she had spoken into the dark six years ago.

“I said: ‘I don’t understand why you left. But I’m not angry. I just need to know if you’re okay. Please call me. I’ll wait as long as it takes.’”

Leo’s breath caught. He pressed the heels of his hands to his eyes.

“I was never okay,” he said. “But I am now. Mostly.”

Maya looked down at the towels. The cotton was still damp. She had brought them for no reason, and yet somehow they had become the center of this accidental, impossible night.

The lights flickered—once, twice—then held. A low hum returned to the machines. The power was coming back.

Neither of them moved.

The fluorescent lights buzzed overhead, harsh and yellow. Maya could see him clearly now: the lines around his mouth, the quiet steadiness in his posture. He was not the boy who had left. He was someone else. A stranger wearing a familiar face.

He stood. “I should let you do your laundry.”

She stood too. “You don’t have to.”

He stopped. They were close enough that the warmth of his lantern reached her arm.

“Maybe I’ll stay,” he said. “For a while.”

She nodded. Then she opened the lid of the washer and began to load the towels, one by one, slowly. She could feel him watching her. She did not look up.

He picked up his book and sat back down.

The rain continued. The machines began to hum. And the old voicemail, for the first time in six years, felt less like a wound and more like a door that had been left slightly ajar.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word technical article for backend engineers titled by you. Topic: designing rate limiting for a multi-tenant API used by small teams and enterprise customers.

You must cover:
1. The difference between authentication, authorization, quotas, and rate limits.
2. Token bucket vs. fixed window vs. sliding window at a practical level.
3. How to avoid punishing an entire tenant for one noisy integration.
4. What headers and error messages should be returned to API clients.
5. Observability: metrics, logs, and alerts that show whether limits are protecting reliability without blocking legitimate use.

Constraints: no code; no vendor-specific services; include a short “recommended baseline design” section at the end.

message.content:
# Designing Rate Limiting for Multi-Tenant APIs: Balancing Small Teams and Enterprise Customers

## 1. Authentication, Authorization, Quotas, and Rate Limits – The Critical Distinctions

Before designing rate limiting, we must clarify four distinct concepts that are frequently conflated:

**Authentication** verifies *who* you are. It answers: "Is this request coming from a valid API key, OAuth token, or client certificate?"

**Authorization** determines *what you may do*. It answers: "Does this tenant have permission to call this endpoint, perform this action, or access this resource?"

**Quotas** define *how much you may consume over a long period* (e.g., 10,000 requests per month, 5 GB of storage per day). Quotas are enforced periodically (often daily or monthly) and are typically soft limits—exceeding them might degrade service or require contacting sales, not immediately reject requests.

**Rate limits** control *how fast you may consume* over short windows (e.g., 100 requests per second, 10,000 per hour). Rate limits protect system stability, prevent accidental loops, and guard against a single noisy client degrading service for others. Exceeding a rate limit should *immediately* reject the offending request.

The practical consequence: quota enforcement can be offline and eventually consistent; rate limiting must be evaluated on every request, in the hot path, with minimal latency.

## 2. Token Bucket vs. Fixed Window vs. Sliding Window – Practical Trade-offs

### Fixed Window (e.g., reset every 60 seconds)
- **Pros**: Simple to implement; requires only a counter per tenant and a window boundary.
- **Cons**: Burst traffic at the window boundary can cause double throughput. Example: a client sends 100 requests at 59.9s and 100 requests at 60.1s—both windows allow 100 requests, so the server sees 200 in 0.2s.
- **Practical use**: Acceptable for very coarse limits (e.g., 10,000/hour) where the boundary spike is tolerable, but dangerous for sub-minute limits.

### Sliding Window Log
- **Pros**: Perfectly smooth—tracks timestamps of every request in the current window. No boundary spikes.
- **Cons**: O(N) memory per tenant where N is the limit. For 10,000 requests per minute, you store 10,000 timestamps in memory for that tenant.
- **Practical use**: Unworkable for high-traffic enterprise tenants. Use only for very low limits (e.g., 10 requests per second).

### Sliding Window Counter (Approximate)
- **Pros**: Uses two counters (current and previous window partial) with a weighted calculation. O(1) memory per tenant. No boundary spike.
- **Cons**: Slightly imprecise (usually within 5-10% of true rate). Implementation requires careful handling of overlapping windows.
- **Practical use**: The best general-purpose choice for multi-tenant APIs. Provides near-perfect fairness with minimal memory.

### Token Bucket
- **Pros**: Allows bursts up to bucket capacity while enforcing average rate. Intuitive: refills tokens at a steady rate; requests consume tokens.
- **Cons**: Requires maintaining both token count and last refill timestamp. Burst behavior can still overwhelm backends if bucket size is too large.
- **Practical use**: Excellent for APIs where clients need occasional spikes (e.g., CI/CD pipelines). **Recommendation**: Combine token bucket with a hard cap on burst size (e.g., bucket of 1,000 tokens, refill 100/s, but never allow more than 1,000 requests in any 10-second window).

**Practical rule**: Use sliding window counter for per-second limits, token bucket for per-minute/ per-hour limits, and fixed window only for daily quotas.

## 3. Protecting the Tenant from One Noisy Integration

The cardinal sin of multi-tenant rate limiting: a malfunctioning integration (e.g., a retry loop, a stuck webhook consumer, a misconfigured script) can exhaust the entire tenant's rate limit, blocking all other users, API keys, and services on that tenant.

### Solution: Hierarchical Rate Limiting

Implement three levels of rate limits, enforced in order:

1. **Global tenant limit** (per-tenant, across all keys). This is the outermost gate. Protects your infrastructure from a tenant going rogue.
2. **Per-integration (API key) limit**. Each API key gets its own bucket/ counter. This is *lower* than the global tenant limit. The sum of all per-key limits should be slightly less than the global limit (e.g., 90% of global).
3. **Per-endpoint limit** (optional). Write-heavy endpoints (create, update) get separate limits from read-heavy endpoints (get, list).

**Behavior**: If "Integration A" goes into a retry storm, it hits its per-key limit first. Other API keys on the same tenant continue to operate normally. Only if every key is saturated does the global tenant limit engage.

Additionally, separate *burst* capacity from *sustained* capacity. Every tenant gets a small burst pool (e.g., 50 requests) that can be used by any key—but when exhausted, each key must stay within its own rate. This prevents a legitimate new integration from being denied just because another key exhausted the tenant's burst cushion.

## 4. Headers and Error Messages – A Contract, Not a Surprise

Clients must be able to programmatically understand their limit status. Every response (success and error) should include:

**Required headers on every response:**

| Header | Content | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| `X-RateLimit-Limit` | The limit for the current enforcement period | `100` (requests per minute) |
| `X-RateLimit-Remaining` | Requests remaining in the current window | `73` |
| `X-RateLimit-Reset` | Unix timestamp when the window expires | `1735689600` |

**When rate limited (HTTP 429 Too Many Requests):**

Return a JSON body with:

```json
{
  "error": {
    "code": "RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED",
    "message": "API rate limit exceeded for this API key. Limit: 100 requests per minute. Reset at: Mon, 01 Jan 2024 00:00:00 UTC.",
    "retry_after": 12
  }
}
```

- **`retry_after`** (seconds until retry): Critical for clients implementing exponential backoff.
- **Never return `message: "Too many requests"` without details.** Developers need to know *which* limit they hit (key-level vs. tenant-level) and *when* they can retry.

For tenant-level blocking: return a different error code, e.g., `TENANT_RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED`, so the client knows it's not just their key but an entire account issue.

## 5. Observability – Proving Limits Work

Rate limits are a defensive mechanism. You must observe three things:

| Sensor | What it measures | How to use it |
|--------|------------------|---------------|
| **Requests per second** (per tenant) | Raw throughput | Baseline for setting limits. Alert if a single tenant > 50% of total capacity. |
| **429 response rate** (per tenant, per key) | How often limits are hit | Alert if 429 rate > 5% of requests for any key (suggests integration is misconfigured). |
| **Rate limit latency** | Time spent checking limits | Must be < 1ms p99. If > 5ms, your rate limiter is the bottleneck. Log p99/p999. |

**Alerts (actionable, not noise):**

1. **P1**: Any tenant's 429 rate exceeds 50% of its requests for > 5 minutes → likely a loop. Contact the tenant.
2. **P2**: Global rate limit latency p99 > 5ms → your rate limiter implementation is failing. Switch to local Redis/ in-memory + replica reads.
3. **P3**: A tenant consistently uses 90%+ of its limit but never hits it → consider offering a plan upgrade.

**Logging**: Every 429 response should log: tenant_id, api_key_id, endpoint, current limit, remaining, reset time. Do *not* log every successful request—too expensive.

**Metrics (internal dashboards)**: Track rate limit state per tenant as a gauge: `remaining_count / limit` ratio. When this ratio stays below 0.1 for an extended duration, it's a signal that limits may be too tight or the client needs a higher tier.

## Recommended Baseline Design

For a backend serving small teams (10-100 req/s burst) and enterprises (1000+ req/s sustained):

- **Algorithm**: Sliding window counter for per-second limits; token bucket for per-minute limits. Store counters in Redis (or equivalent in-memory store with TTL) partitioned by tenant + API key.
- **Hierarchy**: Three tiers—global tenant limit (e.g., 5000 req/min), per-key limit (e.g., 2000 req/min per key, with max 5 keys per tenant), and a system-wide ceiling (e.g., 100,000 req/min total).
- **Burst buffer**: Each tenant gets a 50-request burst pool shared across all keys, refreshed every minute.
- **Headers**: Always return `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`. On 429, include `retry_after` in seconds and human-readable `message` with limit details.
- **Error codes**: Separate `RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` (per-key) from `TENANT_RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` (global).
- **Observability**: Log every 429 with tenant_id and key_id. Alert on 429 rate > 5% per key, or rate limiter latency > 5ms. Visualize remaining/limit ratio per tenant over time.

This design keeps small teams unblocked, protects enterprises from their own mistakes, and ensures your API remains available and fair.

请帮我写一个单文件 HTML 水族箱动画。

要求：
1. 画面里有多条不同颜色、不同大小的鱼，游动要尽量自然。
2. 用户左键点击水族箱时，可以在点击位置放下一粒鱼食。
3. 每条鱼会追逐离自己最近的鱼食并尝试吃掉它；没有鱼食时恢复自由游动。
4. 需要有完整的 HTML、CSS 和 JavaScript，能直接保存为一个 `.html` 文件运行。
5. 视觉效果要精致一些，不要只给伪代码或解释，直接给出完整代码。

请帮我创建一个单文件 HTML 时钟应用，要求直接给出完整代码。

功能和视觉要求：
1. 时钟表盘是圆形，有刻度、数字、时针、分针、秒针，并在表盘内部显示当前时间和日期。
2. 表盘需要有简洁的高级感：白色背景、深色边框、轻微 3D 阴影。
3. 用 JavaScript 动态生成 60 个刻度，整点刻度更粗更深。
4. 时间必须转换为中国标准时间（北京时区，Asia/Shanghai）。
5. 写一个 updateClock() 函数，计算时针、分针、秒针角度；秒针需要尽量平滑运动。
6. 使用 setInterval 或 requestAnimationFrame 驱动刷新。
7. 代码需要兼容 Edge 浏览器。

请将下面的中文商务写作材料翻译成英文。要求：面向现代职场读者，语气清楚、专业、克制；不要翻译成生硬腔调；保留作者关于“商务信函应传达信息并建立善意”的核心观点。

素材来源：根据公共领域英文书《The Book of Business Etiquette》相关章节改写的中文素材
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists the source eBook as public domain in the USA; this Chinese passage is a benchmark rewrite.

【待处理素材】
许多商务信函本不必写，而已经写出的信中，又有不少因为信息残缺、结构混乱，使原本两封信便能完成并归档的交易，拖成六封或八封往返。好的信件来自清楚的思考和周密的安排。销售函有时需要数周打磨；普通业务往来只需几分钟也足够。关键不在于花了多久，而在于最终写出的信是否能准确完成任务。

衡量商务信函的规则很多，但真正不可缺少的只有两点：第一，信必须让收件人明白你的意思；第二，它必须为公司建立善意。最好的信，是用最简短、最礼貌、最完整的方式做到这两点。每封信背后都应有明确理由，哪怕只是向客户说一声谢谢。许多精力浪费在无目的的广告、无效的推销和多余的来信上；这些文字占用了打字机和纸张，却没有推动任何事情前进。

一封好信不应让读者注意它本身的文采，而应让读者接收到它要传达的消息。某出版社曾寄出一批推销新书的测试信，回信者称赞信写得漂亮，却无人订购图书。这说明文字若抢走了信息的焦点，再优雅也不是成功的商务写作。信纸、信头、版式、段落都应整洁、克制、清楚；过分花哨的设计和故作新奇的格式，只会让人分心。

商务写作还应尊重读者的时间。清楚的标题、适当的称呼、按主题分段的正文、明确的请求和完整的联系方式，往往比华丽措辞更能建立信任。若一封信需要收件人回复，写信者应说明希望对方在何时以前回复、需要提供哪些信息，以及下一步将如何处理。这样，信件不仅传递态度，也推动事务向前。

message.content:
Many business letters need never have been written, and of those that are written, a fair number are so incomplete in information or so muddled in structure that a transaction which could have been settled and filed in two letters drags on through six or eight. Good letters come from clear thinking and careful planning. A sales letter may sometimes require weeks of refinement; a routine business reply may be adequate after only a few minutes. What matters is not how long it took, but whether the final draft does its job with precision.

There are many rules for judging a business letter, but only two are truly indispensable. First, the letter must make your meaning clear to the reader. Second, it must build goodwill for the company. The best letters accomplish both goals in the shortest, most courteous, and most complete manner possible. Every letter should have a clear reason behind it—even if that reason is simply to thank a customer. A great deal of energy is wasted on aimless advertising, ineffective solicitation, and unnecessary correspondence—words that occupy typewriters and paper without moving anything forward.

A good letter should not draw attention to its own style; it should let the message come through. One publisher once sent out a batch of test letters promoting a new book. Recipients praised the writing as elegant, but no one placed an order. This shows that when language steals focus from the substance, no amount of polish makes it successful business writing. Letterhead, layout, and paragraphing should be clean, restrained, and clear. Overly ornate design or deliberately novel formatting only distracts.

Business writing should also respect the reader’s time. Clear subject lines, appropriate salutations, body text organized by topic, explicit requests, and complete contact details build trust far more effectively than fancy phrasing does. If a letter requires a reply from the recipient, the writer should specify the desired response date, the information needed, and what the next step will be. A letter written this way does more than convey an attitude—it moves a transaction forward.

【待处理素材】
北冥有魚，其名為鯤。鯤之大，不知其幾千里也。化而為鳥，其名為鵬。鵬之背，不知其幾千里也；怒而飛，其翼若垂天之雲。是鳥也，海運則將徙於南冥。南冥者，天池也。齊諧者，志怪者也。諧之言曰：「鵬之徙於南冥也，水擊三千里，摶扶搖而上者九萬里，去以六月息者也。」野馬也，塵埃也，生物之以息相吹也。天之蒼蒼，其正色邪？其遠而無所至極邪？其視下也，亦若是則已矣。且夫水之積也不厚，則其負大舟也無力。覆杯水於坳堂之上，則芥為之舟；置杯焉則膠，水淺而舟大也。風之積也不厚，則其負大翼也無力。故九萬里，則風斯在下矣，而後乃今掊風；背負青天而莫之夭閼者，而後乃今將圖南。蜩與學鳩笑之曰：「我決起而飛，槍榆枋而止，時則不至而控於地而已矣，奚以這九萬里而南為？」適莽蒼者，三餐而反，腹猶果然；適百里者，宿舂糧；適千里者，三月聚糧。之二蟲又何知！小知不及大知，小年不及大年。奚以知其然也？朝菌不知晦朔，蟪蛄不知春秋，此小年也。楚之南有冥靈者，以五百歲為春，五百歲為秋；上古有大椿者，以八千歲為春，八千歲為秋。此大年也。而彭祖乃今以久特聞，眾人匹之，不亦悲乎！湯之問棘也是已。窮髮之北有冥海者，天池也。有魚焉，其廣數千里，未有知其脩者，其名為鯤。有鳥焉，其名為鵬，背若泰山，翼若垂天之雲，摶扶搖羊角而上者九萬里，絕雲氣，負青天，然後圖南，且適南冥也。斥鴳笑之曰：「彼且奚適也？我騰躍而上，不過數仞而下，翱翔蓬蒿之間，此亦飛之至也，而彼且奚適也？」此小大之辯也。故夫知效一官，行比一鄉，德合一君，而徵一國者，其自視也亦若此矣。而宋榮子猶然笑之。且舉世而譽之而不加勸，舉世而非之而不加沮，定乎內外之分，辯乎榮辱之竟，斯已矣。彼其於世，未數數然也。雖然，猶有未樹也。夫列子御風而行，泠然善也，旬有五日而反。彼於致福者，未數數然也。此雖免乎行，猶有所待者也。若夫乘天地之正，而御六氣之辯，以遊無窮者，彼且惡乎待哉！故曰：至人無己，神人無功，聖人無名。

message.content:
In the Northern Darkness there is a fish, and its name is Kun. The Kun is so huge—I don’t know how many thousand miles long. It changes into a bird, and its name is Peng. The Peng’s back is so vast—I don’t know how many thousand miles across. When it rises in fury and flies, its wings spread like clouds hanging from the sky. When the sea churns, this bird sets off for the Southern Darkness—the celestial lake.

The *Qixie* is a book of wonders. It says: “When the Peng travels to the Southern Darkness, it stirs the water for three thousand miles, and rides a whirlwind up to ninety thousand miles high, leaving on the breath of the sixth month.” Drifting mist, dancing dust—all living things are blown along by the wind. The deep blue of the sky—is that its true color? Or is it just so far away that we can never reach the end? When the Peng looks down from above, it must seem the same to it.

If water is not deep enough, it cannot carry a big boat. Spill a cup of water on a hollow floor, and a mustard seed can float on it like a boat; but set a cup there, and it sticks fast—water too shallow, boat too big. If wind is not thick enough, it cannot hold great wings. So for the ninety-thousand-mile flight, the wind must be beneath it—only then can it ride the wind, its back against the blue sky with nothing to block it, and then set its course for the south.

The cicada and the little dove laugh at the Peng: “We take off with a leap, flutter up to the elm or sandalwood, and if we don’t make it, we just drop to the ground. Why go ninety thousand miles south?”

If you’re going to the nearby woods, you pack three meals and come back with a full stomach. If you’re going a hundred miles, you grind grain the night before. If you’re going a thousand miles, you stock up food for three months. What do those two little creatures know? Little knowledge can’t match great knowledge; a short life can’t match a long one. How do we know? The morning mushroom doesn’t know the new moon; the summer cicada doesn’t know spring and autumn—these are short lives. In the south of Chu there is a sacred tree: five hundred years for one spring, five hundred years for one autumn. In ancient times there was a great oak: eight thousand years for one spring, eight thousand years for one autumn—these are long lives. And yet Peng Zu is famous for his long life—people still try to compare themselves to him. Isn’t that sad?

King Tang once asked Ji about this. In the barren north, there is a dark sea—the celestial lake. In it lives a fish, several thousand miles wide—no one knows how long—called Kun. There is a bird called Peng, with a back like Mount Tai and wings like clouds hanging from the sky. It rides a spiral whirlwind up ninety thousand miles, cutting through clouds and carrying the blue sky, then sets out for the Southern Darkness. A little quail laughs at it: “Where does he think he’s going? I leap and flutter, climb a few yards, then drop down, darting among the weeds. That’s the best flying can be! And where does he think he’s going?”

This is the difference between small and great.

So a man whose wisdom fits a single office, whose conduct serves one village, whose virtue pleases one ruler, who wins the trust of one kingdom—he sees himself just like that little quail. And Song Rongzi laughs at him. The whole world could praise him, and he wouldn’t push harder; the whole world could blame him, and he wouldn’t lose heart. He knows the difference between inner and outer, the boundary between honor and shame. That’s all. He doesn’t fuss over the world’s affairs. Yet even he hasn’t planted his roots firmly.

Master Lie rode the wind and drifted, light and graceful, returning after fifteen days. He wasn’t chasing blessings frantically. But even so—though he didn’t need to walk—he still depended on something. But one who rides the true nature of heaven and earth, masters the six breaths of change, and wanders through the infinite—what does he need to depend on?

So it is said: The Perfect Man has no self; the Spiritual Man has no merit; the Sage has no name.
