### Table: Original-Era Advice vs. Modern Equivalent

| Original-era Advice | Modern Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Use typewritten letters (typed by trained stenographers). | Use professional digital writing (email or document) with proper formatting, grammar, and spell-check. |
| Use standard-size envelopes; window envelopes should show the address clearly. | Use clean, professional email subject lines and clear “To” fields; ensure attachments open correctly. |
| Begin with “Dear Mr. Brown” rather than “Dear Sir” to personalize. | Use the recipient’s name (e.g., “Dear Alex Chen”) or a specific title rather than a generic greeting. |
| Print on plain white paper of good quality; avoid gaudy colors. | Use clean, simple digital templates; avoid distracting fonts, colors, or excessive graphics. |
| Handle one subject per letter for easy filing. | Write one email per distinct topic for easy searching and threading. |

### Dated Advice and Modern Update

**Dated Advice:** The passage advises that “practically all business letters are typewritten” by trained female stenographers, and that men dictating the letters must check the work of these “highly irresponsible” young girls.

**Modern Update (preserving the principle):** The underlying principle is that **professional correspondence must be polished and error-free before it is sent**. Today, the writer is directly responsible for the output. The modern equivalent is to:
- Use digital tools (spell-check, grammar checkers) before sending.
- Peer-review important emails or documents.
- Take personal ownership of accuracy, clarity, and formatting, regardless of who assists with typing or design.

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary (120 words max)
Mary Lennox, a sickly and sour-faced child born in India, is sent to live with her uncle at Misselthwaite Manor after a cholera outbreak kills her parents. Raised by servants who obeyed her every whim, Mary became tyrannical and selfish. On a hot morning, she wakes cross to find a strange servant instead of her Ayah. When she throws a tantrum, the servant looks frightened but says nothing. Mary overhears her mother speaking anxiously with a young officer about the cholera. Suddenly, wailing erupts from the servants’ quarters—the Ayah has died. During the chaos, Mary hides in the nursery, forgotten. She eats leftover food, drinks wine, becomes drowsy, and falls asleep, isolated and alone.

### Mary’s Character Traits with Concrete Details
- **Disagreeable appearance**: She has “a little thin face,” “thin light hair,” and “a sour expression.”
- **Sickly**: She was “born in India and had always been ill in one way or another.”
- **Tyrannical and selfish**: She beats and kicks the strange servant who refuses to summon her Ayah.
- **Angry and vengeful**: She mutters insults like “Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” to the absent Ayah.
- **Neglected and forgotten**: During the cholera crisis, “nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her,” and she hides alone in the nursery.

### Setting and Emotional Distance/Isolation
The setting—a hot, mysterious morning in India during a cholera outbreak—creates emotional isolation by surrounding Mary with frightened, silent servants who refuse to explain what is happening. The “mysteriousness in the air” and “ashy and scared faces” leave Mary confused and alone, unable to connect with anyone. Her mother is distant and preoccupied, and when chaos erupts, Mary is “forgotten by everyone,” retreating to the empty nursery. The half-finished meal and hastily abandoned chairs emphasize a world that has moved on without her, deepening her loneliness.

### Three Discussion Questions (Ages 10–12)
1. Why do you think Mary becomes so angry when the strange servant comes instead of her Ayah? How would you feel in her place?
2. The servants appear “frightened” and won’t tell Mary what is happening. Why might they hide the truth from her?
3. Mary’s mother calls herself “a fool” for staying for a dinner party. What does this tell you about her priorities?

messages.content:
Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### Concise 100-Word Overview
The water cycle describes Earth’s constant water movement among storage pools—atmosphere, land surface, and underground. Water exists as liquid, solid, or gas; 96% is saline ocean water, while fresh water resides in lakes, ice sheets, and groundwater. Solar energy and gravity drive processes like evaporation, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, and groundwater flow. Humans alter the cycle through water use and land use, affecting storage, movement speed, and water quality. Understanding these impacts supports sustainable water use.

### Concept Map: Water Movement Among Major Pools
```
Atmosphere → (Precipitation) → Land Surface & Oceans
Land Surface → (Evaporation, Evapotranspiration) → Atmosphere
Oceans → (Evaporation) → Atmosphere
Land Surface → (Runoff, Streamflow) → Oceans
Land Surface → (Infiltration, Groundwater Recharge) → Groundwater
Groundwater → (Natural Discharge via springs, rivers, ocean) → Surface Water & Oceans
Snowpack/Ice → (Snowmelt) → Land Surface & Streams
```

### Key Terms and One-Sentence Definitions
1. **Evaporation**: Process where solar energy turns liquid water into water vapor.
2. **Evapotranspiration**: Main way water moves into the atmosphere from land and oceans, combining evaporation and plant transpiration.
3. **Precipitation**: Rain, snow, or hail falling from clouds due to gravity.
4. **Runoff**: Water flowing across the land surface toward streams and oceans.
5. **Infiltration**: Water moving from the land surface into the ground.
6. **Groundwater recharge**: Process by which infiltrated water replenishes underground aquifers.
7. **Aquifer**: Underground rock or sediment layer that stores and transmits groundwater.
8. **Watershed**: Large-scale area where water drains across the land surface into a common outlet.

### Human Influence
Human water use and land use affect water movement, storage, and availability. Water use directly removes water from pools, altering storage volumes. Land use changes (e.g., construction, agriculture) modify infiltration, runoff, and evapotranspiration rates, impacting how fast water moves through the cycle. These actions also influence water cleanliness, which affects the amount of usable water in each pool.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Update on warehouse system migration and shipping delays  

**Team,**  

I want to give you a direct, unvarnished update on what’s happening with our warehouse management system migration, how it’s affecting customers, and what we’re doing about it.  

As of last Monday, we began transitioning to a new warehouse management system (WMS). This migration was planned over six months, tested in staging environments for eight weeks, and rolled out in phases to minimize disruption. Despite that preparation, the live deployment introduced unexpected data latency between the new WMS and our shipping carrier APIs. That latency has caused order routing delays for roughly 60 percent of outbound shipments since Tuesday of last week.  

**What happened, plainly:**  

The new system processes order data faster internally, but the handoff to carrier systems (UPS, FedEx, regional carriers) has been intermittently slow. This means pick lists generate correctly in the warehouse, but the carrier label generation lags behind—sometimes by six to twelve hours. Our packing teams have been waiting for labels to print, which has led to trucks departing with partial loads or missing daily cutoff windows.  

**Current customer impact:**  

- Average shipping speed has increased from 1.8 days to 4.3 days over the past seven days.  
- Customer support volume has risen 240 percent week-over-week, driven primarily by “Where is my order?” contacts.  
- About 8 percent of orders shipped last week missed the estimated delivery window entirely.  
- Refund and cancellation requests are up 3x from baseline.  

No single team or vendor caused this. Our engineering team deployed the system as designed; the carrier API integration behaved differently under production load than in staging. The WMS vendor has been responsive, but the fix requires coordinated changes on both sides.  

**What we are doing today:**  

1. **Warehouse teams** are manually overriding label generation for any order that sits unlabeled for more than two hours. This is not sustainable long-term, but it is getting orders out the door while the fix is deployed.  
2. **Engineering** is working with the carrier API provider to adjust timeout thresholds and retry logic. The patch is being tested now.  
3. **Customer support** has been granted authority to offer compensatory credits (up to $15 or 15 percent of order value) without supervisor approval through end of day Friday.  
4. **Logistics** has rerouted a portion of volume to a backup fulfillment center that is still on the legacy system, reducing pressure on the primary warehouse.  

**What will change next week:**  

- By Tuesday, we expect the carrier API patch to be live. This should reduce label-generation lag to under 30 seconds.  
- By Wednesday, we will begin a controlled, gradual ramp of order volume back to the affected warehouse, monitoring label latency in real time.  
- By Friday, we will publish updated delivery promise dates for any order placed during the migration period. These dates will be conservative (6–8 days instead of our usual 3–5) to manage expectations until stability is confirmed.  

**How to escalate urgent issues:**  

If you see a pattern that is not yet addressed—like a specific SKU that consistently fails to generate labels, or a customer escalation that requires a refund above the current credit threshold—use the dedicated #wms-escalations Slack channel. Tag the on-call logistics lead (Ben Tran) and the engineering duty manager (Sarah Chen). Do not wait for a weekly meeting. We are triaging in real time.  

**A note on frontline teams:**  

This situation is not a result of anyone working harder or smarter than usual. Your warehouse team is operating at 110 percent, manually nudging labels through a system that was supposed to automate that work. Your support team is absorbing an unprecedented volume of stressed customers with patience I don’t think I could match. Your engineers are debugging under pressure while the rest of us watch.  

I am not asking for “hustle” or “all-hands-on-deck” energy. I am asking you to keep doing your jobs at normal pace, escalate when something breaks, and protect your own well-being. We will fix the system, then we will fix the backlog, then we will fix the customer experience. In that order.  

**Manager talking points (for your team stand-ups Monday morning):**  

- **Root cause is a data latency issue between our new WMS and carrier APIs, not a people or process failure.** No team performed poorly.  
- **Customers are experiencing 2–3 extra days of shipping time, and support volume is high.** Our top priority is getting current orders out, even if that means using manual overrides.  
- **Backup fulfillment center is live and absorbing overflow.** That is reducing pressure on the primary warehouse by about 15 percent today.  
- **Escalate via #wms-escalations with specific order IDs or SKU patterns.** Do not route escalation through email or DMs; we need full visibility in the channel.  

I will send a follow-up email on Thursday with a progress report (whether we’ve applied the patch and what throughput looks like). Until then, thank you for steadying the ship when the controls were glitchy.  

Best,  

[Name]  
COO

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
The Mapmaker and the Sky-Jumper

The garden smelled like a giant, wet birthday cake. After the big summer rain, every leaf was a tiny green plate holding a clear, sparkling drop. The sun was just peeking out, making everything glisten.

And for one very small creature, it was the most wonderful day of the year.

His name was Sheldon. He was a garden snail with a perfect, swirled shell the color of honey. Sheldon was careful. He liked to know the way before he went anywhere. So, for the whole rainy morning, he had been very busy. He had crawled from the highest tomato plant down to the cool, dark earth, making a map. It wasn’t a map of roads or mountains. It was a map of every single puddle in the garden.

“Puddle One,” he whispered to himself, drawing a wobbly circle with his slimy trail. “Deep and wide, like a tiny sea. Best for a slow, thoughtful sip.”

“Puddle Two,” he said, inching to a little dent in the path. “Shallow and warm. Good for sticking your nose in on a hot day.”

He was just finishing Puddle Three (a long, skinny one under the rain gutter, “splendid for a afternoon cruise”) when something went *whump*.

A small, dusty-gray bird with a white patch on her chest had landed—or rather, crashed—right in the middle of Puddle Four.

“Oof!” chirped the bird, shaking water from her feathers. She looked up and saw Sheldon. “Oh! Hello, tiny helmet-head! What are you doing?”

Sheldon tucked his map-making foot back into his shell for just a second. “I’m a cartographer,” he said. “I’m mapping the puddles.”

The sparrow hopped out and tilted her head. “A map? Of puddles? Why? They’re just big wet spots. You’re supposed to splash in them!”

Sheldon’s eye-stalks wiggled. “But which ones are deep enough to swim in? Which ones are safe? A map helps a traveler be *prepared*.”

“Prepared?” the sparrow laughed. “That sounds boring! I’m Pip. I’m going to fly to the top of the house! All the way up, where the wind is. I’ll see the whole world!”

Sheldon looked at Pip’s messy, damp feathers. “That sounds very high. Do you know how to get back down?”

Pip puffed out her chest. “Flying down is easy! You just sort of... stop flapping.”

Sheldon said nothing. He just looked at the puddle she had landed in. It was very wet.

Pip ignored him. She crouched low on the garden path, ruffled her wings, and *whoosh!* She shot straight up like a fuzzy rocket. She went up past the sunflowers, past the laundry line, past the roof’s edge. She was a tiny speck against the blue sky.

“Wheeee!” her voice came down, thin and happy.

Then she stopped flapping.

For a moment, she hung there. But the wind didn’t hold her up. It just tickled her. She started to drop. Faster. And faster. Her wings flapped in a panic, but she was too stiff. She wasn’t using the wind to *glide*; she was fighting it. She looked like a leaf falling from a tree in a hurry.

Sheldon watched. He could see she was going to miss the soft flower bed. He looked at his map. Puddle Six was right below her—a deep one near the garden hose.

“Pip!” he shouted as loud as a snail can, which is not very loud at all. “Bank left! Aim for the big, floppy zucchini leaf!”

Pip heard him. She tilted her wings. She pointed her beak toward the enormous, soft green umbrella of the zucchini plant. She crashed through the leaf with a *fwump* and rolled onto the earth, dizzy but not hurt.

She sat in the dirt, blinking. “I’m trying so hard,” she whispered. “But I just can’t land.”

Sheldon slowly made his way over. “You’re brave to try,” he said. “But it takes a different kind of bravery to be patient.”

Pip frowned. “Patience isn’t brave. It’s just waiting.”

“Is it?” said Sheldon. “When I make my map, I have to wait for the sun to dry the path so I don’t slip. I have to wait for the moon so the night is cool. I have to stop and look. I have to be *careful*. That takes more courage than rushing.”

Just then, a breeze rustled the wet grass. A single seed from a dandelion drifted by, floating on the air. It didn’t fly hard. It just stretched out its little fuzzy parachute and let the wind carry it.

“Look,” said Sheldon gently. “The seed isn’t fighting. It’s *trusting* the wind. You don’t have to beat the air to fly. You have to feel it.”

Pip watched the seed float slowly, gently, down to a puddle. It landed as softly as a sigh.

She took a deep breath. “Okay. Show me how you be patient.”

Sheldon didn’t tell her. He showed her. He let her watch him map Puddle Seven. He would stretch out his neck, feel the air, wait for a puff of wind, and then move one slow inch. Pip waited beside him. It felt strange at first. The whole world was moving fast—bees buzzing, butterflies darting. But as she watched Sheldon, she began to notice things. She saw how the ants marched in a careful line. She saw the tiny ripples on Puddle Seven made by a single mosquito.

“The puddle is breathing,” she whispered.

Sheldon smiled. “It’s getting smaller. The sun is drinking it. By evening, this puddle will be just a dark spot on the map.”

That gave Pip an idea. She watched the puddle shrink and shrink. She noticed how the wind rippled the water toward one edge. She fluttered up, just a little bit—not to the sky, but to the top of a low stone. Then she jumped.

She didn’t flap hard. She spread her wings wide and felt the breeze. The wind slid under her feathers like a hand. She tilted her body the way the dandelion seed had tilted. Slowly, gently, she floated a few feet and landed—on purpose—right on the edge of Puddle Seven.

Her feet touched the wet stone, and she didn’t wobble.

“I did it!” she chirped.

“You did,” said Sheldon. “You were patient. And that was the bravest part.”

The sun had dried Puddle Seven to a shimmer of dampness. The garden was quiet and golden. Pip didn’t need to fly to the top of the house anymore. Not today.

Instead, she walked beside Sheldon, helping him draw a wobbly new puddle on his map.

“Tomorrow,” she said, “maybe I’ll try landing on the birdbath.”

“And I’ll have a map of the way,” said Sheldon. “Slow and steady.”

Pip chirped a happy, careful chirp. “Slow and steady,” she agreed. And they both felt very brave indeed.

Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden Unveils New “Pollinator Path” Education Trail on July 12**

**HARBORLEAF, CA – June 10, 2026** – Harborleaf Botanical Garden will open a new public education trail dedicated to pollinator conservation on July 12, 2026, offering visitors a hands-on, accessible learning experience designed to help home gardeners create thriving habitats for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.

The “Pollinator Path” is located along the east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile fully accessible trail that winds through the garden’s restored grassland. The trail features native plant demonstration beds, a night-blooming flower station, tactile signage for visually impaired visitors, youth observation cards, and shaded resting points. Starting July 13, the garden will also launch volunteer-led weekend walks along the path, providing guided interpretation for families and community groups.

The development of the Pollinator Path was driven by a steady increase in visitor questions about practical steps to support local pollinators. According to garden staff, guests frequently asked for specific plant recommendations and design ideas that could be applied in small urban yards, school gardens, or suburban lots.

“Our visitors have been telling us for years that they want to do more to help pollinators, but they need simple, actionable guidance that fits their own space,” said Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf Botanical Garden. “The Pollinator Path is our answer. It shows that even small planting decisions—like choosing a single native milkweed for your balcony pot or leaving a patch of unmowed clover—can create connected habitats that cross neighborhood boundaries and strengthen our local ecosystem.”

The trail’s design emphasizes accessibility and multi-sensory learning. Tactile signage includes raised illustrations of pollinator life cycles and plant structures, allowing visitors with visual impairments to feel the shape of a hummingbird’s beak or the texture of a moth’s wing. Youth observation cards, available at the trailhead, encourage children to record which insects they see at each station, turning the walk into a self-guided field journal activity.

At the night-blooming flower station, visitors can observe plants such as evening primrose, moonflower, and night-scented stock—species that attract moths and other nocturnal pollinators. The station is positioned near a shaded rest point with benches, allowing guests to sit and watch as flowers open at dusk.

The native plant demonstration beds showcase a rotating selection of drought-tolerant species adapted to the region’s climate, including California fuchsia, coyote mint, goldenrod, and showy milkweed. Each bed is labeled with common and scientific names, bloom seasons, and the specific pollinators it supports. The garden’s horticulture team will use the beds to host informal weekend talks on topics such as “Planting for Bumblebees” and “How to Build a Moth Garden.”

Volunteer-led weekend walks begin on July 13 and will run every Saturday and Sunday at 10:00 a.m. and 2:00 p.m. through the end of October. Walk leaders are trained to answer questions about plant selection, water use, and garden maintenance without chemical pesticides. The garden does not endorse any commercial products or political positions related to pollinator health, focusing instead on evidence-based gardening practices.

The Pollinator Path is funded entirely through the garden’s existing operating budget and membership contributions. No external grants, corporate sponsors, or celebrity endorsements were used in its creation.

“We believe that education is the strongest tool we have for conservation,” Ortiz added. “By showing people how their own gardens—no matter how small—can become stepping stones in a larger pollinator network, we hope to inspire a community-wide effort that starts with a single flower and spreads block by block.”

The east meadow loop is paved and wheelchair-accessible, with gentle slopes and rest areas every 200 feet. The trailhead is located near the main garden entrance, and admission to the Pollinator Path is included with general garden admission. Harborleaf Botanical Garden is open daily from 8:00 a.m. to sunset.

**About Harborleaf Botanical Garden**
Harborleaf Botanical Garden is a nonprofit public garden dedicated to plant conservation, education, and community engagement. Founded in 1987, the garden maintains more than 200 species of native plants across 35 acres, offering programs for school groups, adults, and families. The garden does not take positions on political matters or partner with commercial endorsers.

**Media Contact**
Elena Vasquez
Communications Manager
Harborleaf Botanical Garden
(555) 019-3400, ext. 210
e.vasquez@harborleafbotanical.org

###

*Note to editors: Advance photos of the Pollinator Path, including native plant demonstration beds and tactile signage, are available upon request. Media preview tours can be arranged for June 28 and July 5, 2026.*

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
- Let physical details and subtext carry the emotion; dialogue should be sparse but meaningful.
- End with a quiet unresolved gesture rather than a full confession.

message.content:
The laundromat on Harbor Street smelled of salt, bleach, and the particular mustiness of damp cotton left too long in a drum. The power had been out for forty minutes. Outside, the coastal fog had rolled in thick as a shroud, swallowing the pier lights and the masts of the boats moored in the marina. Inside, the row of washing machines sat silent and dark, their digital displays blank, their heavy glass lids like dead eyes.

Claire stood at the folding table, her hands flat on the cool steel surface. Her half-finished load of towels—a dark blue, a pale gray—lay limp and sodden in the open drum of machine number four. She had been about to transfer them to the dryer when the lights flickered, died, and left her alone with the sound of the foghorn and the distant, rhythmic slap of water against the harbor wall.

She had a cell phone flashlight, but the battery was low, so she had taken the small battery-powered lantern from her car. It sat now on the edge of the folding table, casting a soft, warm circle of light onto her hands. It was enough to see by. Enough to see the door swing open.

He stepped in out of the fog, shaking a fine mist from the shoulders of his waxed jacket. He was older, of course—gray at his temples, a deeper tiredness in the lines around his eyes—but she knew the set of his shoulders, the way he held his head slightly to one side when he was assessing a situation. She had watched him do it a hundred times from the window of her kitchen, over the hedge that separated their small front yards.

His name was Daniel. He had been her neighbor for seven years. He had taught her how to prune her rosebushes, and she had brought him soup when he had a fever. And then, one April, he had simply not been there. The house went up for sale. The hedge grew wild.

He stopped two strides inside the door. The lantern light caught his face, and she saw the recognition hit him like a physical blow. His mouth opened, closed, then opened again.

“Claire.”

She did not say his name. She held herself very still, her hands still flat on the table. The silence stretched, filled only by the foghorn’s low moan.

“The power,” he said finally, gesturing vaguely toward the street. “I was walking back from the pub. Saw the light.”

“I was drying towels,” she said. The words came out neutral, almost flat. She had not expected to feel this—this pressure in her chest, this tightness in her throat. She had spent years not thinking about him, or at least pretending not to.

He moved closer, tentatively, and stopped at the edge of the lantern’s glow. He did not sit. He did not touch anything. He just stood there, a man made of shadows and gold light.

“I should have said goodbye,” he said.

She looked at the towels. They were a dark blue, like the ocean on a cloudy day. “Why didn’t you?”

He took a breath. Held it. Let it out. “I didn’t know how. I had a job offer, across the country. It came fast. I had a week to pack. And every time I thought of knocking on your door…” He shook his head. “I didn’t have the words.”

“You could have written a note,” she said quietly. “Sent an email. There are ways to say goodbye without having to face someone.”

“I know.” His voice was low, rough. “I was a coward.”

She picked up the lantern and walked to the window. The glass was cold, fogged with condensation. She traced a line through it with her finger, watching the moisture bead and run. Outside, the world had disappeared into a white, silent void.

“When you left,” she said, still facing the window, “I found a voicemail on my phone. You had left it the night before you left. You said you needed to tell me something, but you didn’t say what. You just said to call you back.”

She heard him shift his weight. “I never sent that.”

“I know. I found it months later. I had let the phone battery die, and I didn’t check my messages. I was…” She paused. “I was angry. And then the phone got erased. The message was gone.”

“I don’t remember what I said,” he said. But his voice was strained, and she knew he was lying.

She turned back to face him. The lantern was in her hand now, and the light swung across his face, illuminating the lines around his mouth, the slight tremor in his jaw. She remembered the way he used to laugh, leaning on the hedge with a pair of pruning shears in his hand, telling her she was cutting the roses wrong.

“I think about it sometimes,” she said. “What you might have said.”

He did not answer for a long moment. He looked at the machine where her towels lay wet and forgotten. Then he looked at the fog outside the window.

“Maybe it’s better not knowing,” he said. “Maybe some things are better left unspoken.”

“Is that what you believe now?”

“I don’t know what I believe.” He reached into his pocket and pulled out a small, old iPhone. The screen was cracked. He held it up. “I kept my old phone. I never got a new one. The voicemail is still on it. The one you left me, the night before I left. You said you were glad I was your neighbor. You said you wanted to talk.”

Her breath caught. She remembered leaving that message. She had been standing in her kitchen, looking out at his house, and she had wanted to tell him something she had never been brave enough to say.

“You kept it,” she said. It was not a question.

“I keep it for the same reason you keep the question,” he said. “Because some things don’t have a finish. They just… stay.”

He was close now, close enough that she could see the gray flecks in his eyelashes, the faded green of his irises. The lantern was between them, casting a soft, warm light on both their faces.

She wanted to ask him what he had meant to say. She wanted to tell him that she had spent two years after he left wanting to hate him, and that she had failed. She wanted to say that the voicemail had become a kind of ghost, a promise without a recipient.

But instead, she set the lantern down on the folding table. The light pooled between them, illuminating their hands, their sleeves, the lint on the floor.

“The towels are still wet,” she said.

He smiled, a small, sad smile. “The power will come back on eventually.”

“Eventually.”

She did not move. She did not reach for him. She just stood there, in the small circle of light, with a man who had left without saying goodbye, carrying an old voicemail on a broken phone.

The foghorn moaned again. The water lapped at the harbor wall. And they stayed there, in the quiet, unresolved dark, waiting for the power to return.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word technical article for backend engineers titled by you. Topic: designing rate limiting for a multi-tenant API used by small teams and enterprise customers.

You must cover:
1. The difference between authentication, authorization, quotas, and rate limits.
2. Token bucket vs. fixed window vs. sliding window at a practical level.
3. How to avoid punishing an entire tenant for one noisy integration.
4. What headers and error messages should be returned to API clients.
5. Observability: metrics, logs, and alerts that show whether limits are protecting reliability without blocking legitimate use.

Constraints: no code; no vendor-specific services; include a short “recommended baseline design” section at the end.

message.content:
# Designing Rate Limiting for a Multi-Tenant API: Protecting Small Teams and Enterprise Customers

Rate limiting is often treated as a simple "throttle everything" afterthought, but in a multi-tenant API serving both small teams and enterprise customers, it becomes a complex reliability and fairness problem. A poorly designed rate limiter can silently ruin developer experience for a bootstrap startup while failing to protect infrastructure from an enterprise customer’s runaway batch job.

This article provides a design framework focused on practical trade-offs, not vendor-specific tools. We will clarify foundational concepts, compare the three most common rate-limiting algorithms, address per-tenant isolation, specify what to return to clients, and define the observability signals that tell you whether your limits are working.

## 1. Authentication vs. Authorization vs. Quotas vs. Rate Limits

Engineers often conflate these terms, leading to bad designs. Here is the precise distinction:

- **Authentication** answers: *Who is this request from?* It validates credentials (API keys, OAuth tokens). It does not control what they can do or how often.
- **Authorization** answers: *Is this request allowed to perform this action?* It checks permissions (read vs. write, access to specific resources). It does not manage frequency.
- **Quotas** define *how much total usage is allowed over a billing cycle* (e.g., 10,000 requests per month). Quotas are enforced at the end of a period and often result in a denial after the limit is hit. They are not meant to prevent short-term spikes.
- **Rate limits** define *how fast usage can occur in a short window* (e.g., 100 requests per second). They are designed to protect system stability, not to enforce business agreements. Rate limits should be much tighter than quotas.

**Design implication:** Rate limits must operate at sub-second granularity and should reset automatically. Quotas should be checked off the critical path (e.g., via eventual counters) and trigger a degraded response, not a hard rejection until the end of the month.

## 2. Token Bucket vs. Fixed Window vs. Sliding Window

These three algorithms handle burstiness and fairness very differently.

### Fixed Window
Think of a minute-level counter resetting every 60 seconds. If a tenant sends 60 requests at 0:59 and 60 requests at 1:01, they get 120 requests in two seconds despite a limit of 100 per minute. Fixed windows are simple to implement but allow burst through at window boundaries. They also suffer from the "thundering herd" problem: at the start of each window, all clients rush in.

### Sliding Window (log-based)
This tracks timestamps for every request within a rolling window. If the window is 60 seconds, each new request checks how many timestamps fall within the last 60 seconds. This eliminates boundary bursts, but it requires storing each request timestamp for the entire window duration, which increases memory and time complexity (O(n) per check). It is precise but expensive, especially for high-throughput tenants.

### Token Bucket
A bucket holds up to `N` tokens. Tokens are added at a fixed rate (e.g., 10 tokens per second). Each request consumes one token. If the bucket is empty, the request is denied. This allows bursts up to the bucket size, then smooths out. It does not have the boundary issue of fixed windows, and it is O(1) per check. The downside: you need a timer to refill tokens, and a single large burst can still exhaust the bucket (design the bucket size accordingly).

**Practical recommendation:** Use **token bucket per tenant**. It is the best balance of low overhead and burst tolerance. Configure bucket size to allow short bursts (e.g., 2x the per-second rate) and refill rate equal to the allowed sustained rate. Sliding windows are only justified when you need exact counters for credit-based billing where burst behavior must be auditable to the millisecond.

## 3. Avoiding Punishing an Entire Tenant for One Noisy Integration

A common mistake: a single API key, IP address, or integration (e.g., a webhook client, a misconfigured CI/CD pipeline) triggers the rate limit for the entire tenant, blocking all other services—including critical user-facing calls.

**Solution: Multi-level rate limiting with scopes.**

Implement rate limits at three granularities, evaluated in sequence:

1. **Global per-root-account (tenant):** The hard ceiling. This protects your infrastructure from a tenant going rogue. For a typical enterprise tenant, this might be 5,000 requests per second.
2. **Per-client-ID (sub-account, integration):** Each API key or client credential gets its own bucket. For example, a CI pipeline key has a 50 req/s limit, while a user-facing key has 200 req/s. This prevents one broken script from starving the tenant's production traffic.
3. **Per-route or per-action:** GraphQL mutations, file uploads, and expensive search endpoints should have lower limits than lightweight GET endpoints. Use a separate bucket per "cost group."

**When a per-client-ID limit is hit, reject *only that client*.** The tenant-level bucket is not consumed. The response should clearly indicate which client hit the limit and that the tenant itself has not been restricted. This avoids "hair-trigger" blocking of the whole account.

## 4. Headers and Error Messages for API Clients

Clients need deterministic signals to implement backoff and retry correctly.

### Required Response Headers (for every rate-limited request)
- `X-RateLimit-Limit`: The maximum requests allowed in the current window (e.g., `100`).
- `X-RateLimit-Remaining`: How many requests the current client can still make in this window (e.g., `42`). This should be the bucket's current token count, not a decremented counter, because token bucket refills continuously.
- `X-RateLimit-Reset`: A Unix timestamp (seconds) when the bucket will be at least partially refilled. For a token bucket, this is the time when the next token is added. It helps clients compute a precise retry delay.
- `Retry-After`: HTTP standard header indicating seconds to wait before retrying. Often the same as `X-RateLimit-Reset - now`.

### Error Body
When rate limited (HTTP 429), return a JSON body:
```json
{
  "error": "rate_limit_exceeded",
  "message": "Too many requests for this integration. Retry after the specified time.",
  "scope": "client_id:ci_1234",
  "retry_after_seconds": 5
}
```
Do **not** say "Your account is blocked." Differentiate between client-scoped and tenant-scoped limits. If the *tenant* limit is hit, the message should say "Tenant-wide rate limit exceeded." This prevents support escalations where a user sees "rate limited" and cannot determine whether it is their integration or their entire account.

## 5. Observability: Metrics, Logs, and Alerts

Rate limiting that silently breaks legitimate usage is worse than no rate limiting. You need signals that distinguish "protection working" from "configuration too tight."

### Key Metrics
- `rate_limit.total_hits`: Total number of requests hitting any rate limit.
- `rate_limit.by_scope`: Split by tenant, client_id, and route. This identifies which integrations are consuming limits.
- `rate_limit.by_status`: Count of `429` responses vs. allowed requests for each scope. A high ratio for a specific client points to a misbehaving integration.
- `rate_limit.bucket_exhaustion_seconds`: How long a bucket stayed empty before being refilled. Long periods suggest the limit is too tight for normal traffic.
- `latency.p99` and `error_rate.5xx`: Correlate with rate limit enforcement. If P99 latency drops significantly after throttling kicks in, the rate limiter is doing its job. If both latency and error rates remain high *despite* throttling, the limit may be too low or the backend has a different bottleneck.

### Logs
Every rate-limited request should emit a structured log with: tenant_id, client_id, route, method, current bucket level, limit, and retry_after. Do not log every successful request; only log transitions (e.g., bucket dropped below 30%, or a limit was hit).

### Alerts
- **Critical:** Tenant-level limit hit >10 times per minute for any tenant. This indicates the tenant may be under attack or has a severely misconfigured integration.
- **Warning:** Any single client_id consistently hitting its limit for >5 minutes. Investigation likely reveals a loop or a batch job that should be rate-limited internally.
- **Informational:** Global rate limit utilization >80% across all tenants. This suggests you may need to scale capacity or redistribute load.
- **False positive guard:** Never alert on isolated spikes (e.g., 3 requests in one second hitting a 100 req/s limit). Alert only on sustained patterns.

## Recommended Baseline Design

1. **Algorithm:** Token bucket per tenant, per client ID, and per route cost group. Bucket size = 2x the allowed sustained rate. Refill = the allowed rate.
2. **Fail-closed:** If the rate limiter itself (e.g., Redis) is unreachable, default to *allow* but log aggressively. A brief bypass is better than downtime. If you must fail-closed, set a very low safety limit (e.g., 10 req/s globally) to avoid complete blackout.
3. **Response:** 429 with `X-RateLimit-*` headers and a clear error body differentiating client vs. tenant scope.
4. **Observability:** Expose rate limit counters as Prometheus-style metrics, log all 429s, and set alerts on sustained per-client or per-tenant limit exhaustion, not on brief spikes.
5. **Documentation:** Publish a rate limit policy page with examples of retry-after header use. Provide a small initial burst allowance for new clients to avoid immediate throttling during integration testing.

Rate limiting is not a binary "on/off" switch. It is a continuous negotiation between infrastructure capacity and client behavior. Design it with granular scopes, deterministic feedback, and observability that lets you tune limits without guesswork. Your small teams will appreciate the burst headroom, and your enterprise customers will respect the predictable boundaries.

请帮我写一个单文件 HTML 水族箱动画。

要求：
1. 画面里有多条不同颜色、不同大小的鱼，游动要尽量自然。
2. 用户左键点击水族箱时，可以在点击位置放下一粒鱼食。
3. 每条鱼会追逐离自己最近的鱼食并尝试吃掉它；没有鱼食时恢复自由游动。
4. 需要有完整的 HTML、CSS 和 JavaScript，能直接保存为一个 `.html` 文件运行。
5. 视觉效果要精致一些，不要只给伪代码或解释，直接给出完整代码。

请帮我创建一个单文件 HTML 时钟应用，要求直接给出完整代码。

功能和视觉要求：
1. 时钟表盘是圆形，有刻度、数字、时针、分针、秒针，并在表盘内部显示当前时间和日期。
2. 表盘需要有简洁的高级感：白色背景、深色边框、轻微 3D 阴影。
3. 用 JavaScript 动态生成 60 个刻度，整点刻度更粗更深。
4. 时间必须转换为中国标准时间（北京时区，Asia/Shanghai）。
5. 写一个 updateClock() 函数，计算时针、分针、秒针角度；秒针需要尽量平滑运动。
6. 使用 setInterval 或 requestAnimationFrame 驱动刷新。
7. 代码需要兼容 Edge 浏览器。

请将下面的中文商务写作材料翻译成英文。要求：面向现代职场读者，语气清楚、专业、克制；不要翻译成生硬腔调；保留作者关于“商务信函应传达信息并建立善意”的核心观点。

素材来源：根据公共领域英文书《The Book of Business Etiquette》相关章节改写的中文素材
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists the source eBook as public domain in the USA; this Chinese passage is a benchmark rewrite.

【待处理素材】
许多商务信函本不必写，而已经写出的信中，又有不少因为信息残缺、结构混乱，使原本两封信便能完成并归档的交易，拖成六封或八封往返。好的信件来自清楚的思考和周密的安排。销售函有时需要数周打磨；普通业务往来只需几分钟也足够。关键不在于花了多久，而在于最终写出的信是否能准确完成任务。

衡量商务信函的规则很多，但真正不可缺少的只有两点：第一，信必须让收件人明白你的意思；第二，它必须为公司建立善意。最好的信，是用最简短、最礼貌、最完整的方式做到这两点。每封信背后都应有明确理由，哪怕只是向客户说一声谢谢。许多精力浪费在无目的的广告、无效的推销和多余的来信上；这些文字占用了打字机和纸张，却没有推动任何事情前进。

一封好信不应让读者注意它本身的文采，而应让读者接收到它要传达的消息。某出版社曾寄出一批推销新书的测试信，回信者称赞信写得漂亮，却无人订购图书。这说明文字若抢走了信息的焦点，再优雅也不是成功的商务写作。信纸、信头、版式、段落都应整洁、克制、清楚；过分花哨的设计和故作新奇的格式，只会让人分心。

商务写作还应尊重读者的时间。清楚的标题、适当的称呼、按主题分段的正文、明确的请求和完整的联系方式，往往比华丽措辞更能建立信任。若一封信需要收件人回复，写信者应说明希望对方在何时以前回复、需要提供哪些信息，以及下一步将如何处理。这样，信件不仅传递态度，也推动事务向前。

message.content:
Many business letters should never have been written in the first place. And among those that are written, a fair number—due to missing information or poor structure—drag a transaction that could have been handled in two letters into six or eight rounds of correspondence. Good letters come from clear thinking and careful planning. A sales letter may take weeks to polish; a routine business note may be done in minutes. What matters is not how long it takes, but whether the finished letter does its job accurately.

There are many rules for measuring a business letter, but only two are truly indispensable. First, the letter must make your meaning clear to the reader. Second, it must build goodwill for your company. The best letters do both in the shortest, most courteous, and most complete way. Every letter should have a clear reason behind it—even if it is simply to say thank you to a customer. Too much effort is wasted on aimless advertisements, ineffective sales pitches, and unnecessary correspondence. These words tie up typewriters and paper without moving anything forward.

A good letter should not draw attention to its own style; it should let the message through. A publishing house once sent out a batch of test letters promoting a new book. Many recipients praised the writing as elegant, but no one placed an order. The lesson is clear: when the language steals focus from the message, no amount of polish makes it successful business writing. Stationery, letterhead, layout, and paragraphs should be clean, restrained, and clear. Overly elaborate design or forced novelty only distracts.

Business writing should also respect the reader’s time. Clear subject lines, appropriate salutations, paragraphs organized by topic, a straightforward request, and complete contact details build trust far more effectively than ornate phrasing. If a letter requires a reply, the writer should state by when the response is needed, what information is requested, and what will happen next. That way, the letter does more than convey an attitude—it moves the transaction forward.

请将下面的繁体中文寓言/哲理片段翻译成英文，目标读者为青少年读者。要求：保留想象力和节奏；遇到典故不要长篇解释；译文应清楚、流畅，适合作为儿童文学或寓言文本阅读。

素材来源：《莊子的故事》节选：逍遥游开篇
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23913
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
北冥有魚，其名為鯤。鯤之大，不知其幾千里也。化而為鳥，其名為鵬。鵬之背，不知其幾千里也；怒而飛，其翼若垂天之雲。是鳥也，海運則將徙於南冥。南冥者，天池也。齊諧者，志怪者也。諧之言曰：「鵬之徙於南冥也，水擊三千里，摶扶搖而上者九萬里，去以六月息者也。」野馬也，塵埃也，生物之以息相吹也。天之蒼蒼，其正色邪？其遠而無所至極邪？其視下也，亦若是則已矣。且夫水之積也不厚，則其負大舟也無力。覆杯水於坳堂之上，則芥為之舟；置杯焉則膠，水淺而舟大也。風之積也不厚，則其負大翼也無力。故九萬里，則風斯在下矣，而後乃今掊風；背負青天而莫之夭閼者，而後乃今將圖南。蜩與學鳩笑之曰：「我決起而飛，槍榆枋而止，時則不至而控於地而已矣，奚以這九萬里而南為？」適莽蒼者，三餐而反，腹猶果然；適百里者，宿舂糧；適千里者，三月聚糧。之二蟲又何知！小知不及大知，小年不及大年。奚以知其然也？朝菌不知晦朔，蟪蛄不知春秋，此小年也。楚之南有冥靈者，以五百歲為春，五百歲為秋；上古有大椿者，以八千歲為春，八千歲為秋。此大年也。而彭祖乃今以久特聞，眾人匹之，不亦悲乎！湯之問棘也是已。窮髮之北有冥海者，天池也。有魚焉，其廣數千里，未有知其脩者，其名為鯤。有鳥焉，其名為鵬，背若泰山，翼若垂天之雲，摶扶搖羊角而上者九萬里，絕雲氣，負青天，然後圖南，且適南冥也。斥鴳笑之曰：「彼且奚適也？我騰躍而上，不過數仞而下，翱翔蓬蒿之間，此亦飛之至也，而彼且奚適也？」此小大之辯也。故夫知效一官，行比一鄉，德合一君，而徵一國者，其自視也亦若此矣。而宋榮子猶然笑之。且舉世而譽之而不加勸，舉世而非之而不加沮，定乎內外之分，辯乎榮辱之竟，斯已矣。彼其於世，未數數然也。雖然，猶有未樹也。夫列子御風而行，泠然善也，旬有五日而反。彼於致福者，未數數然也。此雖免乎行，猶有所待者也。若夫乘天地之正，而御六氣之辯，以遊無窮者，彼且惡乎待哉！故曰：至人無己，神人無功，聖人無名。

message.content:
In the Northern Ocean, there is a fish, and its name is Kun. The Kun is so huge—no one knows how many thousand miles it spans. It transforms into a bird, and its name is Peng. The back of the Peng—no one knows how many thousand miles it stretches. When it rises in anger and flies, its wings are like clouds hanging from the sky. This bird, when the seas shift, journeys to the Southern Ocean. The Southern Ocean is the Celestial Pool.

In the book *Qi Xie*, a record of strange things, it says: "When the Peng migrates to the Southern Ocean, it churns the waters for three thousand miles, spirals upward on a whirlwind for ninety thousand miles, and goes away on the breath of summer for six months." The wild horses (misty vapors), the dust—all living things blow upon one another with their breath. The sky is deep blue—is that its true color? Or is it because it is so far away, with no end to be seen? When the Peng looks down, it sees the same way.

If water is not deep enough, it lacks the strength to carry a great boat. Pour a cup of water into a hollow in the floor, and a mustard seed will float on it like a boat. But place a cup there, and it sticks—the water is too shallow, and the boat too big. If wind is not thick enough, it lacks the strength to carry great wings. So for ninety thousand miles, the wind is gathered beneath the Peng. Only then can it ride the wind—carrying the blue sky on its back, with nothing to block it. Only then can it set its course south.

The cicada and the young dove laugh at it, saying: "I take off with a flurry, fly up to the elm or sandalwood, and if I don't reach it, I just drop to the ground. Why go ninety thousand miles south?"

When you go to the green woods nearby, you take three meals and come back, still full. When you go a hundred miles, you grind grain for a night. When you go a thousand miles, you gather food for three months. What do these two little creatures know? Small knowledge cannot match great knowledge; a short life cannot match a long life. How do we know this? The morning mushroom knows nothing of dusk and dawn; the cicada knows nothing of spring and autumn—these are short lives. In the south of Chu, there is the mingling tree, which counts five hundred years as spring and five hundred years as autumn. In ancient times, there was the great chun tree, which counted eight thousand years as spring and eight thousand years as autumn—these are long lives. And yet Pengzu is now famous for his long life; the common people try to compare themselves with him—isn't that sad?

King Tang of Shang asked Ji the Wise the same thing. In the barren north, there is a dark sea—the Celestial Pool. In it, there is a fish thousands of miles wide, and no one knows its length. Its name is Kun. There is also a bird, its name is Peng. Its back is like Mount Tai, and its wings are like clouds hanging from the sky. It spirals upward on a whirlwind like a goat's horn for ninety thousand miles, cuts through the clouds, carries the blue sky, and then sets its course south toward the Southern Ocean.

The little quail laughs at it, saying: "Where does he think he's going? I leap and fly up, but after a few yards I come down. I flutter among the bushes—this is the best flying there is. And where does he think he's going?" This is the difference between small and great.

So those whose wisdom fits one office, whose conduct suits one village, whose virtue pleases one ruler, whose ability wins one kingdom—they look at themselves the same way. But Song Rongzi still laughs at them. Even if the whole world praised him, he would not be encouraged; even if the whole world opposed him, he would not be discouraged. He knows the difference between inner and outer, and distinguishes the boundary of honor and disgrace. That's all. He does not busy himself with worldly things. But still, he had not yet found his foundation.

Liezi rode the wind and went his way, light and graceful, returning after fifteen days. He did not busy himself with seeking happiness. Though he escaped walking, he still depended on something. But if one rides the truth of heaven and earth, and drives the changes of the six breaths, wandering through the infinite—on what does such a one depend? Therefore it is said: The Perfect Man has no self; the Spirit Man has no achievement; the Sage has no name.

请将下面的中文新闻稿素材翻译成英文。目标读者为普通英语新闻读者。要求：准确、自然、新闻稿风格；不要添加事实；保留机构名的含义，可译为 U.S. Geological Survey；不要使用夸张宣传语。

素材来源：改写自 U.S. Geological Survey 水循环图发布新闻稿
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/news/national-news-release/new-usgs-diagram-re-envisions-how-earths-most-precious-commodity-cycles
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain; this Chinese source is a benchmark rewrite based on that public-domain material.

【待处理素材】
美国地质调查局发布新版水循环教学图，帮助教育者更完整地解释地球水在自然系统与人类活动之间的流动。新版图示取代了自2000年以来被大量课堂使用的旧版本。旧图多以一个大循环表现蒸发、降水、径流等自然过程，而新版把人类用水、土地利用、蓄水工程、灌溉和地下水开采等因素纳入同一张图中，展示许多小循环如何彼此连接、相互影响。

该图的开发团队咨询了一百多名教育工作者和三十多名水文学专家，并参考了美国地质调查局长期积累的水文数据。新版图示不仅说明水如何在大气、地表和地下储存、转移和改变形态，也强调水量、水质和水到达时间对可用水资源的影响。例如，降水并不会简单地从天空落下再回到海洋；一部分水会进入土壤和含水层，一部分会被植物吸收并通过蒸腾回到空气中，一部分会被水库、农田、城市排水系统和家庭用水改变路径。

设计者采用信息设计原则，让读者能顺着水的路径观察沿海平原、干旱盆地、湿润盆地和农业盆地之间的联系。图中还强调，不同区域的用水选择会影响下游水量和水质，因此理解水循环不能只看自然过程，也要观察人类活动如何改变水的储存位置、移动速度和清洁程度。新版图示最初提供英文和西班牙文版本，预期将由使用者翻译成更多语言。发布方表示，这一工具旨在帮助学生、教师和自然资源管理者以更清晰的方式理解水的复杂旅程，并认识到日常用水选择会影响水资源的可持续利用。

message.content:
**U.S. Geological Survey Releases Updated Water Cycle Diagram for Educators**

The U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) has released a new educational diagram of the water cycle, designed to help educators more fully explain how Earth’s water moves between natural systems and human activities. The updated diagram replaces a version that had been widely used in classrooms since 2000. While the older diagram primarily depicted natural processes such as evaporation, precipitation, and runoff in a single large cycle, the new version incorporates human water use, land use, water storage infrastructure, irrigation, and groundwater extraction into one graphic, illustrating how many smaller cycles connect and influence one another.

The diagram was developed with input from more than 100 educators and over 30 hydrology experts, and draws on long-term hydrological data collected by the USGS. The new diagram not only shows how water is stored, transferred, and transformed in the atmosphere, on the surface, and underground, but also emphasizes how water quantity, water quality, and the timing of water availability affect usable water resources. For example, precipitation does not simply fall from the sky and return to the ocean; some water enters the soil and aquifers, some is absorbed by plants and released back into the air through transpiration, and some is diverted by reservoirs, farmlands, urban drainage systems, and household use.

Applying principles of information design, the diagram allows viewers to follow water paths and see connections between coastal plains, arid basins, humid basins, and agricultural basins. It also highlights that water-use decisions in one area can affect downstream water quantity and quality. Understanding the water cycle, therefore, requires looking not only at natural processes but also at how human activities alter where water is stored, how fast it moves, and how clean it is. The new diagram is initially available in English and Spanish, with the expectation that users will translate it into additional languages. The USGS stated that the tool is intended to help students, teachers, and natural resource managers better understand the complex journey of water and recognize that daily water-use choices affect the sustainable use of water resources.

且說黛玉自那日棄舟登岸時，便有榮國府打發了轎子并拉行李的車輛久候了．這林黛玉常听得母親說過，他外祖母家与別家不同．他近日所見的這几個三等仆婦，吃穿用度，已是不凡了，何況今至其家．因此步步留心，時時在意，不肯輕易多說一句話，多行一步路，惟恐被人恥笑了他去．自上了轎，進入城中從紗窗向外瞧了一瞧，其街市之繁華，人煙之阜盛，自与別處不同．又行了半日，忽見街北蹲著兩個大石獅子，三間獸頭大門，門前列坐著十來個華冠麗服之人．正門卻不開，只有東西兩角門有人出入．正門之上有一匾，匾上大書"敕造宁國府"五個大字．黛玉想道：這必是外祖之長房了．想著，又往西行，不多遠，照樣也是三間大門，方是榮國府了．卻不進正門，只進了西邊角門．那轎夫抬進去，走了一射之地，將轉彎時，便歇下退出去了．后面的婆子們已都下了轎，赶上前來．另換了三四個衣帽周全十七八歲的小廝上來，复抬起轎子．眾婆子步下圍隨至一垂花門前落下．眾小廝退出，眾婆子上來打起轎帘，扶黛玉下轎．林黛玉扶著婆子的手，進了垂花門，兩邊是抄手游廊，當中是穿堂，當地放著一個紫檀架子大理石的大插屏．轉過插屏，小小的三間廳，廳后就是后面的正房大院．正面五間上房，皆雕梁畫棟，兩邊穿山游廊廂房，挂著各色鸚鵡，畫眉等鳥雀．台磯之上，坐著几個穿紅著綠的丫頭，一見他們來了，便忙都笑迎上來，說：“剛才老太太還念呢，可巧就來了。”于是三四人爭著打起帘籠，一面听得人回話：“林姑娘到了。”黛玉方進入房時，只見兩個人攙著一位鬢發如銀的老母迎上來，黛玉便知是他外祖母．方欲拜見時，早被他外祖母一把摟入怀中，心肝儿肉叫著大哭起來．當下地下侍立之人，無不掩面涕泣，黛玉也哭個不住．一時眾人慢慢解勸住了，黛玉方拜見了外祖母．____此即冷子興所云之史氏太君，賈赦賈政之母也．當下賈母一一指与黛玉：“這是你大舅母，這是你二舅母，這是你先珠大哥的媳婦珠大嫂子。”黛玉一一拜見過．賈母又說：“請姑娘們來．今日遠客才來，可以不必上學去了。”眾人答應了一聲，便去了兩個．不一時，只見三個奶嬤嬤并五六個丫鬟，簇擁著三個姊妹來了．第一個肌膚微丰，合中身材，腮凝新荔，鼻膩鵝脂，溫柔沉默，觀之可親．第二個削肩細腰，長挑身材，鴨蛋臉面，俊眼修眉，顧盼神飛，文彩精華，見之忘俗．第三個身量未足，形容尚小．其釵環裙襖，三人皆是一樣的妝飾．黛玉忙起身迎上來見禮，互相廝認過，大家歸了坐．丫鬟們斟上茶來．不過說些黛玉之母如何得病，如何請醫服藥，如何送死發喪．不免賈母又傷感起來，因說：“我這些儿女，所疼者獨有你母，今日一旦先舍我而去，連面也不能一見，今見了你，我怎不傷心！

message.content:
Let us speak of Daiyu. On the day she left her boat and set foot on the shore, sedan-chairs from the Rongguo Mansion and carts for her luggage had long been waiting. Lin Daiyu had often heard her mother say that her grandmother’s household was different from others. The several third-class servants she had seen of late—their clothing, their food, their manner of living—were already beyond the ordinary; how much more so, then, the family itself? Thus she made herself watchful at every step, mindful at every moment, unwilling to speak a single word or take a single step lightly, for fear of being laughed at by others.

Once she had mounted the sedan-chair and entered the city, she looked out through the gauze window. The bustling streets, the throngs of people, the thriving air—all were different from elsewhere. After traveling for some time, she suddenly saw, to the north of the street, two great stone lions crouching, and a three-bayed gate with beast-head knockers. Before the gate sat a dozen men in splendid clothes and fine caps. The main gate was not open; only the eastern and western side-gates showed people passing in and out. Above the main gate hung a tablet, on which were inscribed in large characters five words: “Imperially Commissioned Ningguo Mansion.” Daiyu thought: This must be the senior branch of my grandmother’s clan. As she pondered, they went further west. Before long, there again appeared a three-bayed gate—this was the Rongguo Mansion. They did not enter by the main gate but went through the western side-gate.

The bearers carried the sedan-chair inside. They went about the distance of a bowshot, and when they were about to turn, they set it down and withdrew. The old women who had been following behind had already descended from their own sedan-chairs and hurried forward. In their place, three or four young pages of seventeen or eighteen, neat in dress and cap, lifted the sedan-chair again. The old women, on foot, surrounded it as they proceeded to a垂花門 [carved screen gate with hanging flowers], where it was set down. The young pages withdrew, and the old women came forward, raised the sedan-chair curtain, and helped Daiyu dismount.

Leaning on an old woman’s hand, Lin Daiyu entered the垂花門. On both sides were covered winding corridors, and in the center was a passageway. In the middle stood a large screen of marble set in a rosewood frame. Passing the screen, she came to three small reception halls; behind these lay the main courtyard of the rear quarters. Facing her were five principal rooms, their beams carved and rafters painted, with side corridors running along the walls on both sides, where hung all manner of birds—parrots, thrushes, and the like. On the terrace steps sat several maids in red and green. As soon as they saw her, they hurried forward with smiles and said: “Just now the old lady was speaking of you—what a coincidence that you have come!” And three or four of them vied to raise the door curtain, while a voice within announced: “Miss Lin has arrived.”

Just as Daiyu entered the room, she saw an old woman with hair as silver as frost, supported by two attendants, coming forward to greet her. Daiyu knew at once that this must be her grandmother. She was about to make her formal bow, but before she could, her grandmother gathered her into her arms and, crying “My precious heart! My flesh and blood!” burst into loud sobs. All those standing about in the room covered their faces and wept as well; Daiyu herself could not stop crying. After a while, the others gradually calmed them and urged them to stop. Only then did Daiyu make her obeisance to her grandmother. [This was the lady née Shi, the matriarch of whom Leng Zixing had spoken, the mother of Jia She and Jia Zheng.]

Then Grandmother Jia pointed out each person to Daiyu: “This is your elder uncle’s wife; this is your second uncle’s wife; this is the wife of your late elder cousin, the First Master Zhu—your elder sister-in-law Zhu.” Daiyu greeted each in turn. Grandmother Jia then said: “Summon the young ladies. Today a guest has come from afar; they need not go to school.” Someone acknowledged the order, and two maids went off. Before long, three nurses and five or six maids clustered around three young ladies, who arrived together.

The first had a slightly plump figure and a medium build; her cheeks were as fresh as newly ripened lychees, her nose was sleek as goose-fat. She was gentle and silent, and at first glance seemed approachable and kind. The second had sloping shoulders and a slender waist, a tall, elegant figure, an oval face, fine eyes and long brows; her glance was bright and spirited, her air refined and cultivated—one look at her and all vulgar thoughts fell away. The third was still young, her figure not yet full-grown. In hairpins, bracelets, skirts, and jackets, the three were dressed alike.

Daiyu quickly rose and stepped forward to greet them. After they had exchanged salutations and become acquainted, they all took their seats. The maids poured tea. Their talk turned to how Daiyu’s mother had fallen ill, how doctors had been called and medicines administered, how the funeral had been arranged—and Grandmother Jia was moved to grief once more. She said: “Of all my children, the one I loved most was your mother. And now she has left me first, without even letting me see her face once more. Now that I see you, how can I not be heartbroken!”
